exact_quotes: If your lifetrap is severe, this is how you experience even minor disruptions in your intimate relationship. You feel that if your connection to the loved person were lost, you would be plunged into utter aloneness.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Lindsay's Falling Into Cellar Image
description: In therapy, Lindsay described her experience of abandonment feelings through imagery: seeing herself falling backwards into a dark cellar where she'll be alone forever, with her partner pushing her and the door about to shut. This image captured the terror and sense of eternal isolation that even minor relationship disruptions trigger.
mechanism: The imagery reveals the catastrophic quality of abandonment fears—not just temporary distress but a sense of being permanently cast into isolation and darkness.
behavioral_signature: feeling of falling or losing ground

intervention.verbal: Close your eyes and give me an image of how you felt.
intervention.behavioral: Rescript the image with adult resources and support
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This image shows how frightening abandonment feels to the child part of you. We can work with this image to bring safety and comfort to that scared part.

exact_quotes: I see myself falling backwards. It's like falling backwards into this dark cellar, where I'll be all alone forever. Greg is pushing me backwards into the cellar, and the door is going to shut, and I'm going to be all alone.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment Escape/Avoidance Mode
description: Some individuals with the Abandonment lifetrap cope by avoiding intimate relationships altogether, preferring solitude to the pain of potential loss. This represents an Escape coping style. The person concludes they are better off alone because the process of loss is too painful to repeat.
mechanism: Avoidance prevents the activation of abandonment fears but at the cost of connection and intimacy. It maintains the lifetrap by preventing corrective experiences with stable partners.
what_activates_it: belief that no stable connection is possible
behavioral_signature: preferring peace of solitude to relationship risk

intervention.primary: Gradual exposure to intimacy with safe partners
intervention.verbal: You chose solitude to protect yourself from pain. But solitude has its own costs. What have you missed by staying alone?
intervention.behavioral: Small steps toward emotional connection with trusted others
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Avoiding relationships keeps you safe but also keeps you stuck. Healing means taking measured risks with people who are trustworthy.

exact_quotes: I just couldn't keep going through it. It was too painful. I could never find someone who would be there for me. I was better off alone. At least I had some peace.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Danger Signals in Dating for Abandonment Lifetrap
description: Five warning signs that a potential partner is triggering the Abandonment lifetrap: (1) partner unlikely to commit due to existing relationship, (2) partner not consistently available (travels, lives far, workaholic), (3) partner emotionally unstable (drinks, drugs, depression), (4) partner is a 'Peter Pan' wanting freedom to come and go, (5) partner is ambivalent—acting deeply in love one moment, indifferent the next.
mechanism: People with Abandonment are attracted to partners who present some hope for stability but not complete hope—a mixture of hope and doubt. The instability feels familiar and generates intense chemistry, but ensures continued reenactment of childhood abandonment.
behavioral_signature: feeling of 'winning' when uncertain partner shows interest

intervention.verbal: An intense sexual attraction may be a sign that your partner is triggering your Abandonment lifetrap. If this is so, the relationship means trouble.
intervention.behavioral: Make a checklist of danger signals and review before committing to new relationships
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: High chemistry often means you're replaying your childhood pattern. The excitement you feel with unstable partners is actually your lifetrap being activated.

exact_quotes: You are attracted most to partners who show some degree of commitment and connection, but not so much that you are absolutely sure that they will stay.
Choosing partners who are not really there for you ensures that you will continue to reenact your childhood abandonment.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 5: Trust a Stable, Committed Partner
description: When you find a partner who is stable and committed, learn to trust them. Believe they are there for you forever and will not leave. This requires stepping out of the abandonment cycle and giving up wild, unstable love for strong and steady relationships.
mechanism: After repeated abandonment experiences, trust becomes the mechanism for breaking the cycle. Without learning to trust stable partners, the person remains trapped in seeking chemistry with unavailable partners or sabotaging good relationships.
what_activates_it: Therapy progress enabling new relationship choices
behavioral_signature: Not seeking evidence of impending abandonment
Accepting partner's presence without constant testing

intervention.primary: Cognitive reframing and behavioral commitment to trust
intervention.verbal: Get off the roller coaster. Give up the wild, unstable love in favor of the strong and steady.
intervention.behavioral: Practice accepting partner's reliability without seeking proof of betrayal
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: After so much experience with abandonment, it is hard to learn to trust. But this is the only way to finally step out of the cycle and find fulfillment in love.

exact_quotes: When You Find a Partner Who is Stable and Committed, Trust Him/Her. Believe That He/She is There for You Forever; and Will Not Leave.
Get off the roller coaster. Give up the wild, unstable love in favor of the strong and steady.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 6: Control Clinging, Jealousy, and Overreaction
description: In a good relationship with a stable partner, learn to control the tendency to overreact to emotional slights and normal separations. The best approach is working on yourself, exploring your own resources, and learning you can be alone and flourish.
mechanism: Abandonment triggers cause disproportionate emotional reactions to normal relationship fluctuations. By developing self-resources and using flashcards, the lifetrap weakens over time through repeated cognitive challenges.

intervention.verbal: Using a flashcard each time your lifetrap is triggered chips away at the lifetrap, weakening it.
intervention.behavioral: Turn attention to own life and ways of developing self; explore own resources
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The better able you are to be on your own, the better you will be in relationships.

exact_quotes: Do Not Cling, Become Jealous, or Overreact to the Normal Separations of a Healthy Relationship.
Using a flashcard each time your lifetrap is triggered chips away at the lifetrap, weakening it.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Lindsay's Abandonment Flashcard
description: A complete flashcard used to help Lindsay manage her abandonment lifetrap reactions in her relationship with Richard, addressing triggered feelings, cognitive reframing, behavioral alternatives, and self-development focus.
mechanism: The flashcard interrupts the automatic abandonment response by providing reality-testing statements, normalizing partner withdrawal, predicting negative consequences of clinging, and redirecting attention to self-development.
what_activates_it: Urge to become angry and needy
behavioral_signature: Pausing before acting on abandonment urges

intervention.verbal: Right now I feel devastated because Richard is withdrawing from me, and I am about to become angry and needy. However, I know that this is my Abandonment lifetrap, and that my lifetrap is triggered by just the slightest evidence of withdrawal. I need to remember that people in good relationships withdraw, and that withdrawal is part of the natural rhythm of good relationships. If I start behaving in an angry and clingy way, I will push Richard even further away. Richard has a right to pull away at times. What I should do instead is work with my thoughts to try to take a longer view of the relationship as a whole. My feelings are way out of proportion to reality. I can tolerate my feelings and remember that in the big picture Richard and I are still connected, and the relationship is good. To best help myself, I should turn my attention to my own life, and ways of developing myself. The better able I am to be on my own, the better I will be in relationships.
intervention.behavioral: Use flashcard each time abandonment lifetrap is triggered; turn attention to self-development
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Using a flashcard each time your lifetrap is triggered chips away at the lifetrap, weakening it.

exact_quotes: Right now I feel devastated because Richard is withdrawing from me, and I am about to become angry and needy.
I need to remember that people in good relationships withdraw, and that withdrawal is part of the natural rhythm of good relationships.
My feelings are way out of proportion to reality.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abby's Realization About Kurt
description: Abby learned to trust that Kurt was really there for her, even when physically absent. She recognized that what she had been seeking was already present in her relationship—someone who is there for her but who also wants her to stand on her own.
mechanism: Through therapy, Abby reframed her perception of Kurt's absences and recognized that a healthy relationship involves both connection and autonomy, resolving her abandonment-driven distortions.
behavioral_signature: Accepting partner's physical absence without panic

intervention.verbal: It's funny, but I feel like the end of the Wizard of Oz. What I was looking for the whole time was in my own backyard. I already have with Kurt what I have always wanted most, someone who is there for me but who wants me to stand on my own.
intervention.behavioral: Practice trusting partner during separations
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Learning to trust shows you that what you need may already be present in your life.

exact_quotes: It's funny, but I feel like the end of the Wizard of Oz. What I was looking for the whole time was in my own backyard.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Patrick's Learning to Keep Power in Relationships
description: Patrick ended his marriage to Francine after understanding she would never change. He is now learning to keep himself in relationships rather than giving himself entirely away, recognizing that total self-sacrifice makes losing someone catastrophic.
mechanism: Patrick's abandonment pattern led him to give everything to partners, making loss catastrophic. By learning to retain his power and sense of self, he reduces vulnerability to abandonment while improving relationship health.
behavioral_signature: Dating while maintaining sense of self
Not giving everything away to partner

intervention.verbal: I always thought the whole point of a relationship was to hold on to the other person. I would do anything to keep her. But now I see that I can let people go and survive. I can let people go and walk away, and eventually I'll be fine.
intervention.behavioral: Practice retaining sense of self and power in relationships
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: If you give everything to the other person, it is a catastrophe to lose that person. Learning to hold on to your power in relationships protects you.

exact_quotes: I always thought the whole point of a relationship was to hold on to the other person. I would do anything to keep her. But now I see that I can let people go and survive.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Lindsay's Healthy Relationship with Richard
description: Lindsay developed a relationship with Richard, a recovering alcoholic of 12 years who was consistently emotionally present. Unlike her previous relationships, she stayed friends with Richard for months before becoming lovers, which stabilized the relationship and reduced her clinging and accusations.
mechanism: The friendship period before romantic involvement reduced Lindsay's vulnerability and prevented the rapid escalation that typically triggered her abandonment patterns. Richard's steady, unruffled temperament helped contain her intense emotions.

intervention.verbal: Emotional people and calm, rational people often form relationships with one another.
intervention.behavioral: Build friendship before romantic involvement; choose stable, consistent partners
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Staying friends for months before becoming lovers can stabilize the relationship and help you feel less vulnerable.

exact_quotes: Richard's steady love for Lindsay helped contain her intense emotions, just as her relationship with us did.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Therapy for Severe Lifetraps
description: When a lifetrap is severe and prevents forming good relationships, therapy is recommended. The therapy relationship itself can help the person reach a place where they can bring good relationships into their life.
mechanism: The therapeutic relationship provides a corrective emotional experience that demonstrates trustworthy connection, allowing the patient to develop capacity for healthy relationships outside therapy.
behavioral_signature: Using therapy relationship as template for outside relationships

intervention.verbal: If your lifetrap is severe, and you cannot seem to form a good relationship, consider therapy. A therapy relationship can help get you to the place where you can bring good relationships into your life.
intervention.behavioral: Engage in therapy relationship as practice for healthy relating
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: A therapy relationship can help get you to the place where you can bring good relationships into your life.

exact_quotes: If your lifetrap is severe, and you cannot seem to form a good relationship, consider therapy. A therapy relationship can help get you to the place where you can bring good relationships into your life.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust and Abuse Lifetrap Definition
description: The Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap involves expecting people to hurt, abuse, humiliate, cheat, lie, manipulate, or take advantage of you. It originates from childhood experiences of being abused, manipulated, humiliated, or betrayed by family members.
mechanism: Childhood abuse creates a template where relationships are dangerous and unpredictable rather than safe. The person maintains hypervigilance, searches for ulterior motives, and cannot trust even close others, perpetuating isolation or reenactment of abuse.

intervention.verbal: Your experience of relationships is a painful one. Relationships are not places to relax and become vulnerable. Rather, they are dangerous and unpredictable.
intervention.behavioral: Gradual trust-building exercises with safe others
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your childhood taught you that people will hurt you. Learning to distinguish safe from unsafe people is possible.

exact_quotes: I expect people to hurt or use me.
It is only a matter of time before the people I love will betray me.
I have to protect myself and stay on my guard.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Frank: Mistrust in Personal and Work Life
description: Frank, 32, presents with marital problems due to mistrust. Despite knowing his wife loves him, he expects the relationship to be a trick. He becomes suspicious when his wife is briefly late, shaking her and demanding explanations. He also suspects his therapists of conspiring with his boss.
mechanism: Frank's childhood physical abuse by his father created pervasive mistrust that extends to all relationships. His hypervigilance and explosive reactions threaten his marriage despite his wife's reliability.
what_activates_it: Coincidental similarities between therapy content and boss's feedback

intervention.primary: Building trust with therapists as foundation for trusting others
intervention.verbal: We would never do that to you. We're on your side, remember?
intervention.behavioral: Reality testing suspicions; building trust gradually
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your experiences with your father make it hard to trust anyone, but not everyone is like your father.

exact_quotes: Even though I know she loves me, I have trouble trusting her. It's like I keep expecting the whole thing to be a big trick.
Is there any way you could know my boss? Is there any way you could have discussed me with him?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Madeline: Never Had Long-Term Romantic Relationship
description: Madeline, 29, has never had a long-term romantic relationship. In her early twenties she was promiscuous while drinking heavily. After sobriety, she cannot tolerate men's advances—leaving a party in anger when a man gave her a small kiss. She was sexually abused by her stepfather from age 9 to 12.
mechanism: Sexual abuse created the belief that men only want to use her for sex. Her promiscuity during drinking was surrender coping; her current avoidance is escape coping. Both prevent healthy intimacy.
behavioral_signature: Extreme reaction to minor physical contact
Avoidance of all romantic relationships when sober

intervention.primary: Trauma processing and gradual trust building
intervention.verbal: When did these feelings about men begin?
intervention.behavioral: Gradual exposure to safe male relationships
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your stepfather's abuse taught you that men will use you. Learning to distinguish safe from unsafe men is part of healing.

exact_quotes: I guess I'm here because I'm worried if I'll ever have a normal relationship with a guy.
Usually I figure, no matter how nice a guy is, I know what he's really after... Sex. Only sex.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust and Abuse Lifetrap Questionnaire Items XI-XX
description: Additional questionnaire items for assessing the Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap. These items assess submission to avoid abandonment, gender-based trust issues, childhood amnesia, dissociation, paranoid ideation, sado-masochistic fantasies, avoidance of intimacy, unexplained fear, perpetrating abuse, and feelings of helplessness.
mechanism: Each endorsed item indicates a specific manifestation of the mistrust/abuse schema, whether through surrender (tolerating abuse), escape (avoidance, dissociation), or counterattack (becoming abusive).
behavioral_signature: Tolerating mistreatment to avoid being alone

intervention.verbal: XI. You allow other people to take advantage of you because 'it is better than being alone.' XII. You feel that men/women cannot be trusted. XIII. You do not remember large portions of your childhood. XIV. When you are frightened of someone, you 'tune out,' as if part of you is not really there. XV. You often feel people have hidden motives or bad intentions, even when you have little proof. XVI. You often have sado-masochistic fantasies. XVII. You avoid getting close to men/women because you cannot trust them. XVIII. You feel frightened around men/women and you do not understand why. XIX. You have sometimes been abusive or cruel to other people, especially the ones to whom you are closest. XX. You often feel helpless in relation to other people.
intervention.behavioral: Rate each item and total score to determine lifetrap severity
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These questions help identify specific ways the mistrust pattern shows up in your life, including how you may have adapted to early abuse.

exact_quotes: You allow other people to take advantage of you because 'it is better than being alone.'
When you are frightened of someone, you 'tune out,' as if part of you is not really there.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Reenactment of Abuse in Good Relationships
description: Even when in a good relationship with a trustworthy partner, individuals with the Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap may unconsciously recreate abusive dynamics. They may become the abuser or provoke abuse, thereby reenacting their childhood experience.
mechanism: The schema distorts perception and behavior such that innocent partners are perceived as threatening, or the person engages in behaviors that provoke negative responses, confirming the belief that relationships are dangerous.
behavioral_signature: Setting tests that cannot be passed
Accusing partners of trying to hurt when they are not
Magnifying disloyalties and minimizing acts of love
Becoming the abuser in the relationship

intervention.verbal: Can you name times she deliberately set out to hurt you?
intervention.behavioral: List evidence for and against partner's trustworthiness objectively
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your childhood experiences may be causing you to see threat where there is none. Examining the actual evidence can help distinguish past from present.

exact_quotes: Even when you are in a good relationship, you may do things to turn it into an abusive one. You may become the abuser or the abused. Either way you reenact your childhood abuse.
You can twist the things they say, so innocent remarks take on the cast of cuts and insults.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Case Example: Frank's Inability to Trust Adrienne
description: Frank repeatedly brings up a minor incident from before marriage (Adrienne meeting ex-boyfriend for lunch) as evidence she cannot be trusted, despite lack of evidence of wrongdoing. Adrienne clarifies she was simply letting Joe down easy and has been faithful. Frank acknowledges rationally she is right but emotionally cannot take the chance to trust.
mechanism: Early abuse created such profound fear of betrayal that even minimal ambiguous events become proof of untrustworthiness. The pain of hoping and being disappointed as a child makes trust feel too risky.

intervention.verbal: THERAPIST: Can you name times she deliberately set out to hurt you? ... Can you name any other times she set out to hurt you? FRANK: Nah. I mean, I know she's right. But still I can't take that chance. I can't trust her. I can't believe she won't let me down.
intervention.behavioral: List all evidence of trustworthiness versus untrustworthiness
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: It hurt too much as a child to hope and be disappointed. We need to examine whether current evidence supports your fears or whether you are reliving old pain.

exact_quotes: Perhaps it hurt Frank too much as a child to hope and be disappointed. It was a long time before he was willing to take that risk again.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Pervasive Worldview of Malevolent Others
description: When abuse was pervasive, the entire worldview becomes organized around the belief that people cannot be trusted, are out to hurt you, and secretly enjoy your suffering. This becomes the emotional tone surrounding all close relationships.
mechanism: Generalization from early abusive relationships to all relationships creates a schema that filters all interpersonal information through a lens of threat and malicious intent.
behavioral_signature: Expecting others to enjoy causing harm
Feeling unsafe in all intimate contexts

intervention.verbal: Your basic sense of people is that they are out to hurt you and secretly enjoy your suffering. This was true of your abusers, but is it true of everyone?
intervention.behavioral: Identify specific people who do not fit this pattern
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When abuse was severe, it's natural to expect everyone to be like your abusers. But many people in the world are kinder than those in your family.

exact_quotes: Depending upon the pervasiveness of your abuse, your whole world view may be based upon the idea that people cannot be trusted. Your basic sense of people is that they are out to hurt you and secretly enjoy your suffering.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Self-Devaluation That Invites Mistreatment
description: Individuals with the Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap may engage in behaviors that lower their value in relationships: giving in too easily, putting themselves down, allowing partners to take advantage, sending messages that they are not worth treating well. This can encourage even good partners to treat them badly.
mechanism: Self-devaluation communicates to partners that mistreatment is acceptable, creating a self-fulfilling prophecy where the person is treated poorly, confirming beliefs about relationships.

intervention.verbal: You lower your value in the relationship by putting yourself down and allowing mistreatment. This sends the message that you are not worth treating well.
intervention.behavioral: Practice assertive responses and boundary-setting in role-plays
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you act as if you deserve poor treatment, partners may begin to believe it. Valuing yourself highly encourages others to do the same.

exact_quotes: You may also do things that encourage partners who might otherwise be good to treat you badly. You do this by lowering your value in the relationship: you give in too easily to whatever the person wants, put yourself down, allow your partner to take advantage of you, send out messages that you are not worth treating well.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Case Example: Madeline's Self-Devaluation with Alan
description: Madeline describes repeatedly telling Alan that he thought he was too good for her because she slept with him quickly and had slept around. Eventually, he began to believe it and left her. This illustrates how self-devaluing statements can become self-fulfilling prophecies.
mechanism: By repeatedly communicating her belief that she was damaged goods and unworthy, Madeline convinced Alan of what she believed about herself, driving him away.
behavioral_signature: Telling partners they think they're too good for her

intervention.verbal: MADELINE: I used to always tell him, 'You just think you're too good for me because I slept with you the first night we met.' THERAPIST: What happened? MADELINE: I guess eventually he started to believe it because he ended up leaving me.
intervention.behavioral: Identify and replace self-devaluing communications
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you constantly put yourself down, others may come to believe what you say about yourself.

exact_quotes: I used to always tell him, 'You just think you're too good for me because I slept with you the first night we met.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Helplessness to Defend Self Against Abuse
description: Despite recognizing mistreatment, individuals with the Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap may feel unable to defend themselves, experiencing the same paralysis felt during childhood abuse. This manifests as an inability to say no or stand up for oneself.
mechanism: Childhood abuse conditions a freeze response and learned helplessness. The presence of perceived threat in adulthood triggers the same immobilized state, making self-protection impossible.

intervention.verbal: No matter what they did, I couldn't say no.
intervention.behavioral: Practice assertive responses; use imagery to grow stronger in threatening scenes
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: As a child, you couldn't protect yourself. But you are no longer helpless. You can learn to claim the power of an adult.

exact_quotes: When men behaved sadistically toward her, she had that old childhood feeling of not being able to move. She was not able to stand up for herself. As she says, 'No matter what they did, I couldn't say no.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Counterattack Response Mode in Mistrust and Abuse
description: When individuals adopt the belief that 'the best defense is a good offense,' they preemptively attack others before being attacked. This counterattack coping style leads to aggressive behavior that paradoxically brings about the feared mistreatment or drives others away.
mechanism: Expecting attack, the person strikes first, not noticing that they become the only one attacking. This aggression either provokes retaliation (confirming beliefs) or alienates the partner entirely.

intervention.primary: Recognizing and interrupting the counterattack pattern
intervention.verbal: You expect the other person to attack, so you make sure you attack first. You do not notice that time passes and you are the only one attacking.
intervention.behavioral: Monitor anger outbursts and identify triggers; practice pause before responding
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your aggressive responses are attempts to protect yourself from expected harm. But they often create the very conflict you fear.

exact_quotes: This is an example of Counterattack as a coping style. This is when you believe the saying, 'The best defense is a good offense.' Since you expect the other person to attack, you make sure you attack first. You do not notice that time passes and you are the only one attacking.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Case Example: Frank's Counterattack Toward Adrienne
description: Adrienne describes how Frank is always accusing her and attacking her, while claiming she puts him down. When she asked if he was okay after slipping on ice, he yelled at her thinking she was mocking him. She is careful not to put him down but he treats her like an enemy.
mechanism: Frank's counterattack mode causes him to perceive caring behavior as mockery and to constantly attack Adrienne preemptively, creating the hostile environment he fears.
behavioral_signature: Yelling in response to caring inquiry

intervention.verbal: ADRIENNE: When I asked him if he was all right, he yelled at me. He thought I was making fun of him. I swear, I wasn't! I just wanted to know if he was all right. ... He acts like I'm his enemy.
intervention.behavioral: Check with partner about actual intentions before reacting
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your past taught you to expect attack. But not everyone is your enemy. Checking intentions before reacting can prevent unnecessary conflict.

exact_quotes: The other night he tripped on the ice and almost fell down. When I asked him if he was all right, he yelled at me. He thought I was making fun of him.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Destructive Anger in Mistrust and Abuse
description: Individuals with the Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap carry significant anger at people for past mistreatment. This anger emerges destructively in relationships, sometimes resulting in abusive or cruel behavior toward loved ones. Outbursts can paradoxically bring about feared mistreatment or drive partners away.
mechanism: Accumulated rage from childhood abuse becomes displaced onto current relationships. Without healthy outlets, anger damages relationships and may lead the person to become an abuser themselves.

intervention.verbal: Your anger comes out in destructive ways. This is the first thing that has to stop. It damages you almost as much as it damages them.
intervention.behavioral: Identify healthy outlets for anger; stop all abusive behavior immediately
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You have good reason to be angry about how you were treated. But directing that anger at people who don't deserve it damages your relationships and yourself.

exact_quotes: Even in good relationships, this anger is bound to become an issue. Your anger comes out in destructive ways. It may be that you have been abusive or cruel to the people you love. This is the first thing that has to stop. It damages you almost as much as it damages them.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation Lifetrap Questionnaire
description: A 10-item self-report assessment measuring the presence and severity of the Emotional Deprivation lifetrap. Items assess feelings of being unloved, misunderstood, disconnected, and lacking warmth, affection, and emotional attunement from others. Uses a 6-point Likert scale from 'Completely untrue of me' to 'Describes me perfectly.'
mechanism: Identifies core beliefs and experiences related to chronic emotional deprivation, helping patients recognize patterns of unmet emotional needs that originated in childhood and persist into adulthood.
behavioral_signature: Endorsing items about needing more love
Difficulty letting people love or guide them

intervention.verbal: Questions include: 1. I need more love than I get. 2. No one really understands me. 3. I am often attracted to cold partners who can't meet my needs. 4. I feel disconnected, even from the people who are closest to me. 5. I have not had one special person I love who wants to share him/herself with me and cares deeply about what happens to me. 6. No one is there to give me warmth, holding, and affection. 7. I do not have someone who really listens and is tuned into my true needs and feelings. 8. It is hard for me to let people guide or protect me, even though it is what I want inside. 9. It is hard for me to let people love me. 10. I am lonely a lot of the time.
intervention.behavioral: Complete questionnaire honestly, sum scores, interpret using provided ranges
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This questionnaire helps identify whether you have developed patterns of expecting emotional deprivation in relationships, which can guide our work together.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation Questionnaire Scoring Guide
description: Interpretation guidelines for the Emotional Deprivation questionnaire scores. Scores range from 10-60, with specific cutoffs indicating severity levels from 'very low' to 'very high' lifetrap relevance.
mechanism: Provides standardized interpretation of self-report data to determine clinical significance of emotional deprivation patterns.

intervention.verbal: 10-19 Very low. This lifetrap probably does not apply to you. 20-29 Fairly low. This lifetrap may only apply occasionally. 30-39 Moderate. This lifetrap is an issue in your life. 40-49 High. This is definitely an important lifetrap for you. 50-60 Very high. This is definitely one of your core lifetraps.
intervention.behavioral: Use score to determine treatment priority
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your score helps us understand how much this pattern affects your life and whether we should focus on it in treatment.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: The Experience of Emotional Deprivation
description: Emotional deprivation is experienced as a sense of something missing, a feeling of emptiness and aloneness. It is harder to define than other lifetraps because the original deprivation began so early, before the child had words to describe it. The experience is captured by the image of a neglected child—a sad, heavy knowledge of being destined to be alone.
mechanism: Because deprivation occurred before verbal development, patients often cannot articulate what is wrong. They experience chronic emptiness and disconnection without understanding its source, leading to pervasive loneliness and difficulty identifying their needs.
behavioral_signature: Feeling alone even when with others
Saying 'I feel alone' or 'I feel detached'

intervention.behavioral: Help patient identify and name the feeling of emotional emptiness
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The feeling of something missing that you experience has its roots in early childhood, before you had words for it. Learning to recognize and name this feeling is the first step toward healing.

exact_quotes: The experience of emotional deprivation is harder to define than some of the other lifetraps. Often it is not crystallized into thoughts. This is because the original deprivation began so early, before you had the words to describe it.
Emotional deprivation feels like something is missing. It is a feeling of emptiness. Perhaps the image that most captures its meaning is that of a neglected child.
It is a feeling of aloneness, of nobody there. It is a sad and heavy sense of knowledge that you are destined to be alone.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Insatiable Quality of Emotional Deprivation
description: Some people with the Emotional Deprivation lifetrap show a tendency to be demanding in relationships, experiencing an insatiable quality where no matter how much people give them, it never feels like enough. This persistent feeling of deprivation occurs even in the face of clear evidence of caring.
mechanism: The early emotional void creates a bottomless pit that cannot be filled by current relationships. Even generous partners cannot compensate for the primal deprivation, leading to chronic dissatisfaction and accusations that partners don't care enough.
what_activates_it: Receiving gifts or attention from partner
behavioral_signature: Comparing what you give versus receive
Others telling you that you are too needy

intervention.verbal: Ask yourself, 'Do people keep telling me that I am too needy, or that I ask for too much?'
intervention.behavioral: Track instances of feeling deprived and objectively assess what was given
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you feel disappointed even when someone is clearly caring for you, this is a sign that your lifetrap is triggered rather than a sign that the person isn't giving enough.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Caregiver Role as Compensation for Emotional Deprivation
description: People with Emotional Deprivation often compensate by choosing helping professions or becoming the caregiver in relationships. They give nurturance to others as a way to indirectly address their own unmet emotional needs, always being the listener while never sharing their own problems.
mechanism: By focusing on others' needs, the person avoids confronting their own deprivation while vicariously experiencing the nurturance they provide. This creates an imbalanced relationship where they understand others better than others understand them.
what_activates_it: Opportunities to help or counsel others
behavioral_signature: Choosing helping professions (social work, nursing, therapy)
Always being the listener in friendships
Understanding others better than being understood

intervention.behavioral: Track ratio of giving to receiving emotional support in relationships
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your ability to care for others is valuable, but it may also be a way of avoiding your own unmet needs. You deserve to receive as much as you give.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Chronic Disappointment Pattern in Emotional Deprivation
description: A key sign of the Emotional Deprivation lifetrap is feeling chronically disappointed in other people over a long period of time. Not a single case of disappointment, but a pattern of experiences leading to the conclusion that you cannot count on people to be there for you emotionally.
mechanism: The lifetrap filters experiences to confirm the belief that emotional needs won't be met, leading to hypersensitivity to perceived slights and a cumulative sense that relationships always disappoint.
behavioral_signature: Pattern of feeling unable to count on others emotionally

intervention.behavioral: Review relationship history for objective evidence of deprivation versus lifetrap distortion
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you notice a pattern of chronic disappointment across many relationships, this may reflect your lifetrap rather than the actual behavior of all these different people.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Origins of Emotional Deprivation in Maternal Figure
description: The origins of emotional deprivation lie in the person who serves as the maternal figure, who is chiefly responsible for giving the child emotional nurturance. This first relationship becomes the prototype for all close relationships that follow, bearing the stamp of that first experience with mother throughout the individual's life.
mechanism: The maternal relationship establishes the template for all intimate relationships. Deprivation in this primary attachment creates a working model that expects and recreates emotional absence in future relationships.

intervention.behavioral: Explore relationship with maternal figure through imagery
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your first close relationship—usually with your mother—became the template for how you expect all close relationships to feel. Understanding this connection helps explain current patterns.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Six Origins of Emotional Deprivation
description: The child received less than average maternal nurturance in six specific dimensions: cold/unaffectionate mother who doesn't hold the child enough; child doesn't feel loved, valued, precious, or special; insufficient time and attention; mother not tuned into child's needs and unable to empathize; inadequate soothing preventing child from learning to self-soothe or accept soothing; lack of parental guidance and direction.
mechanism: Each dimension of nurturance that is deficient creates a corresponding gap in the child's emotional development and capacity for intimate connection, leading to chronic feelings of emptiness and unmet needs.

intervention.verbal: Consider: 1. Was mother cold and unaffectionate? Did she not hold and rock you enough? 2. Did you have a sense of being loved and valued—of being precious and special? 3. Did mother give you enough time and attention? 4. Was mother tuned into your needs? Did she empathize with your world? Did she really connect with you? 5. Did mother soothe you adequately? 6. Did your parents guide you and provide a sense of direction?
intervention.behavioral: Use these questions to explore childhood experiences through imagery
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: There are many specific ways a child can be emotionally deprived. Identifying which ones affected you helps target what needs healing.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Case Example: Jed - Severe Emotional Neglect
description: Jed's mother was seventeen when pregnant, and his father refused to acknowledge him. Once his mother realized Jed couldn't win his father back, she lost all interest in him. Jed remembers having no one to take care of him from a very young age. His mother would say 'Be quiet, go to sleep' and ignore him, proceeding as if he wasn't there. Jed experiences such intense loneliness and disconnection that he has considered suicide.
mechanism: Total emotional neglect from both parents created severe deprivation across all dimensions—nurturance, empathy, and protection. This manifests as emotional deadness, inability to connect intimately with anyone, and profound isolation.
behavioral_signature: No closeness with family, friends, or romantic partners
Intense loneliness leading to suicidal thoughts

intervention.behavioral: Imagery exercises to access pain beneath anger

exact_quotes: I am emotionally dead. My lack of intimacy with women applies to all my relationships. I'm not close to anyone, not to any family members, not to friends.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Case Example: Elizabeth - Narcissistic Mother
description: Elizabeth's mother was responsible but narcissistic, viewing her child as an extension of herself rather than a separate person with needs. The mother wanted Elizabeth to marry rich, teaching her to be pretty and charming as the price of her love. When company left, the mother ignored Elizabeth. Elizabeth grew up to fulfill this role, marrying a rich man who expects her to perform for company and ignores her otherwise.
mechanism: The mother's narcissistic use of the child created a false, conditional love dependent on performance. The child's true emotional needs were invisible, leading to chronic feelings of invisibility and a pattern of relationships where she performs for others without having her own needs met.
what_activates_it: Being alone with partner after social events
behavioral_signature: Always the listener, never shares problems

intervention.behavioral: Imagery exercises to access anger beneath pain

exact_quotes: She taught me to be pretty and charming. It was the price of her love. She taught me to perform for company. She took me shopping. She dressed me up like a doll. But when we were done shopping and the company was gone, she ignored me. I just wasn't relevant anymore.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Case Example: Dustin - Cold Successful Mother
description: Dustin's mother was a successful lawyer who focused almost exclusively on work, remaining self-absorbed and withdrawn at home. Though she provided the best material things, there was a sense of coldness. She secretly regarded Dustin as a nuisance and blamed him for the low intensity of her feelings toward him. Dustin covered his grief over the absent mother with a hard shell of anger, appearing as a spoiled, petulant boy.
mechanism: Material provision without emotional warmth creates a confusing form of deprivation. The child received 'everything' materially but nothing emotionally, leading to difficulty recognizing the deprivation and a Counterattacking style that covers grief with anger and demands.
behavioral_signature: Initially saying mother gave him everything

intervention.behavioral: List past relationships to identify pattern

exact_quotes: Before I started therapy I really had no idea I was caught in this process. Each time I thought I just happened to fall in love with a woman who just happened to be unavailable.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Chemistry Scale Rule-of-Thumb for Partner Selection
description: A practical screening tool where patients rate romantic chemistry on a 0-10 scale. If chemistry is rated 9 or 10, this signals potential lifetrap activation rather than genuine compatibility. The technique helps patients pause before pursuing relationships driven by schema chemistry.
mechanism: High chemistry often indicates that a partner triggers childhood lifetraps rather than offering healthy connection. The intensity feels compelling because it recreates familiar emotional patterns from deprivation, creating false sense of rightness.
what_activates_it: meeting a new potential romantic partner
behavioral_signature: rating potential partners on chemistry scale

intervention.verbal: If you meet someone for whom you feel a high degree of chemistry, rate how much chemistry on a 0 to 10 scale. If you rate the person a 9 or 10, then think twice about becoming involved with this person. The strong chemistry you feel will be based on lifetraps that they trigger in you, rather than positive qualities that will make the relationship last.
intervention.behavioral: Before pursuing any new romantic interest, rate chemistry level. If 9-10, write down what specifically triggers the attraction and compare to past relationship patterns.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Very high chemistry often means this person reminds you of the emotional patterns from your childhood. Partners rated 6-8 may bring deeper fulfillment because the connection is based on real compatibility, not lifetrap activation.

exact_quotes: If you rate the person a 9 or 10, then think twice about becoming involved with this person
more often, the strong chemistry you feel will be based on lifetraps that they trigger in you, rather than positive qualities that will make the relationship last

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Accepting Moderate Chemistry in Healthy Relationships
description: Guidance that patients should not settle for 0-5 chemistry but should actively consider partners in the 6-8 range. These moderate-chemistry relationships often provide the deepest fulfillment and lasting intimacy.
mechanism: Breaking the pattern of pursuing only high-chemistry (lifetrap-activating) partners while learning that genuine connection can develop with emotionally available partners who generate moderate initial attraction.
what_activates_it: comparing current partner to past intense relationships
behavioral_signature: staying in relationships despite initial boredom
giving time for connection to develop

intervention.primary: Cognitive reframing of what healthy attraction feels like
intervention.verbal: We are not saying that you have to settle for spending the rest of your life with a partner who only generates a response of 0-5 in you. We feel that there has to be some chemistry for the relationship to work. But, if there is only romantic chemistry, it almost certainly will not work in the long run. There are plenty of 6's, 7's, and 8's out there. One of them might bring you the deep fulfillment of an intimate, loving relationship, perhaps for the first time in your life.
intervention.behavioral: Commit to giving relationships with moderate chemistry at least 3-6 months before evaluating.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Moderate chemistry with an emotionally available partner can grow into deep fulfillment. Intense chemistry often fades or leads to pain, while steady warmth can become the foundation for lasting love.

exact_quotes: There are plenty of 6's, 7's, and 8's out there. One of them might bring you the deep fulfillment of an intimate, loving relationship, perhaps for the first time in your life

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Give Healthy Relationships a Chance Despite Boredom
description: Step 5 in emotional deprivation treatment: when in a relationship with an emotionally generous partner, resist the urge to leave when feeling bored or dissatisfied. The strange sensation of having needs met can initially feel uncomfortable or unexciting.
mechanism: Boredom in healthy relationships often reflects unfamiliarity with nurturing rather than genuine incompatibility. Staying through initial discomfort allows new relational patterns to develop.
behavioral_signature: expressing boredom with partner in therapy
considering ending relationship despite good qualities
comparing current partner unfavorably to past

intervention.verbal: Many times people with this lifetrap feel bored and dissatisfied in healthy relationships, and they want to walk away. Do not walk away so fast, even if the relationship seems unexciting. Maybe you just need to get used to the strange sensation of having your emotional needs met.
intervention.behavioral: Make a list of concrete evidence that partner is emotionally generous. When urge to leave arises, review list and stay for agreed minimum period.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The boredom you feel may actually be the unfamiliar experience of having your needs met. Give yourself time to adjust to this new sensation before making decisions.

exact_quotes: Many times people with this lifetrap feel bored and dissatisfied in healthy relationships, and they want to walk away
Maybe you just need to get used to the strange sensation of having your emotional needs met

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dustin and Michelle: Working Through Fading Chemistry
description: After the failed relationship with Christine (cold partner), Dustin became involved with Michelle, a warm and caring woman. Though initially attracted, his chemistry faded as they deepened the relationship. Despite feeling bored and wanting to end it, positive signs emerged: he was allowing her to care for him, and his boredom was actually masked irritation about unmet needs he wasn't expressing.
mechanism: Demonstrates how emotional deprivation manifests even in healthy relationships through secret need-keeping, leading to anger at partner for not meeting unexpressed needs.
behavioral_signature: not telling partner what is needed

intervention.verbal: You say you're bored, but let's look closer. Could you actually be irritated? What might you be needing from Michelle that you haven't asked for?
intervention.behavioral: Identify one need you haven't expressed to Michelle and share it this week.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: What feels like boredom is often hidden anger about needs you haven't voiced. When you keep needs secret, even a caring partner can't meet them.

exact_quotes: Dustin realized that most of the time, in fact, he was not really bored with Michelle but irritated. He was angry that she was not giving him what he needed. Of course, Dustin was not telling Michelle what he needed.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Secret Need-Keeping Pattern
description: A common surrender behavior in Emotional Deprivation where the person keeps their emotional needs hidden from their partner, then feels angry when needs aren't met. This ensures deprivation continues even with emotionally available partners.
mechanism: By not expressing needs, the person guarantees they won't be met, confirming the belief that emotional needs cannot be satisfied. The anger that results further damages the relationship.
what_activates_it: opportunity to ask for what is wanted
behavioral_signature: not telling partner what is needed

intervention.verbal: You keep what you want a secret, then get angry when you do not get it. Keeping your needs secret is a way of surrendering to your lifetrap. You make sure that even though your partner is a warm person, your needs still will not get met. If you are with a loving partner, tell the person what you need.
intervention.behavioral: Each day, express one emotional need directly to partner using 'I need...' statements.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you keep needs secret, you guarantee they won't be met. This confirms your belief that no one can meet your needs, but it's actually you preventing it.

exact_quotes: You keep what you want a secret, then get angry when you do not get it. Keeping your needs secret is a way of surrendering to your lifetrap.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Share Vulnerability and Ask for What You Want
description: Critical treatment step requiring patients to allow themselves to be vulnerable with caring partners—to ask for what they need and let partners nurture, protect, and understand them. This directly counters the self-protective wall built in childhood.
mechanism: The wall of invulnerability that protected against childhood disappointment now prevents adult intimacy. Taking the risk of vulnerability with trustworthy partners creates corrective emotional experiences.

intervention.verbal: Allow your partner to take care of you, protect you, and understand you. We realize that this can be frightening. It means making yourself vulnerable to your partner. You have become very invested in doing the opposite, keeping yourself invulnerable to protect yourself from disappointment. As a child you had a good reason for this. But ask yourself, 'This time, is it different? Can I trust this person?' If the answer is 'yes,' perhaps you should take a chance.
intervention.behavioral: Share one vulnerable feeling or need with partner this week. Notice their response and report back.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The wall you built to protect yourself as a child is now keeping out the love you need. With a trustworthy partner, the risk of vulnerability is worth taking.

exact_quotes: Allow your partner to take care of you, protect you, and understand you
ask yourself, 'This time, is it different? Can I trust this person?' If the answer is 'yes,' perhaps you should take a chance

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Stop Blaming and Express Needs Directly
description: Step 6 in emotional deprivation treatment: replace patterns of resentment accumulation, angry demanding, and blaming with calm, direct expression of needs. Focus on the vulnerable feelings beneath the anger.
mechanism: Accumulated resentment and angry demands drive partners away and make need-meeting less likely. Showing vulnerability beneath anger increases partner's ability to respond with care.

intervention.verbal: Do not harbor resentment. Express your needs directly to your partner. When you are angry, tell your partner how you feel. Do it calmly, without blame. Below the anger is a feeling of hurt, of vulnerability. Share that with your partner. If you show only the angry, demanding surface, you will drive your partner away and make it less likely that he or she can meet your needs.
intervention.behavioral: When anger arises, pause and identify the vulnerable feeling underneath. Express the vulnerability rather than the anger.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Anger and demands push people away. When you show the hurt underneath your anger, your partner can actually respond to what you really need.

exact_quotes: Below the anger is a feeling of hurt, of vulnerability. Share that with your partner
If you show only the angry, demanding surface, you will drive your partner away

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Communication as Connection Foundation
description: Core principle that successful intimate relationships require willingness to communicate thoughts and feelings, share oneself, and connect. This directly counters the emotional deprivation pattern of withdrawal and secrecy.
mechanism: Without communication, partners cannot know each other's needs. Sharing creates connection; withholding maintains isolation and deprivation.

intervention.verbal: Much of what we are saying comes down to communication. If you want a relationship to work, you have to be willing to communicate your thoughts and feelings to your partner. You have to share yourself. You have to connect.
intervention.behavioral: Daily practice of sharing one thought and one feeling with partner.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Relationships work when both people share their inner worlds. Keeping thoughts and feelings inside keeps you isolated, even in a relationship.

exact_quotes: If you want a relationship to work, you have to be willing to communicate your thoughts and feelings to your partner. You have to share yourself. You have to connect.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Gradual Lifetrap Change Through Persistent Effort
description: The realistic outlook that lifetrap change is neither sudden nor easy. It requires persistent effort against the lifetrap whenever it is triggered—engaging thoughts, feelings, and behavior. The more severe the childhood damage, the harder the work required.
mechanism: Lifetraps are deeply ingrained patterns that resist change. Progress occurs through repeated countering of the lifetrap across all dimensions of experience.
behavioral_signature: working on thoughts, feelings, and behaviors simultaneously

intervention.verbal: It is not easy to change. How much you change is a function of how hard you work and persist. Your lifetrap will not fall away suddenly. It is a matter of slowly chipping away at the lifetrap—of countering the lifetrap each time it is triggered. You must throw your whole being against the lifetrap—your thoughts, feelings, and behavior.
intervention.behavioral: Track small victories and improvements to maintain motivation during gradual change.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Change takes time and persistent effort. Each time you counter your lifetrap, you chip away at it a little more.

exact_quotes: Your Emotional Deprivation lifetrap will not fall away suddenly. It is a matter of slowly chipping away at the lifetrap
You must throw your whole being against the lifetrap—your thoughts, feelings, and behavior

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Severity-Effort Relationship in Lifetrap Recovery
description: The principle that more severe childhood damage requires harder therapeutic work—an additional unfairness beyond the original trauma. Seriously damaged individuals may need professional help.
mechanism: Early and severe emotional deprivation creates more entrenched neural and behavioral patterns that require more intensive intervention to modify.

intervention.verbal: It is sad that the more you were damaged as a child, the harder you will have to work. This is one more unfairness in the string of unfairnesses against you. If you were seriously damaged as a child, you may need professional help.
intervention.behavioral: Assess severity of childhood deprivation and plan treatment intensity accordingly.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: If your childhood was very difficult, healing will take more work. This isn't fair, but it's reality. Professional help can make the work more effective.

exact_quotes: It is sad that the more you were damaged as a child, the harder you will have to work. This is one more unfairness in the string of unfairnesses against you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion Partner Choice Patterns
description: People with Social Exclusion lifetrap may feel romantic chemistry with opposite types who have outward signs of belonging they lack—partners who are good-looking, high status, popular, comfortable in social situations, or normal and conventional. Alternatively, they may be attracted to other outsiders with whom they feel a special bond.
mechanism: Choosing sociable partners can either help overcome the lifetrap by forcing socialization, or reinforce it by creating excessive dependence on the partner to smooth social situations. Choosing other outsiders provides comfort but may maintain the pattern of being different from mainstream groups.
what_activates_it: desire to feel part of in-group
behavioral_signature: pursuing partners with high social status
becoming excessively dependent on partner in social situations
making fun of normal people with fellow outsider partner
keeping romantic partner separate from other social worlds

intervention.primary: Awareness building and balanced partner selection
intervention.verbal: Consider whether you are choosing this partner to avoid addressing your own social discomfort. Are you becoming more dependent on them to handle social situations for you?
intervention.behavioral: Practice initiating social interactions independently even when with sociable partner
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your partner choice can either help you grow socially or keep you stuck. Being aware of these patterns helps you make healthier choices.

exact_quotes: You may be drawn to people who are the opposite of you—who have the outward signs of belonging that you lack.
You might feel a special bond with other socially excluded people. You can bolster one another in feeling different.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Adam - Outsider Bonding Relationship Pattern
description: Adam describes his girlfriend Susan as a fellow outcast—an arty type who wore black and painted weird pictures. Together they made fun of everyone else and felt sorry for others for being 'pathetically and boringly normal.' This illustrates how outsiders band together for mutual support.
mechanism: The couple's shared identity as different from mainstream provides emotional comfort and elevates their status in their own eyes, but may maintain avoidance of mainstream social integration.
behavioral_signature: making fun of conventional people together
exact_quotes: My girlfriend Susan was an outcast also. She was this arty type who always wore black and painted these weird pictures. When we were together, we made fun of everyone else. We felt sorry for everyone else for being so pathetically and boringly normal.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Adam - Alienated From All Groups
description: Adam describes feeling that he doesn't fit in anywhere. He is athletic but not a jock, not an intellectual, not a bohemian, not a yuppie. He feels torn between all these worlds and doesn't really identify with any of them, representing total alienation from all social subgroups.
mechanism: The inability to identify with any social subgroup prevents even the partial belonging that outcasts often find by banding together with similar others.
behavioral_signature: inability to identify with any group
chronic sense of not fitting in anywhere

intervention.primary: Focus on individual connections rather than group membership

exact_quotes: I don't fit in anywhere. I'm athletic, but I'm not a jock. But I'm not an intellectual either. I'm not a bohemian, but I'm not a yuppie. I'm torn between all these worlds, and I don't really identify with any of them.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion Career Choices
description: People with Social Exclusion lifetrap are drawn to careers that do not involve much meaningful social interplay. They may become artists, scientists, freelance writers, reporters, computer professionals, or start their own companies. They avoid jobs requiring networking or political expertise for advancement.
mechanism: Career choices serve as long-term escape from social situations. The solitary nature of certain work protects against social anxiety but prevents opportunities for corrective social experiences.
behavioral_signature: starting own business to control social interactions
working from home or traveling frequently

intervention.primary: Gradual exposure to workplace social demands
intervention.verbal: How might your career choice be serving to protect you from social situations? What opportunities for connection are you missing?
intervention.behavioral: Identify one workplace social activity to engage in weekly
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your career provides security but may also limit your social growth. Finding balance allows you to expand while still honoring your need for meaningful work.

exact_quotes: You are probably drawn to activities that do not involve much meaningful social interplay.
But the last thing you are likely to choose is a job in which your upward mobility depends on having to make contacts and connections with other people.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Debra - Avoiding Client Entertainment
description: Debra describes having work that requires entertaining clients—taking them to dinner and cocktails—but she avoids it. This avoidance is hurting her professionally as she doesn't keep her clients. This illustrates how Social Exclusion can negatively impact career success.
mechanism: Social avoidance in work contexts leads to professional consequences, creating a self-fulfilling prophecy of failure that may reinforce feelings of not belonging.
behavioral_signature: losing clients due to lack of relationship building

intervention.primary: Gradual exposure to client entertainment situations

exact_quotes: I have the kind of work where I'm supposed to be entertaining clients, taking them out to dinner and for cocktails, but I avoid it. It's really hurting me. I don't keep my clients.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Escape as Foundation of Social Exclusion
description: Escape is the primary coping mechanism for Social Exclusion and serves as the rock upon which the lifetrap is built. Avoidance of social situations ensures nothing can change—skills cannot improve, beliefs cannot be disconfirmed. The person may be more comfortable but remains stuck.
mechanism: Escape prevents exposure to corrective experiences that could disconfirm negative beliefs about social acceptance. Without new experiences, the childhood framework remains unchallenged and the lifetrap persists indefinitely.
what_activates_it: fear of being exposed as different

intervention.primary: Shift from escape to confrontation and mastery
intervention.verbal: Changing requires a basic shift from a stance of Escape to one of confrontation and mastery. The people who overcome social exclusion are the ones who make this shift.
intervention.behavioral: Create hierarchy of avoided situations and begin gradual exposure
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your avoidance keeps you safe from anxiety in the short term, but it prevents you from ever finding out that you might actually be accepted.

exact_quotes: Escape is your primary means for coping with your lifetrap. It is the rock upon which your Social Exclusion is built.
Your avoidance of social situations ensures that nothing can change for you. Your skills cannot improve. Your beliefs cannot be disconfirmed.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion Treatment Steps Overview
description: Twelve-step treatment protocol for Social Exclusion lifetrap: 1) Understand childhood exclusion, 2) List anxiety-provoking situations, 3) List avoided situations, 4) List counterattack behaviors, 5) Identify alienating qualities, 6) Plan to overcome real flaws, 7) Reevaluate unchangeable flaws, 8) Make flashcards, 9) Create exposure hierarchy, 10) Initiate conversations, 11) Be yourself, 12) Stop overcompensating.
mechanism: Comprehensive approach addresses cognitive, behavioral, emotional, and interpersonal dimensions of the lifetrap through understanding origins, challenging beliefs, gradual exposure, and authentic self-presentation.
what_activates_it: engagement in therapy for Social Exclusion

intervention.behavioral: Work through each step systematically, moving to next step only after mastering current one
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These steps will help you understand where your feelings of being an outsider come from, challenge unrealistic beliefs, and gradually build confidence in social situations.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion Imagery - Connecting to Childhood
description: First treatment step involves remembering childhood experiences of feeling different or excluded. Patient darkens the room, sits comfortably, and lets images come of feeling different or inferior as a child, often starting with a current triggering situation and bridging back to childhood memories.
mechanism: Accessing childhood memories of exclusion creates emotional connection to the origins of the lifetrap, allowing integration of past and present experiences and preparation for schema healing work.
what_activates_it: current triggering situation as starting point
behavioral_signature: recalling memories of being laughed at, humiliated, teased, bullied
recalling memories of being alone or not fitting in

intervention.verbal: Remember when you felt different or inferior. You can start with an image of a current situation that triggers feelings of Social Exclusion. Stay with it, and let an image come of when you felt the same way as a child.
intervention.behavioral: After accessing childhood memory, bring adult self into image to comfort the excluded child
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding where your feelings of being an outsider originated helps you see that you're reacting to the past, not just the present.

exact_quotes: Remember not to force the images. Just close your eyes and let them come.
Memories of social exclusion are painful. We want you to comfort that excluded child.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Debra - Party to Childhood Kickball Imagery
description: In session, Debra describes a party image: standing next to a guy, relieved someone is talking to her, but so nervous she talks fast with anxious eyes. He becomes uncomfortable and walks away. Bridging to childhood, she sees herself at friend Gina's house—kids picking sides for kickball, being picked last, and hearing the team groan when they get her.
mechanism: The imagery demonstrates how current social anxiety connects directly to childhood experiences of rejection and humiliation, showing the lifetrap's developmental origins.
behavioral_signature: relief at having anyone to talk to

intervention.primary: Affect bridge from present to past

exact_quotes: I'm so relieved that someone is talking to me, that I don't have to stand there alone, but I'm so nervous I can't talk to the guy.
I'm the last kid to be picked, and, when the team gets me, they all start to groan.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Adam - Waterfall Window Imagery
description: Adam describes a childhood camping trip memory of standing in a waterfall while other kids swam nearby. Through the blurry water and muffled sound, he suddenly felt profound loneliness—that it was always like this, with everyone coming at him from far away, watching like through a window while everyone else plays and is normal, and he's outside looking in.
mechanism: This imagery captures the essential experience of Social Exclusion—being physically present but emotionally disconnected, observing others' normal interactions from a removed, isolated position.
behavioral_signature: physical isolation even in group setting
sense of fundamental difference from others

intervention.verbal: I bring myself in, and I dive into the falls where my childhood self is standing, and I tell him that he is not alone anymore. I am here, and I will help him connect with other people.
intervention.behavioral: Bring adult self into imagery to comfort childhood self

exact_quotes: I had the thought that it was always like this, with everyone coming at me from far away. And I'm watching, like through a window. Everyone else is out there playing and being normal with each other, and I'm outside looking in.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion Step 2 - List Anxiety-Provoking Non-Avoided Situations
description: Patient creates list of everyday social situations that make them anxious or uncomfortable but which they do not avoid. Examples include parties, meetings, eating in public, talking in front of groups, dating, talking to authority figures, being assertive, having conversations.
mechanism: Identifying situations that trigger anxiety but are not completely avoided provides material for cognitive work and reveals patterns of perceived undesirability.

intervention.verbal: For each situation, write down: 1) The situation, 2) Ways you feel undesirable, different, or inferior in that situation, 3) Your worst-case scenario of what could go wrong.
intervention.behavioral: Complete written chart with three columns for each anxiety-provoking situation
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding exactly what makes you uncomfortable and what you fear will help us target your specific concerns.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Debra - Anxiety Situations List
description: Debra's list of social situations that make her anxious but she doesn't escape: greeting doorman, calling prospective clients, lunch at work cafeteria, church coffee hour, meetings with coworkers, talking to unfamiliar people, getting to know apartment building neighbors.
mechanism: The list reveals the pervasive nature of social anxiety affecting even routine daily interactions, showing how the lifetrap colors all social contact.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Journey from Loneliness to Connection
description: The final message for overcoming Social Exclusion frames recovery as a positive journey toward connection rather than merely escaping loneliness. The ultimate reward is a satisfying social life where one feels part of a group or community.
mechanism: Reframing recovery as movement toward positive outcomes rather than away from negative states increases motivation and helps patients visualize concrete benefits of change.
behavioral_signature: Going out several nights per week
Sharing truth about oneself with friends

intervention.verbal: The journey out of Social Exclusion is a journey from loneliness to connection. Try to see it in this positive light. The ultimate reward is a satisfying social life. You can feel part of a group or the community. This is a vital part of life, of which you are now deprived. Why miss out in this way?
intervention.behavioral: Track progress toward social connection goals; celebrate social achievements
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Recovery isn't just about stopping loneliness—it's about gaining something wonderful: real connection with others.

exact_quotes: The journey out of Social Exclusion is a journey from loneliness to connection.
The ultimate reward is a satisfying social life. You can feel part of a group or the community. This is a vital part of life, of which you are now deprived.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence Lifetrap Definition
description: The Dependence lifetrap involves feeling that life itself is overwhelming and believing you cannot cope or take care of yourself in the world. People with this lifetrap feel they must turn to others for help to survive, experiencing a constant struggle to fulfill normal adult responsibilities. At the core is a sense of inadequacy—feeling like a small child in a world of adults.
mechanism: The belief in personal incompetence leads to avoidance of adult responsibilities, which prevents skill development and reinforces the belief. Reliance on others confirms the schema that one cannot function alone.

intervention.behavioral: Systematically face tasks previously avoided; build competence through direct experience
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your dependence is one large untested hypothesis. You have never found out that you actually can function alone.

exact_quotes: If you have the Dependence lifetrap, life itself seems overwhelming. You feel that you cannot cope.
An image that captures the essence of dependence is that of a small child who feels that suddenly the world is too much and starts crying for mommy.
At the core of your experience of dependence is the sense that it is a constant struggle to fulfill the normal responsibilities of adult living.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Margaret: Trapped in Abusive Marriage with Agoraphobia
description: Margaret is 28 years old and feels trapped in her marriage to an abusive husband. She is agoraphobic with panic attacks and avoids many situations including trains, restaurants, grocery stores, shopping malls, crowds, and movie theaters. Her husband Anthony has been unemployed for two years and blames her for all his problems. She has a frightened look and raises protective impulses in others.
