mechanism: Dependence lifetrap keeps Margaret trapped in an abusive relationship because she fears being alone more than she fears the abuse. Agoraphobia represents the dramatic expression of dependence—inability to venture into the world without someone to take care of her.

intervention.verbal: What are you afraid of? What might happen to you?
intervention.behavioral: Gradual exposure to avoided situations while building sense of competence
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your fear of being alone keeps you trapped in a harmful situation.

exact_quotes: What if something happens to me and there is no one to take care of me?
My telephone is my lifeline to the world.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: William: Still Dependent on Parents at 34
description: William is 34 years old, still living with his parents, working in his father's accounting firm, and unable to commit to his girlfriend Carol despite dating her for years. He has a frightened, shy, timid manner. He left home only once for college but returned after one year. He has tremendous work anxiety and cannot decide if Carol is the right person, paralyzed by her ultimatum to marry or end the relationship.
mechanism: Overprotective father did everything for William, including schoolwork, preventing him from developing competence or trust in his own judgment. This created enmeshment and inability to separate from family or make independent decisions.

intervention.verbal: What was happening with you when you became critical of Carol?
intervention.behavioral: Make decisions without seeking parental approval; tolerate uncertainty
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You've never tested whether you can function on your own because you've always had someone doing things for you.

exact_quotes: What if I'm making a mistake, what if she's not the one? What if there's someone better? How do I know if she's the right one, or if I'm just settling for less?
I feel like an inadequate person.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Christine: Counterdependent Social Worker
description: Christine is 24 years old, a social worker who prides herself on extreme independence and self-reliance. She has supported herself since college and walks through dangerous neighborhoods without fear. After breaking her leg skiing, she had to return to her parents' home and accept help, which caused intense stress and depression. She entered therapy because accepting help from family was intolerable.
mechanism: Christine counterattacks against dependence by overcompensating with extreme independence. Her inability to accept help reveals hidden dependence needs. Each time she accepts assistance, she fears she might become totally dependent.
behavioral_signature: Depression when forced to accept care

intervention.primary: Address hidden dependence through accepting appropriate help
intervention.verbal: I know I would do the same for them in a flash, without even thinking about it. Why can't I accept help from people?
intervention.behavioral: Practice accepting reasonable amounts of help without catastrophizing
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your inability to accept any help is just as much a sign of dependence issues as needing too much help.

exact_quotes: I'm just not the kind of person who likes to be taken care of. I just don't like it.
It is like if I turn to anyone at all I might become totally dependent.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence Questionnaire
description: A 10-item self-report assessment measuring the Dependence lifetrap. Items assess feeling like a child regarding adult responsibilities, inability to cope alone, needing others to take care of you, trouble tackling new tasks without guidance, and lack of trust in own judgment. Scoring ranges from 10-60 with clinical thresholds.
mechanism: Quantifies degree of dependence beliefs and behaviors to identify whether this is a core lifetrap and track treatment progress.

intervention.verbal: Rate each item: 1=Completely untrue of me, 2=Mostly untrue, 3=Slightly more true than untrue, 4=Moderately true, 5=Mostly true, 6=Describes me perfectly. Items: 1) I feel more like a child than an adult when it comes to handling the responsibilities of daily life. 2) I am not capable of getting by on my own. 3) I cannot cope well by myself. 4) Other people can take care of me better than I can take care of myself. 5) I have trouble tackling new tasks unless I have someone to guide me. 6) I can't do anything right. 7) I am inept. 8) I lack common sense. 9) I cannot trust my own judgment. 10) I find everyday life overwhelming.
intervention.behavioral: Complete questionnaire honestly; any 5's or 6's indicate lifetrap may apply even with low total score
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This helps identify if dependence is a core pattern affecting your life.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence Questionnaire Scoring Key
description: Scoring interpretation for the Dependence Questionnaire: 10-19 Very low (probably does not apply), 20-29 Fairly low (may only apply occasionally), 30-39 Moderate (an issue in your life), 40-49 High (definitely important lifetrap), 50-60 Very high (definitely one of your core lifetraps).
mechanism: Provides clinical thresholds for identifying severity of dependence lifetrap and prioritizing treatment targets.

intervention.verbal: 10-19 Very low: This lifetrap probably does not apply to you. 20-29 Fairly low: This lifetrap may only apply occasionally. 30-39 Moderate: This lifetrap is an issue in your life. 40-49 High: This is definitely an important lifetrap for you. 50-60 Very high: This is definitely one of your core lifetraps.
intervention.behavioral: Use score to guide treatment focus and intensity
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your score helps us understand how much dependence affects your daily functioning.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Automatic Thoughts of Incompetence
description: Typical automatic thoughts in the Dependence lifetrap reflect a sense of incompetence: 'This is too much for me,' 'I can't handle this,' 'I'm going to fall apart,' 'I'm not going to be able to handle my responsibilities.' These thoughts are accompanied by desperation and panic.
mechanism: These automatic thoughts maintain dependence by triggering anxiety before attempting tasks, leading to avoidance or seeking others' help, which prevents development of competence and confirms the schema.

intervention.verbal: What evidence do you have that you truly cannot handle this? Have you ever handled similar situations before?
intervention.behavioral: Log automatic thoughts and test them against evidence
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These thoughts feel true but they're predictions, not facts. We can test whether they're accurate.

exact_quotes: Your typical thoughts reflect your sense of incompetence: 'This is too much for me,' 'I can't handle this,' 'I'm going to fall apart,' 'I'm not going to be able to handle my responsibilities.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Fear of Losing Caretakers
description: Dependent individuals have automatic thoughts reflecting fear of abandonment by those they depend on: 'What would I do without this person?,' 'How will I get by on my own?' These thoughts generate desperation and panic, driving plotting and scheming to ensure someone will be available.
mechanism: Fear of losing caretakers maintains dependence by motivating clingy behaviors and tolerance of abuse or mistreatment to keep the person present. This drains mental energy and prevents autonomous functioning.

intervention.primary: Challenge catastrophic predictions about being alone
intervention.verbal: What specifically would happen if this person left? What resources do you actually have?
intervention.behavioral: Practice brief periods alone; build alternative support network
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your fear of being alone makes you willing to accept treatment you shouldn't have to accept.

exact_quotes: Other typical thoughts reflect your fear of abandonment—your fear that you will lose the people upon whom you are most dependent: 'What would I do without this person?,' 'How will I get by on my own?'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Distrust of Own Judgment
description: A core feature of dependence is complete lack of trust in one's own judgment. Dependent individuals have little sense of their ability to make good decisions, leading to chronic indecisiveness. They rush from person to person seeking advice, change their minds repeatedly, and seek constant reassurance after decisions.
mechanism: Distrust of judgment leads to seeking external validation, which prevents developing confidence in one's own decision-making and maintains dependence on others for guidance.
behavioral_signature: Relying on one trusted person's judgment

intervention.verbal: What is your gut feeling about this? If you couldn't ask anyone, what would you decide?
intervention.behavioral: Make small decisions alone and track outcomes; resist seeking reassurance
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The only way to trust your judgment is to practice using it and see that your decisions work out.

exact_quotes: Difficulty trusting your judgment is a core feature of dependence. You are indecisive.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Enmeshment and Fusion in Dependence
description: Enmeshment and fusion refer to the sense that you and another person have become like one, making it hard to tell where your identity starts and the other person's begins. This occurs when overprotective parenting combines with subjugation, resulting in the child losing their sense of self and becoming totally dependent on others to fill an internal emptiness.
mechanism: When a parent forces their own image onto a child and subjugates the child's natural inclinations, the child gradually loses their sense of self. This creates an emptiness inside that can only be filled by relying on someone else who has a sense of self, perpetuating total dependence.
behavioral_signature: Trouble separating from family for even short periods
Cannot grow up and leave home
Relying on others to define self

intervention.behavioral: Gradually practicing independent functioning and decision-making
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you were growing up, you weren't allowed to develop your own sense of who you are. You learned to rely on others to fill that space. Now you need to discover and strengthen your own identity.

exact_quotes: Enmeshment and fusion refer to the sense that you and another person have become like one. It is hard to tell where your identity starts and the other person's begins.
If you have no sense of self, you are totally dependent. There is an emptiness inside, and the only way to fill it is to rely on someone else, someone who has a sense of self.
He once described himself as an empty hole inside.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Origins of Dependence in Underprotectiveness
description: Underprotectiveness is the second origin of the Dependence lifetrap, creating the Counterdependent person. Parents who are weak, ineffectual, beset with their own problems, or simply absent and neglectful fail to provide adequate guidance or protection. The child senses the lack of protection, feels unsafe, and never stops longing for a dependent role while being forced into premature self-sufficiency.
mechanism: Children forced to make decisions beyond their years develop competence and independence on the surface, but underneath feel insecure and wish for normal dependence. They become parentified children who question their decisions, feel chronic anxiety about competence, and yearn for someone to relieve the weight of responsibility.
behavioral_signature: Making decisions alone beyond one's years
Acting like an adult while feeling like a child inside

intervention.behavioral: Learning to ask for help and accept guidance
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You had to grow up too fast and take care of yourself before you were ready. The part of you that wanted normal dependence as a child is still there, still longing to be cared for.

exact_quotes: These patients have a combination of the Dependence and Emotional Deprivation lifetraps. From very early, the child senses the lack of protection and feels unsafe. The child never stops longing for that dependent role.
I always had the feeling that I was swimming out of my depth. I always had the feeling that my decisions weren't good ones, that I wished there was someone I could ask.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Origins of Dependence in Underprotectiveness - Childhood Experiences
description: Four specific childhood experiences that lead to the Counterdependent form of Dependence lifetrap through underprotective parenting.
mechanism: Each experience creates a sense of being overwhelmed and swimming out of one's depth, leading to surface competence masking underlying insecurity.

intervention.verbal: 1. You do not get enough practical guidance or direction from your parents. 2. You have to make decisions alone beyond your years. 3. You have to be like an adult in your family, even when underneath you still feel like a child. 4. You are expected to do things and know things that are over your head.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These experiences forced you to become independent before you were ready, leaving you feeling constantly anxious underneath.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Christine - Counterdependent Origin from Underprotective Parents
description: Christine's mother was addicted to alcohol and prescription tranquilizers and could not take care of herself. Her father was never around, preferring his buddies and clubs. Christine became a parentified child, forced to be self-sufficient while underneath wishing for normal dependence.
mechanism: With no one to provide guidance and protection, Christine had to become competent and independent to survive, making her child self into her mother's parent. But underneath she did not feel secure.
what_activates_it: Need to care for impaired parent
exact_quotes: My mother was addicted to alcohol and prescription tranquilizers. She could not take care of herself, let alone take care of me. And my father was never around. He had his buddies, his clubs.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence in Intimate Relationships
description: People with Dependence lifetrap can become dependent on parents, siblings, friends, lovers, spouses, mentors, bosses, therapists, or even their own children. The dependent parent may make their child into their parent, reversing the natural roles.
mechanism: The person seeks out or creates dependent relationships with anyone available, including inappropriate targets like children, to maintain their dependent state.
behavioral_signature: Feeling safer with child present than alone

intervention.behavioral: Practicing tasks alone without seeking a safe person
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You've learned to look for someone to depend on in every situation. Learning to function alone will free you from this constant search.

exact_quotes: I know it sounds strange, but my daughter is one of my safe people. I can do a lot of things with Jill that I can't do alone, like go to the supermarket.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Danger Signals in Dating for Dependence Lifetrap
description: Warning signs that a romantic partner is triggering your Dependence lifetrap, indicating you are reenacting your childhood situation by choosing partners who foster your dependence.
mechanism: These partners perpetuate the dependent state of childhood by taking over all responsibilities and treating the person like a child, preventing development of autonomy.
behavioral_signature: Losing sense of self around partner

intervention.verbal: 1. Your partner is like a father/mother figure, who seems strong and protective. 2. He/She seems to enjoy taking care of you and treats you like a child. 3. You trust his/her judgment much more than your own. He/She makes most of the decisions. 4. You find that you lose your sense of self around him/her—and that your life goes on hold when he/she is not around. 5. He/She pays for almost everything, and takes care of most financial records. 6. He/She criticizes your opinions, taste, and competence in everyday tasks. 7. When you have a new task to undertake, you almost always ask his/her advice, even if he/she has no special expertise in that realm. 8. He/She does almost everything for you—you have almost no responsibility. 9. He/She almost never seems frightened, insecure, or vulnerable about him/herself.
intervention.behavioral: Review relationships against these warning signs
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: If this list describes your relationship, then you are still living in the dependent state of your childhood. It costs you your will, your freedom, and your pride. It costs you your very self.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Surrendering to Dependence Lifetrap
description: The surrender response to Dependence involves finding partners who would support autonomy and twisting them until they fit the shape of the Dependence lifetrap. The person makes themselves dependent on almost all relationships including friends and strangers.
mechanism: Even when a healthy partner is available, the person undermines their own autonomy by constantly seeking someone to take care of them, asking for advice and reassurance, and avoiding responsibility.

intervention.behavioral: Completing tasks without seeking safe person or reassurance
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You tend to make yourself dependent in almost all situations, even when help isn't needed. Learning to function without seeking safety will build your confidence.

exact_quotes: Even if you find a partner who would like to support your autonomy, there are pitfalls to avoid. You can twist a healthy partner until he or she fits the shape of your Dependence lifetrap.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Margaret - Safe Person Seeking at Grocery Store
description: Margaret demonstrates surrender to Dependence by immediately looking for someone to turn to for help when entering a grocery store, identifying a nice-looking woman who might take care of her if things got bad, even though she has never actually needed such help.
mechanism: The automatic search for a safe person maintains dependence by never allowing the person to experience that they can function alone.
behavioral_signature: Never actually using the help but needing to know it's available

intervention.verbal: Is that usually the first thing you do, look for someone to take care of you?
intervention.behavioral: Practice tasks without identifying safe person first

exact_quotes: Well, the first thing I did was look for someone I could turn to if I needed help. There was this woman shopping a little ahead of me, and I thought she looked nice, and would maybe take care of me if things got really bad.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Behavioral Patterns Maintaining Dependence Lifetrap
description: Eleven specific behaviors that maintain the Dependence lifetrap by preventing development of autonomy and competence.
mechanism: Each behavior perpetuates the belief that one cannot function independently, preventing corrective experiences that would build confidence.

intervention.verbal: 1. You turn to wiser or stronger people all the time for advice and guidance. 2. You minimize your successes and magnify your shortcomings. 3. You avoid new challenges on your own. 4. You do not make your own decisions. 5. You do not take care of your own financial records or decisions. 6. You live through your parents/partner. 7. You are much more dependent on your parents than most people your age. 8. You avoid being alone or traveling alone. 9. You have fears and phobias that you do not confront. 10. You are quite ignorant when it comes to many areas of practical functioning and daily survival skills. 11. You have not lived on your own for any significant period of time.
intervention.behavioral: Systematically tackle each behavior starting with easiest
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These behaviors keep you trapped in dependence. By changing them one by one, you'll discover you're more capable than you believe.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Signs of Counterdependence
description: Three behavioral signs that indicate counterdependence - the overcompensation response to the Dependence lifetrap where the person appears extremely independent but is actually swimming out of their depth.
mechanism: By never allowing normal dependency and constantly taking on challenges, the person ignores the part of them that wants healthy dependence and rest, maintaining chronic anxiety and pressure.

intervention.verbal: 1. You never seem to be able to turn to anyone for guidance or advice. You have to do everything on your own. 2. You are always taking on new challenges and confronting your fears, but you feel under constant pressure while doing it. 3. Your partner is very dependent on you, and you end up doing everything and making all the decisions.
intervention.behavioral: Practice asking for help; regulate amount taken on
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You ignore the part of you that wants a little healthy dependence, that just wants to stop coping for a while and rest.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Christine's Imagery of Yearning for Normal Dependence
description: An imagery exercise revealing the counterdependent person's hidden yearning for normal dependency, expressed through the image of wanting to lay one's head in mother's lap.
mechanism: The imagery bypasses the defensive counterdependent stance to access the underlying need for healthy dependence that was denied in childhood.
behavioral_signature: Image of child wanting physical comfort from mother
Desire to rest and be cared for

intervention.verbal: What image do you see? / I see myself as a child. My mother's sitting on the couch, and all I want to do is go across the room, sit down, and lay my head in my mother's lap.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This image shows the part of you that yearned for normal childhood dependence but never got it.

exact_quotes: I see myself as a child. My mother's sitting on the couch, and all I want to do is go across the room, sit down, and lay my head in my mother's lap.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Christine's Treatment Outcome for Dependence Lifetrap
description: Christine demonstrates successful treatment outcome for the Dependence lifetrap, achieving balance between caring for others and receiving care. She reports feeling calmer and no longer struggling out of her depth, having learned to recognize when she is too involved in helping others and stepping back to focus on her own life.
mechanism: Treatment success involves recognizing overinvolvement patterns as signals, stepping back, and establishing reciprocal caregiving relationships rather than one-sided dependency or caretaking.

intervention.verbal: I feel like it was a matter of bringing some balance into my life, so that I take care of other people and they take care of me. I never had that before. I feel calmer now, like I'm not always struggling out of my depth.
intervention.behavioral: Monitor for signs of overinvolvement and step back to focus on own life
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Recovery involves creating reciprocal relationships where you both give and receive care, rather than being in a dependent or caretaking role.

exact_quotes: I feel like it was a matter of bringing some balance into my life, so that I take care of other people and they take care of me. I never had that before. I feel calmer now, like I'm not always struggling out of my depth.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Journey Out of the Dependence Lifetrap Summary
description: The journey out of the Dependence lifetrap is characterized as a movement from childhood to adulthood, trading fear and avoidance for a sense of mastery and the ability to function independently in the world. It involves giving up the struggle to get people to take care of you and learning self-reliance.
mechanism: Recovery requires relinquishing dependency behaviors, building self-efficacy through mastering life tasks, and developing genuine belief in one's own coping abilities.

intervention.verbal: Give up the exhausting struggle to get people to take care of you. Learn to take care of yourself. Learn to believe in your own ability to cope by mastering the tasks of life.
intervention.behavioral: Systematically master previously avoided tasks to build competence
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The goal is to move from feeling like a dependent child to feeling like a capable adult who can handle life's challenges.

exact_quotes: The journey out of the Dependence lifetrap is a movement from childhood to adulthood.
It is a trading of fear and avoidance for a sense of mastery—for the sense you can function independently in the world.
Give up the exhausting struggle to get people to take care of you. Learn to take care of yourself.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Robert: Panic Attacks and Health Vulnerability
description: Robert is a 31-year-old man suffering from panic attacks who has been going from doctor to doctor convinced something is physically wrong despite being told it is anxiety. He experiences sudden feelings of doom, dizziness, lightheadedness, difficulty breathing, pounding heart, and unreality. He fears having a nervous breakdown, going crazy, or dying from a brain tumor or heart problem.
mechanism: Panic attacks are maintained by catastrophic interpretation of bodily sensations, leading to escalating anxiety and reinforcement of beliefs about vulnerability to illness or losing control.

intervention.verbal: What does it feel like to you? What do you think is happening? What exactly were you thinking about when the attack began?
intervention.behavioral: Gradual exposure to avoided activities that trigger panic sensations
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your panic attacks are driven by catastrophic interpretations of normal body sensations, not by actual physical illness.

exact_quotes: Usually how it goes is that all of a sudden, out of the blue, this feeling comes over me. It's like a feeling of doom. I get dizzy and light-headed and feel like I can't breathe.
It feels like I'm having a nervous breakdown. Like I'm going to go crazy on the spot.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Heather: Multiple Phobias Causing Marital Problems
description: Heather is a 42-year-old woman whose fears are causing marital problems. She won't fly, go in water, use elevators, go into the city at night due to danger fears, and obsessively saves money. Her husband Walt describes living with her as 'like living in prison.' Her fears have grown worse over years and restrict both their lives.
mechanism: Multiple phobias and safety behaviors progressively restrict life activities, creating relationship conflict and reinforcing the belief that avoidance is necessary for safety.

intervention.verbal: What kind of vacation would it be for me if I had to spend the whole time worrying about the plane trip back or the trips up in the elevator?
intervention.behavioral: Systematic exposure to avoided situations using hierarchy
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your avoidance of feared situations keeps you trapped and prevents you from learning that you can cope.

exact_quotes: She won't do anything. We can't go on vacation because she won't get on the plane, and she won't go in the water, and she won't get in the elevator.
Living with her is like living in prison. It's driving me crazy!

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability Lifetrap Questionnaire
description: A 10-item self-report questionnaire measuring the strength of the Vulnerability lifetrap. Items assess fear of catastrophe, worry about illness, finances, crime, panic attacks, and losing control. Scored on a 1-6 scale with interpretation ranges from Very Low (10-19) to Very High (50-60).
mechanism: Self-assessment helps patients identify and quantify their vulnerability-related fears and avoidance patterns across multiple domains.

intervention.verbal: 1. I cannot escape the feeling that something bad is about to happen. 2. I feel that catastrophe can strike at any moment. 3. I worry about becoming a street person or vagrant. 4. I worry a lot about being attacked by a criminal, mugger, thief, etc. 5. I worry about getting a serious illness, even though nothing has been diagnosed by a physician. 6. I am too anxious to travel alone on planes, trains, etc. 7. I have anxiety attacks. 8. I am very aware of physical sensations in my body, and I worry about what they mean. 9. I worry I will lose control of myself in public or go crazy. 10. I worry a lot about losing all my money or going broke.
intervention.behavioral: Complete questionnaire and calculate total score
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This questionnaire helps you understand how strongly the Vulnerability lifetrap affects your life.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability Lifetrap Questionnaire Scoring Key
description: Scoring interpretation for the Vulnerability questionnaire with five severity levels: 10-19 Very Low (lifetrap probably does not apply), 20-29 Fairly Low (may only apply occasionally), 30-39 Moderate (is an issue in your life), 40-49 High (definitely important lifetrap), 50-60 Very High (definitely one of your core lifetraps). Note that any 5's or 6's indicate the lifetrap may still apply even with low total score.
mechanism: Provides standardized interpretation of vulnerability severity to guide treatment planning.

intervention.verbal: 10-19 Very low. This lifetrap probably does not apply to you. 20-29 Fairly low. This lifetrap may only apply occasionally. 30-39 Moderate. This lifetrap is an issue in your life. 40-49 High. This is definitely an important lifetrap for you. 50-60 Very high. This is definitely one of your core lifetraps.
intervention.behavioral: Review scores and identify specific items rated highest
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your score helps determine how much focus this lifetrap needs in your treatment.

exact_quotes: If you have any 5's or 6's on this questionnaire, this lifetrap may still apply to you, even if your score is in the low range.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability Lifetrap Definition
description: The Vulnerability lifetrap is characterized by the feeling that catastrophe is about to strike and that you lack the resources to deal with it. It is two-pronged: you both exaggerate the risk of danger and minimize your own capacity to cope. The primary feeling is anxiety.
mechanism: The lifetrap operates through dual cognitive distortions: overestimating threat probability while underestimating coping capacity, creating chronic anxiety and avoidance behaviors.

intervention.verbal: Catastrophe is about to strike, and you lack the resources to deal with it. This lifetrap is two-pronged: You both exaggerate the risk of danger and minimize your own capacity to cope.
intervention.behavioral: Systematic exposure to feared situations while challenging catastrophic interpretations
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your vulnerability feelings come from overestimating danger and underestimating your ability to cope. Treatment helps correct both distortions.

exact_quotes: The primary feeling associated with the Vulnerability lifetrap is anxiety.
Catastrophe is about to strike, and you lack the resources to deal with it.
This lifetrap is two-pronged: You both exaggerate the risk of danger and minimize your own capacity to cope.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability Type: Health and Illness
description: The Health and Illness type of Vulnerability involves hypochondriacal preoccupation with health despite physicians finding nothing wrong. Includes constant body scanning for signs of illness, panic attacks triggered by normal sensations, and catastrophic interpretation of physical symptoms as signs of AIDS, cancer, multiple sclerosis, or other dread diseases.
mechanism: Hypervigilance to bodily sensations leads to detection of normal variations, which are catastrophically interpreted as disease symptoms, triggering panic and reinforcing scanning behaviors.

intervention.verbal: What exactly were you thinking about when the attack began? These are the famous words of a panicker: 'What if.'
intervention.behavioral: Gradual reintroduction of avoided activities that produce physical sensations
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your panic is driven by catastrophic thoughts about normal body sensations, not by actual illness.

exact_quotes: You constantly scan your body for signs that something is wrong. You are sensitized to your body.
Any strange sensation, no matter how naturally caused, can trigger panic.
These are the famous words of a panicker: 'What if.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Health Vulnerability Hypervigilance Pattern
description: People with health-type vulnerability are hypervigilant to anything in their environment relevant to illness. They may read everything about illness or avoid any mention entirely; may run to doctors continuously or avoid doctors entirely out of fear of diagnosis. Either extreme reflects constant preoccupation with thoughts of illness.
mechanism: Hypervigilance maintains the lifetrap by ensuring that illness-related information is constantly processed, reinforcing catastrophic beliefs regardless of whether information is sought or avoided.
behavioral_signature: excessive reading about illness OR complete avoidance
frequent doctor visits OR doctor avoidance

intervention.verbal: You are hypervigilant to anything in your environment that is relevant to the possibility of illness.
intervention.behavioral: Establish moderate, balanced approach to health information
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Both obsessively seeking and completely avoiding health information keeps you trapped in worry.

exact_quotes: You are hypervigilant to anything in your environment that is relevant to the possibility of illness.
You may read everything you can get on the subject, or you may avoid any mention of illness entirely.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability Escape Pattern and Lifestyle Impact
description: Escape from health vulnerability involves avoiding activities that give rise to panic or physical sensations. This can include avoiding exercise, sex, and enjoyable activities. The avoidance puts a serious dent in lifestyle quality.
mechanism: Avoidance prevents extinction of fear response and reinforces belief that avoided activities are dangerous, progressively restricting life activities.

intervention.verbal: By trying to Escape his Vulnerability lifetrap, Robert was putting a serious dent in his lifestyle.
intervention.behavioral: Systematically reintroduce avoided activities using hierarchy
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Avoiding activities that cause physical sensations keeps you trapped and shrinks your life.

exact_quotes: Robert was avoiding all forms of exercise, including sex, when he first came to therapy.
By trying to Escape his Vulnerability lifetrap, Robert was putting a serious dent in his lifestyle.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Enlist Support from Loved Ones
description: Meet with spouse, lover, family, and friends to inform them about your efforts to overcome vulnerability. Ask them to reduce overprotection and reassurance gradually, and encourage them to express their own vulnerability to you.
mechanism: Breaking the reinforcement cycle where loved ones inadvertently maintain the lifetrap through excessive protection and reassurance. Normalizing the relationship dynamic reduces dependency on safety behaviors.
what_activates_it: Recognition of how others reinforce avoidance
behavioral_signature: Having conversations with loved ones about reducing protection

intervention.verbal: Tell them you are trying to overcome your feelings of vulnerability. Ask them to start protecting you less and reassuring you less. You can ask them to gradually phase these things out. Encourage people to express more of their own vulnerability to you.
intervention.behavioral: Schedule meetings with key people in your life to discuss changing the protective dynamic
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You have chosen many of the people around you because they reinforce your lifetrap. You have to get them to stop if you want to overcome your Vulnerability lifetrap.

exact_quotes: You have chosen many of the people around you because they reinforce your lifetrap. You have to get them to stop if you want to overcome your Vulnerability lifetrap.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Walt - Relief from Overprotective Role
description: Walt, Heather's partner, expresses relief at being able to give up the overprotective role. He wants to discuss his own problems, especially work issues, without Heather falling apart.
mechanism: Demonstrates how partners of vulnerable individuals are often burdened by the one-sided protective dynamic and welcome the opportunity to share their own struggles.
what_activates_it: Partner's decision to address vulnerability lifetrap

intervention.verbal: It's nice not to have to be the strong one all the time. It was wearing on me. I mean, I have problems too. I'd like to discuss them with Heather without her falling apart. I have some problems at work especially that I really would like to talk out.
intervention.behavioral: Create space for mutual sharing of concerns in relationship
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Most partners will jump at the opportunity to give up such an overprotective role. Usually partners are weary of being so solicitous.

exact_quotes: It's nice not to have to be the strong one all the time. It was wearing on me. I mean, I have problems too. I'd like to discuss them with Heather without her falling apart.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Examine Probability of Feared Events
description: Challenge the exaggerated probability estimates of feared events by gathering objective information, soliciting others' opinions, and comparing felt probability to realistic odds.
mechanism: Corrects cognitive distortion of probability overestimation by replacing intuitive (lifetrap-driven) judgments with evidence-based assessments, thereby reducing anxiety.
behavioral_signature: Writing down felt vs realistic probability estimates

intervention.verbal: What do you think the odds are of your airplane crashing? And when you are on an airplane, what do you think the odds are then? Do you know that the odds really are closer to one in a million?
intervention.behavioral: For each feared situation, write down the odds as it feels to you, then write down realistic odds based on others' opinions and research
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Right now you use intuition to judge the odds of feared events occurring. The problem is that your intuition is simply wrong because it is under the sway of your lifetrap. Greater accuracy will decrease your anxiety.

exact_quotes: Right now you use intuition to judge the odds of feared events occurring. You feel that the likelihood of danger is high. The problem is that your intuition is simply wrong because it is under the sway of your lifetrap.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Socratic Questions for Probability Assessment
description: Series of questions designed to expose the discrepancy between felt probability of catastrophe and actual statistical probability, demonstrating how the lifetrap distorts risk perception.
mechanism: Creates cognitive dissonance between emotional reasoning and factual evidence, weakening the authority of fear-based intuition.
what_activates_it: Patient expressing fear of specific catastrophe

intervention.verbal: What do you think the odds are of your airplane crashing? And when you are on an airplane, what do you think the odds are then? Do you know that the odds really are closer to one in a million?
intervention.behavioral: Have patient research actual statistics for feared events
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your intuition about danger is distorted by your lifetrap. Let's look at the actual evidence.

exact_quotes: What do you think the odds are of your airplane crashing?
Do you know that the odds really are closer to one in a million?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Heather - Distorted Probability of Airplane Crash
description: Heather estimates airplane crash probability at one in a thousand normally but six in ten when actually on a plane, demonstrating how anxiety inflates perceived risk during exposure to feared situations.
mechanism: Illustrates state-dependent probability distortion where proximity to feared situation dramatically increases perceived danger despite unchanged objective risk.
behavioral_signature: Giving much higher probability estimates when in feared situation
Unable to access rational probability assessment during anxiety

intervention.verbal: What do you think the odds are of your airplane crashing? I don't know. I guess about one in a thousand. And when you are on an airplane, what do you think the odds are then? When I'm on the plane, the odds seem higher. Maybe six out of ten.
intervention.behavioral: Track probability estimates before vs during feared situations
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Notice how your estimates change dramatically based on whether you're in the situation, even though the actual odds haven't changed.

exact_quotes: When I'm on the plane, the odds seem higher. Maybe six out of ten.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Robert - Panic Attack Probability Distortion
description: Robert estimates 99% probability of losing his mind during panic attacks, while others estimate 25%. Actual probability is essentially zero since no documented case exists of someone going crazy during a panic attack.
mechanism: Demonstrates extreme probability overestimation driven by catastrophic misinterpretation of panic symptoms, maintained despite complete absence of evidence for feared outcome.
behavioral_signature: Unable to access factual information during panic

intervention.verbal: The odds of someone losing their mind while having a panic attack are practically zero. This is because, as far as we know, it has never happened. Panic Disorder is a vastly studied subject, but there is not one reported case of a person going crazy during a panic attack.
intervention.behavioral: Research documented cases of panic attack outcomes
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The odds that you will die, go crazy, or lose control during a panic attack are no higher than they are at any other time. The panic attack does not make it any more likely.

exact_quotes: The odds of someone losing their mind while having a panic attack are practically zero. This is because, as far as we know, it has never happened.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Catastrophizing in Vulnerability
description: The tendency to jump to the worst possible conclusion and consider it the most likely outcome. This cognitive distortion is central to the Vulnerability lifetrap.
mechanism: Exaggerating the odds of catastrophic events triggers disproportionate anxiety and avoidance, reinforcing the belief that the world is dangerous and one is unable to cope.

intervention.verbal: Exaggerating the odds is part of your tendency to catastrophize. You jump to the worst possible conclusion and consider it to be the most likely one. In fact, the likelihood of most catastrophic events happening to you is extremely low.
intervention.behavioral: Track predictions vs outcomes to gather disconfirming evidence
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your lifetrap makes you jump to the worst conclusion. Let's examine whether the catastrophe you fear is really as likely as it feels.

exact_quotes: Exaggerating the odds is part of your tendency to catastrophize. You jump to the worst possible conclusion and consider it to be the most likely one. In fact, the likelihood of most catastrophic events happening to you is extremely low.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Write Flashcards for Each Fear
description: Create a flashcard for each specific fear that reminds you how the lifetrap leads to catastrophizing, encourages facing what you're escaping, and helps you give up overprotective behaviors.
mechanism: Provides immediate cognitive intervention during triggered moments, countering automatic catastrophic thoughts with pre-prepared rational responses.

intervention.verbal: Write a flashcard for each one of your fears. Remind yourself of how the lifetrap leads you to catastrophize. Encourage yourself to face what you are escaping, and give up your overprotectiveness.
intervention.behavioral: Create flashcards for top 5 fears and carry them for immediate access
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Use the flashcards whenever your lifetrap is triggered. It can counter your tendency to catastrophize. Keep reassessing the odds. Enter the situation. Eventually, your anxiety will pass and you will feel comfortable.

exact_quotes: Use the flashcards whenever your lifetrap is triggered. It can counter your tendency to catastrophize.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Heather's Elevator Fear Flashcard
description: Complete example of a Vulnerability flashcard addressing fear of riding elevators, including acknowledgment of fear, identification of lifetrap activation, reality testing, commitment to behavioral exposure, and challenging safety behaviors.
mechanism: Provides structured cognitive intervention that validates fear while reattributing it to lifetrap, encourages exposure despite anxiety, and explicitly challenges compulsive checking as unnecessary.

intervention.verbal: I know that right now I am afraid to ride the elevator. I am afraid of some catastrophe, like the building catching fire, and the elevator getting stuck. I feel this is very likely to happen. But what really is happening is that my Vulnerability lifetrap is getting triggered. I'm probably exaggerating the degree of risk. Therefore, I will force myself to go into this situation anyway, in spite of my fears, to see that it's not really dangerous. I know I want to count the floors five times before I get on the elevator. I feel that the checking will make me feel safer. But I don't need to do the checking. I am safe enough without it. The checking is just a superstition. Besides, it's a burden and I want to give it up.
intervention.behavioral: Read flashcard before entering feared situation, proceed with exposure without safety behaviors
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This flashcard helps you catch your lifetrap in action and reminds you to enter the situation despite your fears, while also challenging your safety rituals.

exact_quotes: I know that right now I am afraid to ride the elevator. I am afraid of some catastrophe, like the building catching fire, and the elevator getting stuck. I feel this is very likely to happen.
The checking is just a superstition. Besides, it's a burden and I want to give it up.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Talk to Inner Child - Be a Strong Parent
description: Use imagery to access childhood vulnerability feelings, then bring adult self into the image to comfort and protect the frightened inner child, creating internalized safety and reassurance.
mechanism: Addresses the developmental origin of vulnerability feelings by providing the protective parenting that was missing, creating new internal representations of safety that can be accessed when triggered.
what_activates_it: Willingness to engage in imagery work

intervention.verbal: Get an image of a time as a child that you felt vulnerable. Don't force it. Just tell me the first one that comes into your mind.
intervention.behavioral: Practice imagery reparenting daily, especially before facing feared situations
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The feelings connected to your lifetrap are child feelings. They are the feelings of your vulnerable inner child. You need to develop an inner parent to help this inner child.

exact_quotes: The feelings connected to your lifetrap are child feelings. They are the feelings of your vulnerable inner child. You need to develop an inner parent to help this inner child.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Heather's Concentration Camp Memory Reparenting
description: Heather accesses a childhood memory of learning about her mother's concentration camp experience at age six, feels overwhelming fear, then brings adult self into image to provide safety and protection to frightened child.
mechanism: Identifies the origin moment of generalized vulnerability, allows emotional processing of childhood fear, and creates new ending where adult self provides protection the child needed.
what_activates_it: Instruction to access childhood vulnerability memory

intervention.verbal: Bring myself into the image as an adult. Sit down at the table with the child Heather. Say: 'You don't have to be afraid. You're safe. You're in your own house and I am here and you're safe. No one will hurt you. There are no Nazis here. If you want to go out and play, I will come with you. I'll protect you. I'll help you face what you fear.'
intervention.behavioral: Practice this specific imagery when vulnerability is triggered
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: We want you to bring in this adult whenever your lifetrap is triggered. Reassure yourself that there is nothing to fear. Help your inner child feel safe enough to confront the situation.

exact_quotes: You don't have to be afraid. You're safe. You're in your own house and I am here and you're safe. No one will hurt you. There are no Nazis here. If you want to go out and play, I will come with you. I'll protect you. I'll help you face what you fear.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Protective Shell Creates Illusion of Well-Being
description: People with Defectiveness lifetrap develop a protective shell that makes them appear to be doing well on the surface, while inside they still feel defective and unloved. This shell prevents the true self from healing because what stays hidden cannot be addressed.
mechanism: The shell provides temporary relief from shame but prevents genuine connection and healing. By settling for illusion rather than authenticity, the core wound remains untouched and the lifetrap persists.
behavioral_signature: Appearing confident while feeling worthless inside

intervention.primary: Remove protective shell to allow true self to emerge
intervention.verbal: You have to stop settling for illusion and start going for the real thing.
intervention.behavioral: Practice being genuine in close relationships
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: A true self that stays hidden cannot heal. Your protective shell keeps you from addressing the core issue.

exact_quotes: The problem with the shell is that you never really address the core issue. A true self that stays hidden cannot heal. You have to stop settling for illusion and start going for the real thing.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness Not Based on Actual Defects
description: The Defectiveness lifetrap is not usually based on a real defect. Even people with serious physical or mental handicaps do not necessarily develop this lifetrap. The crucial factor is how one was made to feel by parents and family members.
mechanism: Parental treatment determines self-worth, not actual qualities. Being loved, valued, and respected by family regardless of actual strengths and weaknesses prevents feelings of worthlessness and shame.
behavioral_signature: Feeling defective despite objective evidence to contrary

intervention.verbal: The crucial factor is not the presence of a defect, but rather how you are made to feel about yourself by your parents and the other members of your family.
intervention.behavioral: Review evidence of actual defects versus perceived defects
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your feelings of defectiveness reflect how you were treated as a child, not anything about who you really are.

exact_quotes: It is very important to realize that the Defectiveness lifetrap is not usually based on a real defect.
The crucial factor is not the presence of a defect, but rather how you are made to feel about yourself by your parents and the other members of your family.
If you are loved, valued, and respected by your family members—regardless of your actual strengths and weaknesses—you will almost certainly not feel worthless, ashamed, or defective.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Danger Signals While Dating - Defectiveness
description: Ten warning signs that Defectiveness lifetrap is affecting dating patterns, including avoidance of dating, attraction to critical or abusive partners, pursuing unattainable partners, and settling for partners one doesn't love.
mechanism: These patterns either avoid the risk of exposure (escape) or recreate familiar childhood dynamics of criticism and rejection (surrender), preventing healthy relationship development.
behavioral_signature: Series of short intense affairs or multiple simultaneous affairs
Attraction to critical partners who put you down
Attraction to physically or emotionally abusive partners
Most attracted to partners not interested in you
Only pursuing most attractive/desirable unattainable partners
Comfortable only with partners who don't want to know you deeply
Only dating people felt to be below you whom you don't love
Attraction to unavailable partners (married, long-distance, etc.)
Being critical, abusive, or neglectful toward partners

intervention.verbal: Be careful when there is very strong chemistry. You probably have the most powerful attraction to partners who criticize and reject you.
intervention.behavioral: Stop dating partners who do not treat you well rather than try to win them over
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Critical partners will feel familiar because they echo your childhood environment, but this reinforces your feelings of defectiveness.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Intimacy Avoidance in Defectiveness
description: People cope with Defectiveness by avoiding long-term intimate relationships through having no relationships, only short relationships, or multiple relationships. This ensures no one gets close enough to see inner flaws.
mechanism: By avoiding long-term commitment, the person prevents exposure of perceived defects. Partners who also avoid intimacy may be chosen, or relationships may be structured to minimize close contact.
behavioral_signature: Avoiding dating people who really interest you

intervention.verbal: Like Eliot, you might avoid dating people who really interest you. You only date people you know you could never love.
intervention.behavioral: Practice being genuine in one close relationship
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: By avoiding intimacy, you never give yourself the chance to discover that someone can know the real you and still love you.

exact_quotes: By avoiding long-term commitment, you make certain that no one gets close enough to see your inner flaws.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Attraction to Critical and Rejecting Partners
description: People with Defectiveness lifetrap experience the most powerful attraction to partners who criticize and reject them, as these partners reinforce feelings of defectiveness and feel familiar by echoing the childhood environment.
mechanism: Strong chemistry occurs with partners who trigger the lifetrap because criticism and rejection match the person's self-image. This creates masochistic relationships where mistreatment is tolerated as deserved.
what_activates_it: Meeting critical or rejecting potential partners
behavioral_signature: Staying with partners who are nasty and insulting

intervention.primary: Pattern recognition and partner selection change
intervention.verbal: We strongly recommend that you stop dating partners who do not treat you well rather than try to win them over and gain their love.
intervention.behavioral: End relationships with critical/rejecting partners; date people who treat you well
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The chemistry you feel toward critical partners is a danger signal, not a sign of true love. It reflects your childhood wounds, not healthy attraction.

exact_quotes: If you have the Defectiveness lifetrap, be careful when there is very strong chemistry. You probably have the most powerful attraction to partners who criticize and reject you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness Lifetraps in Relationships
description: Twelve signs that Defectiveness lifetrap is operating within committed relationships, including becoming critical once accepted, hiding true self, jealousy, unfavorable comparisons, need for reassurance, and hypersensitivity to criticism.
mechanism: These behaviors either protect from exposure of perceived defects, seek constant validation of worth, or counterattack to manage shame by devaluing others.
behavioral_signature: Becoming critical once feeling accepted, losing romantic feelings
Acting demeaning or critical toward partner
Hiding true self, never feeling partner knows you
Constantly needing reassurance of partner's value of you
Allowing partner to criticize, put down, or mistreat you
Difficulty accepting valid criticism, becoming defensive or hostile
Becoming despondent over setbacks or rejections

intervention.verbal: If you do form a relationship with a partner who loves you and whom you could love, there are many ways you can reinforce your Defectiveness lifetrap within the relationship.
intervention.behavioral: Monitor and reduce critical behaviors; practice accepting love
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These behaviors keep your lifetrap alive even in good relationships. Recognizing them is the first step to changing them.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Criticalness as Counterattack in Defectiveness
description: Narcissistic presentation of Defectiveness involves becoming critical of partners to feel more comfortable with someone seen as one-down. This protects from worry about being found out, judged, or rejected.
mechanism: By devaluing partners, the person keeps from caring too much about how partners feel toward them. The more love felt for someone, the more valuable they become, triggering reflexive criticism to reduce their value and thus reduce threat of rejection.
behavioral_signature: Having exact specifications for perfect partner that no one meets
Criticizing most the person loved most
Losing interest after winning someone over

intervention.verbal: You believe that a truly desirable partner will see your flaws and ultimately reject you.
intervention.behavioral: Stop criticizing partner; notice when urge to criticize arises and what triggered it
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your criticalness is a way of protecting yourself from caring too much and risking rejection. The vehemence of criticism toward your partner may actually be a sign of your love.

exact_quotes: In fact, the vehemence with which he criticizes her is one of the signs of his love.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Groucho Marx Effect - Devaluing Accepting Partners
description: The belief that anyone who could love you must be low in value, based on the premise that in loving you they do something wrong. This is captured in Groucho Marx's line about never wanting to belong to a club that would have him as a member.
mechanism: Because the person believes they are defective, anyone who loves them must have poor judgment or low standards, thus lowering the partner's value and eliminating the relationship's ability to heal the lifetrap.
behavioral_signature: Excitement during pursuit but boredom after winning
Devaluing partner once relationship is secure

intervention.verbal: You may feel that anyone who could love you must be low in value anyway. This is the old Groucho Marx line, 'I never would want to belong to a club that would have me as a member.'
intervention.behavioral: When noticing devaluation of partner, remind yourself this is the lifetrap talking
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your partner's love for you doesn't mean they have poor judgment—it means they see your real worth that you've been taught to discount.

exact_quotes: You may feel that anyone who could love you must be low in value anyway. This is the old Groucho Marx line, 'I never would want to belong to a club that would have me as a member.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: False Self in Romantic Relationships
description: In romantic relationships, the most intimate context, the false self operates at full capacity. The person feels like they're always putting on an act, believing only the false self is worthy of love.
mechanism: By hiding the true self, the person never believes their partner loves the real them. This reinforces the sense that the real self is shameful and unlovable. The greatest terror is of being exposed.
behavioral_signature: Feeling like putting on an act
Waiting for partner to discover the 'mistake'

intervention.verbal: By not being completely open, you reinforce your sense that the real self is shameful and unlovable. Your greatest terror may be of being exposed.
intervention.behavioral: Share one genuine thought or feeling with partner daily
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The only way to discover that your true self can be loved is to let someone see it. Your false self prevents the healing you need.

exact_quotes: By hiding your true self, you never believe that your partner loves the real you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Terror of Exposure in Defectiveness
description: The greatest fear in Defectiveness lifetrap is being exposed—that eventually the partner will see through the act to the defective person beneath. This creates chronic anticipatory anxiety that the relationship will end when the truth is discovered.
mechanism: The person lives in constant fear that their 'true' defective nature will be seen, leading to rejection. This creates waiting for the inevitable rejection, sometimes leading to preemptive ending of relationships.
behavioral_signature: Constant anxiety about being found out

intervention.verbal: Your greatest terror may be of being exposed: that eventually your partner will see through your act to the defective person beneath.
intervention.behavioral: Gradually reveal true self and observe partner's actual response
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your fear of being exposed keeps you hiding. But when people do see the real you, they usually don't react the way you fear.

exact_quotes: Your greatest terror may be of being exposed: that eventually your partner will see through your act to the defective person beneath.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Alison - Waiting for Inevitable Rejection
description: Alison describes her chronic fear that her partner Matthew will eventually tell her it's all been a mistake and he doesn't really love her. She doesn't understand why he hasn't done it yet but feels it's only a matter of time. She considers ending the relationship because the anxiety is unbearable.
mechanism: Defectiveness creates expectation of rejection that becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy through hypervigilance and insecure behaviors that strain the relationship.

intervention.verbal: And you're just waiting.
intervention.behavioral: List evidence for and against prediction of rejection
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You've been waiting for rejection for years, but it hasn't come. What does that tell you about the accuracy of your prediction?

exact_quotes: I just know that I'll marry him and one day he'll turn around and tell me that it's all been a mistake, that he doesn't really love me. I don't really understand why he hasn't done it yet. But he's gonna do it sometime.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 9: Write a Letter to Your Critical Parent(s)
description: A therapeutic writing exercise where patients compose letters to family members who criticized them in childhood. The letters express anger and sadness about mistreatment, assert the patient's good qualities that were overlooked, describe what was needed but not received, and state current expectations for the relationship. The letters are typically not sent but serve as emotional release and self-validation.
mechanism: Writing the letter allows patients to externalize the internalized critical voice, vent suppressed emotions, create a narrative of what happened, and practice standing up for themselves. This breaks the pattern of defending or excusing abusive parents and validates the patient's childhood experience.
behavioral_signature: Patient writes detailed letter addressing specific criticisms received

intervention.verbal: Tell them what they did to you when you were a child. Tell them how it felt to be criticized and invalidated. Explain why you did not deserve to be treated this way. Stress the good qualities you had that they overlooked or downplayed. Tell them how you wished it could have been. Tell them about the support and approval you needed—what it would have meant to you and how it would have changed your life. And tell them what you want from them now. Do not make excuses for these family members, or rationalize their criticisms. You can do that later, if you choose. The road to healing is long; at the end, you may forgive them, when you do not feel defective anymore. But first you must stand up for yourself and vent the feelings you have buried.
intervention.behavioral: Write a letter to each family member who criticized you as a child. Include: what they did, how it felt, why you didn't deserve it, your good qualities they missed, what you needed, and what you expect now. Do not send unless you choose to later.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This letter helps you express feelings you've buried for years about how you were treated. You're not required to send it - the healing comes from writing your truth. By refusing to make excuses for your parents, you validate your own experience and stand up for the child you were.

exact_quotes: Do not make excuses for these family members, or rationalize their criticisms. You can do that later, if you choose. The road to healing is long; at the end, you may forgive them, when you do not feel defective anymore. But first you must stand up for yourself and vent the feelings you have buried.
Writing a letter like this can be a healing process. It is a statement of what happened to you. 'Your truth shall set you free.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Eliot's Letter to His Critical Father
description: Case example showing a complete therapeutic letter written by Eliot to his father who criticized him and unfavorably compared him to his brother Rick throughout childhood. The letter expresses anger about cruelty, sibling comparison, constant criticism, and the resulting shame, while asserting Eliot's positive qualities and setting boundaries for future contact.
mechanism: The letter demonstrates how patients can confront childhood mistreatment by naming specific behaviors (comparison to sibling, disgust, criticism), connecting past wounds to current problems (marriage issues, workaholism, infidelity, cocaine use), and establishing new relationship rules.
behavioral_signature: Connects childhood wounds to adult problems

intervention.verbal: Dear Dad, You were cruel to me when I was a child. You acted as though I had nothing worthwhile about me, nothing special or great. I just didn't matter to you. You didn't care that I had feelings, that I could feel pain or get hurt. You couldn't be bothered to give me love. What hurt most was how you always compared me to [my brother] Rick. You made me feel like nothing next to him... You criticized everything about me. I had no part of me I felt safe to show you. Anything I loved, I kept hidden from you. When I remember my childhood, the thing that stands out most is feeling ashamed. Despite how you treated me, I had some great qualities as a kid. I was smart. I could wheel and deal... I hate you for what you did to me... All of you let me down. Did you ever think what it would have meant for me to have you once seem happy about me, once seem proud or pleased with who I was?... If you want to maintain a relationship, you are going to have to change how you treat me. And if you can't, our relationship is over.
intervention.behavioral: Use as model for writing own letter to critical parent
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This example shows how to be honest about what happened without defending your parent. Notice how Eliot names specific behaviors, describes his feelings, asserts his worth, and sets clear boundaries.

exact_quotes: What hurt most was how you always compared me to [my brother] Rick. You made me feel like nothing next to him. When you were around Rick, you'd act all happy and excited. But when you were around me, you were mean and disgusted.
When I remember my childhood, the thing that stands out most is feeling ashamed.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 10: Write a Flashcard for Yourself
description: A cognitive coping technique where patients create a portable reminder card to read whenever the Defectiveness lifetrap is triggered. The flashcard acknowledges the current feeling, reframes it as the lifetrap being triggered, lists positive qualities with evidence, and invalidates parental criticisms. It serves as a weapon against the internalized critical voice.
mechanism: The flashcard externalizes the internal battle between the critical/unloving internalized parent and the vulnerable child who wants love and acceptance. It helps patients catch the automatic self-criticism habit and substitute a more balanced, self-loving response based on evidence.
behavioral_signature: Uses constructive rather than punitive self-talk

intervention.verbal: Make a flashcard that you can take out and read whenever your Defectiveness lifetrap is triggered—whenever, as Alison says, 'the voice of my father starts yammering in my head.' The flashcard should have on it all of the qualities in you that are good. It should invalidate your parents' criticisms—why the things they said were either wrong or less important than they made them out to be. Use objective evidence. Tell yourself to use a constructive tone with yourself, not a punitive tone.
intervention.behavioral: Create a portable flashcard with: 1) Acknowledgment of current feeling, 2) Reframe as lifetrap being triggered, 3) List of your good qualities with evidence, 4) Refutation of parental criticisms, 5) Self-compassionate encouragement. Carry it and read it whenever triggered.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Getting down on yourself is a hard habit to break. The flashcard helps you catch yourself every time and remember the truth about your worth. It's a weapon against the critical voice inside you.

exact_quotes: The flashcard is a weapon against the voice of the lifetrap. It keeps you aware that there are two sides. There is the critical or unloving parent you have internalized, who is always putting you down, ignoring you, and making you feel defective and ashamed. But there is also the vulnerable child inside, who wants love, acceptance, approval, and validation.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Alison's Defectiveness Flashcard
description: A complete example of a therapeutic flashcard for the Defectiveness lifetrap, demonstrating the structure: acknowledging current feelings of humiliation and inadequacy, reframing as lifetrap activation, stating positive qualities (sensitive, intelligent, loving, good), citing evidence (people who have found her worthy of love), and committing to change (allowing people close enough to know her).
mechanism: The flashcard provides a ready-made cognitive response to lifetrap activation that validates feelings while challenging distorted beliefs with evidence. It reminds the patient that feelings of inferiority are lifetrap-driven, not reality-based.
behavioral_signature: Patient recalls evidence of being loved

intervention.verbal: A DEFECTIVENESS FLASHCARD: Right now I feel humiliated and inadequate. I feel surrounded by people, especially women, who seem superior to me in every way—looks, brains, personality. I feel their presence diminishes me totally. But this is not true. What is really going on is that my lifetrap is being triggered. The truth is that I am worthy too. I am sensitive, intelligent, loving, and good. The truth is that many people have found me to be worthy of love: [lists their names]. Generally I have not given people a chance to get close enough to really know and appreciate me. But believing what I say on this card will help me move in this direction.
intervention.behavioral: Use this format to create your own flashcard with your specific positive qualities and evidence
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This card helps you remember your worth when the lifetrap makes you feel inferior. The feeling of being diminished isn't reality—it's your lifetrap being triggered.

exact_quotes: Right now I feel humiliated and inadequate. I feel surrounded by people, especially women, who seem superior to me in every way—looks, brains, personality. I feel their presence diminishes me totally. But this is not true. What is really going on is that my lifetrap is being triggered.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 11: Try to Be More Genuine in Close Relationships
description: A treatment step addressing different presentations of Defectiveness. For Counterattackers (like Eliot) who appear invulnerable, the task is to reveal vulnerability, share secrets, acknowledge flaws, and let people inside. For Surrenderers (like Alison) who are too vulnerable, the task is to protect themselves better. Disclosure should be paced according to partner's level of vulnerability to maintain relationship balance.
mechanism: Genuine self-disclosure in relationships corrects the belief that one's true self is unlovable. By gradually revealing imperfections and experiencing continued acceptance, patients gather evidence against their Defectiveness beliefs. Pacing prevents overwhelming partners while still achieving authenticity.
behavioral_signature: Reveals secrets gradually to trusted people

intervention.verbal: If you are more like Eliot, try to be more genuine in close relationships. Stop trying to give the impression that you are perfect. Be vulnerable. Share some of your secrets. Acknowledge some of your flaws. Let other people inside more. You will find that your secrets are not as humiliating as they feel to you. Everyone has flaws. You can pace how much you reveal of yourself. Reveal yourself little by little. Keep a sense of control. As you become more intimate and you sense that your partner seems genuinely to care for you, you can disclose more and more.
intervention.behavioral: Identify one secret or flaw you've hidden from your partner. Choose an appropriate moment to share it. Observe their response. Gradually increase disclosure as trust builds. Pace yourself according to partner's vulnerability level.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Many things we hide aren't as bad as we think. By sharing them and seeing that people still love us, we discover our true self is lovable. There's an expression: 'You are only as sick as your worst secret.'

exact_quotes: There is an expression, 'You are only as sick as your worst secret.' Many things that we hide from people are not as bad as we think. Once we share them with someone, we see that they are not so shameful. We see that the person still loves us, and we feel better about ourselves.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Eliot Shows Vulnerability by Expressing Jealousy
description: Case example demonstrating therapeutic progress when Eliot, a Counterattacker, expresses normal jealousy to his wife Maria instead of his usual defensive behavior of hitting on other women. His wife's response ('So you care? I didn't think you cared!') reveals how his defensive hiding had prevented intimacy.
mechanism: By showing a normal, appropriate emotional response (jealousy) instead of counterattacking, Eliot demonstrated caring to his wife. His typical defensive behaviors had hidden his love, creating distance in the relationship.
what_activates_it: Party situation with wife talking to male friend
behavioral_signature: Refrained from defensive flirting with others

intervention.primary: Behavioral change in moment of activation
intervention.verbal: ELIOT: I did something that surprised me. The other night Maria and I went to a party. One of her friends from college, this guy Richard, was there. They were talking and I got jealous. I always get jealous when they're together, because they seem so into talking to each other. They look so happy. Like Maria and I never are. My usual maneuver would have been to start hitting on some chick. But I didn't do that. Instead I told Maria. I told her I felt jealous. THERAPIST: What happened? ELIOT: Her reaction was, 'So you care? I didn't think you cared!'
intervention.behavioral: When triggered, express the underlying vulnerable feeling rather than counterattacking
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your defensive behaviors hide your caring from your partner. Showing normal emotions like jealousy can actually increase intimacy because it shows you care.

exact_quotes: Because he was so afraid of looking insecure, Eliot withheld from Maria the signs of his caring. He was afraid to show her he loved her. In this case, showing a normal and appropriate amount of jealousy was actually helpful.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 12: Accept Love from the People Close to You
