description: A treatment step addressing the discomfort Defectiveness patients feel when receiving positive treatment. Patients are uncomfortable being praised, cared for, or supported, and tend to push it away or discount it. Learning to accept love often brings up grief for what was missed in childhood.
mechanism: Receiving love triggers the Defectiveness schema because it conflicts with the core belief of being unworthy. By practicing acceptance (e.g., saying 'thank you' to compliments instead of denying them), patients gradually update their self-schema and process grief over past deprivation.
behavioral_signature: May experience grief when receiving love

intervention.verbal: One of the hardest things for you is to let people love you. You are very uncomfortable being treated well. It is so alien. You are much more comfortable being mistreated or ignored. It is hard for you to tolerate situations where people take care of you, praise you, and support you. You try to push it away or discount it. We want you to accept love as well. Stop pushing away the people who love you.
intervention.behavioral: When someone gives you a compliment, simply say 'thank you' without denying it. When someone offers comfort, allow yourself to receive it. Notice any grief that arises about what you missed.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Being treated well feels strange because it's so different from what you learned to expect. But you deserve love. Accepting it may bring up sadness about what you missed, and that's actually part of healing.

exact_quotes: One of the hardest things for you is to let people love you. You are very uncomfortable being treated well. It is so alien. You are much more comfortable being mistreated or ignored.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Twelve Self-Sabotage Patterns in the Failure Lifetrap
description: The Failure lifetrap manifests through specific behavioral patterns that reinforce feelings of inadequacy and prevent achievement. These patterns include avoiding skill development, choosing careers below potential, refusing promotions, inability to tolerate entry-level positions, repeated job terminations, career floating, pursuing impossible careers, fear of initiative, feeling fraudulent despite success, minimizing accomplishments, living vicariously through successful partners, and compensating through non-achievement assets.
mechanism: These behaviors create a self-fulfilling prophecy where the expectation of failure leads to avoidance and escape behaviors, which then produce actual failure, reinforcing the original belief of incompetence.
behavioral_signature: Choosing successful partners to live vicariously through

intervention.primary: Pattern identification and behavioral change planning
intervention.verbal: Review this list and identify which patterns apply to you. Most of these patterns boil down to the issue of Escape: you avoid taking the steps necessary to advance yourself.
intervention.behavioral: List your career history and identify which of the 12 patterns have characterized your approach to achievement
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: By identifying your specific pattern of self-sabotage, you can target the exact behaviors that are maintaining your feelings of failure and develop a concrete plan to change them.

exact_quotes: The Failure lifetrap feeds on itself in such a way that the entire arena of work becomes a disaster for you. Your expectation of failure becomes a self-fulfilling prophesy.
Many of these patterns boil down to the issue of Escape: you avoid taking the steps necessary to advance yourself.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Counterattack/Compensation Strategies for Failure Lifetrap
description: Individuals with the Failure lifetrap may compensate by excelling in non-achievement domains or by choosing successful partners. Men might excel in sports or seducing women; women might focus on looks or caregiving abilities. These compensations provide alternative sources of self-esteem while avoiding the core achievement issue.
mechanism: Compensation creates a fragile sense of worth that easily collapses because it does not address the fundamental avoidance of achievement challenges. It represents another escape strategy that maintains the underlying lifetrap.

intervention.verbal: These compensations are fragile. They easily collapse, and yield to the feeling of failure. You need to deal with the issue of achievement more directly.
intervention.behavioral: Identify your compensation strategies and recognize them as another form of avoidance rather than genuine self-esteem building
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: While excelling in other areas feels good temporarily, it doesn't address your core feelings of failure. True healing requires facing the achievement domain directly.

exact_quotes: Excelling in other roles is a way of compensating for the lifetrap. Men might excel in sports or seducing women; women might excel in their looks or ability to give to others.
These compensations are fragile. They easily collapse, and yield to the feeling of failure.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Eight Steps to Changing the Failure Lifetrap
description: A structured treatment protocol for the Failure lifetrap consisting of eight sequential steps: (1) assess accuracy of failure feelings, (2) connect with inner child who felt like failure, (3) help inner child see unfair treatment, (4) become aware of talents and accomplishments. If failure is accurate: (5) identify pattern in failures, (6) make a change plan, (7) create flashcard, (8) involve loved ones.
mechanism: The protocol addresses both cognitive distortions (for those with inaccurate failure perceptions) and behavioral avoidance (for those with accurate failure history), providing a comprehensive approach to schema change.

intervention.verbal: For those of you like Brian who have an inaccurate sense of failure, steps one to four should be sufficient. But most of you have a more accurate sense of failure. You need behavior change.
intervention.behavioral: Work through each step sequentially, with additional steps 5-8 if actual failure is present
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This structured approach helps you first understand your feelings and their origins, then make concrete behavioral changes to overcome avoidance patterns.

exact_quotes: Most of you need more. You need behavior change. You have to change your fundamental stance of escape and avoidance into one of confrontation and mastery.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure Step 1: Assess Whether Feeling of Failure Is Accurate or Distorted
description: The first treatment step involves objectively assessing whether the patient's perception of failure is accurate or distorted. This requires comparing oneself to peers from various achievement levels to determine actual standing in career and accomplishments.
mechanism: Distinguishing between accurate and inaccurate failure perceptions is crucial because treatment differs: distorted perceptions require primarily cognitive work, while accurate failure requires behavioral change.
behavioral_signature: Examining evidence for and against failure beliefs

intervention.verbal: List a range of people with whom you went to high school, college, or graduate school. Be sure to choose people from the bottom, middle, and top of your class. Write down what each person has accomplished within their chosen fields. How far have they progressed? How much are they paid? How much responsibility do they have at work? Where do you stand in comparison?
intervention.behavioral: Create a written list comparing your achievements to peers across the achievement spectrum
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Before we can address your feelings of failure, we need to determine whether they're based on reality or distorted thinking. This comparison helps us see where you actually stand.

exact_quotes: The first thing to do is assess the accuracy of your feeling of failure. As we have noted, most of the time, like Kathleen, you will have a lot of realistic evidence. You have in fact failed relative to your peers. But sometimes, like Brian, your perception of failure is inaccurate.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure Step 2: Get in Touch with Inner Child Who Felt Like a Failure
description: This step uses imagery to connect current failure feelings with childhood experiences. The patient recalls memories of being criticized, humiliated, compared, or discouraged, then uses imagery bridging from current triggers to childhood scenes to understand lifetrap origins.
mechanism: By connecting current emotional reactions to their childhood sources, patients gain insight into the origins of their lifetrap and can begin to separate past experiences from present reality.
behavioral_signature: Allowing childhood image to emerge spontaneously

intervention.verbal: Sit in a dark, quiet room and close your eyes. Get an image of the current event. Make the image as vivid and emotional as possible. Then let an image come of when you felt the same way as a child. Do not force the image. Just let it float to the top of your mind.
intervention.behavioral: Practice imagery bridging when failure feelings are triggered, documenting both current and childhood scenes
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your images can help you understand the origins of your lifetrap. That child who felt like a failure is still very much alive in you.

exact_quotes: Your images can help you understand the origins of your lifetrap. That child who felt like a failure is still very much alive in you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure Step 3: Help Inner Child See Unfair Treatment
description: This step helps patients recognize that childhood failure often resulted from being pushed in directions not naturally suited to them, having parents with their own agendas, or being undermined by competitive or critical parents. Patients are encouraged to get angry at those who made them feel like failures.
mechanism: Recognizing the unfairness of childhood experiences helps externalize the source of failure feelings and reduces self-blame, while expressing anger provides emotional release and strengthens the healthy adult perspective.
what_activates_it: Understanding parental influence on failure beliefs
behavioral_signature: Identifying natural talents that were ignored

intervention.verbal: What were some of your strengths and talents as a child? Were people's expectations of you realistic? How well could you have done if you had been praised, supported, and guided in areas of achievement where you had potential? Get angry at the people who made you feel like a failure. Talk back to your lifetrap. Stand up for yourself.
intervention.behavioral: Write letters (not to send), do imagery exercises, or directly confront those who contributed to failure feelings
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Many times when we fail as a child, it is because we are being pushed in a direction that is not naturally ours. Recognizing this unfairness can help you stop blaming yourself.

exact_quotes: Many times when we fail as a child, it is because we are being pushed in a direction that is not naturally ours. Some parents have their own agendas, and want a child to excel in certain areas regardless of what the child's particular talents and inclinations might be.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Assertive Confrontation of Critical Figures
description: A technique for directly confronting people who contributed to failure feelings, emphasizing calm, composed behavior while stating points simply and repeatedly. The focus is on expressing feelings, stating unfairness of past treatment, and requesting changed behavior going forward.
mechanism: Assertive confrontation builds self-efficacy and challenges the submissive stance that often accompanies the Failure lifetrap. The controlled expression of grievances provides emotional relief regardless of the other person's response.

intervention.verbal: Behave well. Stay calm and composed. State your points simply and briefly. If the person argues, just keep restating your position until you have had your say. Tell the person how they make you feel, and how you wish they could be instead.
intervention.behavioral: Plan and execute a controlled confrontation, or write a letter/do imagery if direct confrontation is not feasible
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You will find that it feels good to confront people if you can do it in this controlled, assertive way. Even if they don't change, you'll feel proud of standing up for yourself.

exact_quotes: The important thing is for you to confront the person in a way that makes you proud of yourself. Behave well. Stay calm and composed. State your points simply and briefly.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure Step 4: Become Aware of Talents, Skills, Abilities, and Accomplishments
description: This step emphasizes recognizing multiple types of intelligence (verbal, mathematical, visual-spatial, musical, physical, mechanical, interpersonal) and creating a comprehensive list of personal talents and accomplishments. Patients must resist the tendency to minimize achievements and maximize failures.
mechanism: Recognition of actual abilities counters the cognitive distortion of global failure and identifies areas where success is possible, providing a foundation for realistic career and life planning.

intervention.verbal: What are your special abilities? Do you have a flair for drawing? Are you mechanical or logical? Do you have a gift for sports or dance? Are you creative in some way? Do you get along well with people? It is the rare person who truly has no talents. Make a list of your talents, skills, abilities, and accomplishments in the area of achievement, especially your natural talents. Review this list each day to remind yourself of your potential.
intervention.behavioral: Create a written list of talents with help from others; review daily
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: There are many kinds of intelligence. By identifying your natural talents, we can see whether you've been capitalizing on them. The most successful people find their natural talents and use them.

exact_quotes: Remember an important principle: there are many kinds of intelligence. The intelligence you need to do well in school is not the only kind.
We believe that the people who are most successful are those who can find their natural talents and capitalize on them.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Brian's Talents and Accomplishments List
description: Brian, a patient with distorted failure feelings despite objective success as a prominent political writer, created a talents list including: good writer, creative with good ideas, argues persuasively, self-educated about politics, good sense of humor about politics, accomplishes a lot when anxiety is low, and prominent figure in New York political scene.
mechanism: The written list serves as concrete evidence against the Failure schema, providing a reality check that can be reviewed when failure feelings are triggered.

intervention.verbal: MY TALENTS AND ACCOMPLISHMENTS: 1. I am a good writer. 2. I have good ideas. I am creative. 3. I can argue persuasively. 4. I am highly self-educated about politics. 5. I have a good sense of humor, especially about politics. 6. When my anxiety isn't too high, I accomplish a lot at work. 7. I am a prominent figure in the New York political scene.
intervention.behavioral: Create similar list tailored to own talents and accomplishments
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Having a concrete written list gives you evidence to counter negative self-talk when failure feelings arise.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure Step 5: Identify the Pattern in Your Failures
description: For patients with actual failure history, this step involves taking a focused life history to identify recurring patterns in school and career. Key questions include whether failure was present from the start or emerged after initial potential, how parents handled success/failure, and whether avoidance has been the primary pattern.
mechanism: Pattern identification reveals that failure typically results from Escape behaviors rather than innate deficiency, shifting the focus from fixed trait ('I'm stupid') to changeable behavior ('I avoid challenges').
what_activates_it: Confirmation of actual failure in Step 1
behavioral_signature: Recognition of escape as underlying mechanism

intervention.primary: Focused life history for pattern recognition
intervention.verbal: Start from the very beginning. Go through your school and career life. Were you a failure from the start? Or were there indications of potential at the beginning that faded from lack of support? Try to see what your pattern has been in your career. Most likely, your pattern will boil down to the issue of Escape.
intervention.behavioral: Document complete educational and career history, identifying specific escape/avoidance behaviors at each stage
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You will find that your failure is the direct consequence of your tendency to avoid—rather than the result of some innate deficiency, lack of talent, or ineptness.

exact_quotes: Most likely, your pattern will boil down to the issue of Escape. You will find that your failure is the direct consequence of your tendency to avoid—rather than the result of some innate deficiency, lack of talent, or ineptness.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Core Experience of Feeling Trapped in Subjugation
description: Subjugated individuals consistently report feeling trapped, oppressed, and burdened by life. The constant responsibility of meeting others' needs is exhausting, and life loses its joy and freedom. Choices are dictated by effects on others rather than personal desires.
mechanism: When focus shifts entirely to others' wants and needs, the person experiences chronic oppression and exhaustion. Freedom is lost because all decisions filter through anticipated effects on others rather than personal preferences.

intervention.primary: Recognition of trapped feeling as signal
intervention.verbal: Notice when you feel trapped or burdened. This feeling is a signal that the subjugation pattern is active.
intervention.behavioral: Track instances when you feel trapped and identify whose needs you were prioritizing over your own
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The feeling of being trapped is not just life - it's a sign that your needs are being systematically ignored, usually by yourself.

exact_quotes: The feeling of subjugation is oppressive. It is a burden to live life under the weight of that feeling.
Subjugation deprives you of your freedom because the choices you make are dictated by their effects on other people.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Loss of Self-Identity in Subjugation
description: Subjugation robs the person of a clear sense of what they want and need, and ultimately who they are. The person becomes passive, feeling that life happens to them rather than being shaped by them. They may not know their own desires or preferences.
mechanism: Chronic suppression of personal needs leads to disconnection from internal signals of desire and preference. Without practice attending to one's own wants, the capacity to identify them atrophies.
behavioral_signature: saying 'I don't care, whatever you want'
not knowing career or life goals
waiting passively for things to improve

intervention.verbal: What would you want if no one else's feelings or reactions mattered?
intervention.behavioral: Practice making small choices based solely on your own preference, not others' comfort
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You've spent so long attending to others that you've lost touch with your own desires. We need to rebuild that connection.

exact_quotes: Subjugation robs you of a clear sense of what you want and need—of who you are.
Rather than an actor, you are a reactor.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Carlton's Inability to Direct His Own Life
description: Carlton exemplifies subjugation's effect on self-direction. Hounded by his father to enter the family business, he obeyed despite knowing it wasn't right for him. He has no idea what he actually wants to be because he never took steps to find out.
mechanism: Early subjugation to father's wishes prevented exploration of personal interests and goals. The pattern continued into adulthood, leaving Carlton unable to identify or pursue authentic life direction.
behavioral_signature: working in father's business against own wishes
feeling unable to get what he wants

intervention.verbal: CARLTON: I just feel like I can't get what I want in life. I don't know how to get it. THERAPIST: You feel like all you can get is what other people deign to give you. You don't go after what you want.
intervention.behavioral: Identify childhood interests that were suppressed and explore reconnecting with them
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You never learned to pursue your own goals because you were always meeting your father's. We need to discover what you actually want.

exact_quotes: Carlton, who was hounded by his father since childhood to go into the family business and who obeyed, knows inside that he does not want to be a businessman, but has no idea of what he does want to be.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Difficulty Setting Limits and Making Requests
description: Subjugated individuals have great difficulty setting limits on others' demands and asking others to change their behavior. They say yes to unreasonable requests and remain silent when others' behavior disturbs them.
mechanism: Fear of disapproval, conflict, or abandonment prevents limit-setting. The belief that one's own needs don't matter makes requests feel selfish or trivial.

intervention.verbal: What would you say if you believed your needs were just as important as theirs?
intervention.behavioral: Practice setting one small limit this week, even if it feels uncomfortable
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your inability to set limits isn't flexibility - it's a pattern that leaves your needs chronically unmet.

exact_quotes: But you have difficulty setting limits on the demands other people make of you. When people ask you to do things that are unreasonable, such as more than your share of the work, you say 'yes.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Lowered Self-Esteem and Entitlement
description: Subjugation lowers self-esteem through the implicit message that one is not entitled to legitimate rights in relationships. The person believes everyone has rights except them.
mechanism: Chronic subordination of one's needs to others creates the implicit belief that one's needs are less worthy. This belief becomes self-reinforcing as the person acts consistently with it.

intervention.verbal: What rights does every person have in relationships? Now, why would you be the one exception?
intervention.behavioral: List legitimate rights and practice claiming one this week
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You've come to believe your needs don't count. But your needs are just as legitimate as anyone else's.

exact_quotes: Your subjugation lowers your self-esteem. You do not feel entitled to the legitimate rights of all people in relationships. Everyone has rights except you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Carlton's Cold Food - Trivializing Own Needs
description: In a restaurant, Carlton received cold food but wouldn't ask the waitress to take it back, dismissing it as trivial. He ate the cold food while complaining to his wife all night, demonstrating the pattern of not asserting legitimate needs.
mechanism: Subjugated individuals minimize their desires as trivial to justify not asserting them, but the accumulation of unmet 'trivial' needs creates chronic dissatisfaction and passive resentment.
behavioral_signature: complaining to third parties instead of addressing problem

intervention.verbal: CARLTON: It just seemed like such a trivial thing to make a big fuss over. THERAPIST: But in the end, when you add all the trivial desires together, you are left with a life in which few of your needs are met.
intervention.behavioral: Next time something bothers you, address it directly rather than dismissing it as trivial
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Each desire you dismiss as trivial adds up. Eventually you have a life where none of your needs are met.

exact_quotes: We hear this argument often from subjugated patients: that they do not fight for what they want because their desires seem so trivial. But in the end, when you add all the trivial desires together, you are left with a life in which few of your needs are met.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Distinguishing Subjugation from Being Easygoing
description: Easygoing people have areas where they have strong feelings and will assert themselves. They may not express opinions on minor matters but will stand up for major issues. In subjugation, there are almost no strong opinions across the board regardless of stakes. Unexpressed anger is a key differentiating clue.
mechanism: True flexibility involves choice - the person could assert but chooses not to on minor matters. Subjugation involves inability to assert even on major matters, accompanied by suppressed anger.
behavioral_signature: no strong opinions on any issues
inability to assert on major matters

intervention.verbal: Easygoing people will stand up for certain things in major matters. Is there anything you feel strongly about and would fight for? Do you notice unexpressed anger building up?
intervention.behavioral: Monitor for anger signals that would indicate subjugation rather than genuine flexibility
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Being truly easygoing means you choose not to assert on minor things. Subjugation means you can't assert even when it really matters to you.

exact_quotes: Easygoing people have some areas in which they have strong feelings, and will assert what they feel.
In subjugation, there are almost no strong opinions across the board.
Unexpressed anger is another clue that you are subjugated rather than easygoing.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Merging or Losing Self in the Subjugator
description: Because subjugated individuals lack a strong sense of self, they risk losing themselves entirely in their subjugator. Boundaries become blurred, and they may adopt the other person's goals, opinions, and values as their own. This extends to vulnerability to cult groups.
mechanism: Without a developed sense of self, the person fills the void by absorbing the identity of whoever subjugates them. The subjugator's preferences become experienced as one's own.
behavioral_signature: attraction to charismatic leaders or groups
difficulty distinguishing own preferences from partner's

intervention.verbal: What do you believe that's different from what your partner believes? What would you want that's different from what they want?
intervention.behavioral: Identify three values or opinions that are uniquely yours, separate from your partner
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you don't know who you are, you fill that void with someone else's identity. We need to build your separate sense of self.

exact_quotes: You can become so immersed in trying to meet the needs of other people that you begin to blend or merge into these people.
You might lose yourself in the other. There is a chance that you might subjugate yourself to a group, particularly a group with a charismatic leader.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Two Types of Subjugation: Self-Sacrifice and Submissiveness
description: There are two major reasons why subjugated patients allow others to control them, corresponding to two types: (1) Self-Sacrifice - subjugation out of guilt, wanting to relieve others' pain; (2) Submissiveness - subjugation out of fear, anticipating rejection, retaliation, or abandonment.
mechanism: Self-sacrifice is driven by guilt and empathy; the person voluntarily suppresses needs to avoid guilt about others' suffering. Submissiveness is driven by fear; the person involuntarily suppresses needs to avoid punishment or abandonment.

intervention.verbal: Do you give in mainly because you feel guilty and want to help, or mainly because you're afraid of what will happen if you don't?
intervention.behavioral: Identify which emotion (guilt vs fear) drives your subjugation in different relationships
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding whether guilt or fear drives your pattern helps us target the right emotion in treatment.

exact_quotes: The first is that they subjugate themselves out of guilt, or because they want to relieve the pain of others; and the second is that they subjugate because they anticipate rejection, retaliation, or abandonment.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Self-Sacrifice Type of Subjugation
description: Self-sacrificers feel responsible for others' well-being. They may have experienced too much responsibility for a parent's physical or emotional welfare as a child. They are empathic, feel others' pain deeply, and want to ease it. Their subjugation is mostly voluntary - driven by others' pain and weakness rather than force.
mechanism: Guilt drives the lifetrap. The person feels guilty whenever they put themselves first, become angry, assert themselves, or fail to alleviate pain. To relieve guilt, they revert to self-sacrifice and bury anger, creating a cycle.
behavioral_signature: being the listener for everyone's problems

intervention.verbal: You are going to have to learn to tolerate this guilt in order to change. Each time you feel guilty, notice it, and assert yourself anyway.
intervention.behavioral: Practice one self-focused behavior this week and sit with the guilt without reverting to caretaking
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Guilt is the chain that keeps you subjugated. You'll need to tolerate feeling guilty in order to break free.

exact_quotes: Your lifetrap gets its strength primarily from the emotion of guilt.
You are going to have to learn to tolerate this guilt in order to change.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Carlton's Anger-Guilt Cycle with His Wife
description: Carlton displays a pattern of anger alternating with guilt in his relationship with Erica. The more he tries to please her, the more she demands. Her demands make him angry, but whenever he feels angry, he immediately feels guilty and tries to please her twice as hard.
mechanism: Self-sacrifice creates resentment when others take without reciprocating. When anger arises, guilt about the anger triggers renewed self-sacrifice, which creates more resentment, perpetuating the cycle.

intervention.verbal: Notice the pattern: you feel angry, then guilty, then you subjugate even harder. Can you sit with the anger instead of immediately feeling guilty?
intervention.behavioral: When you notice anger at your wife, express it directly instead of burying it in guilt
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The guilt that follows your anger keeps the cycle going. We need to break it by letting you feel angry without immediately punishing yourself.

exact_quotes: But, whenever Carlton feels angry, he immediately feels guilty and tries to please his wife twice as hard. In this way, he alternates between anger at his wife and guilt about his anger.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: William: Co-dependent Doctor with Self-Sacrifice Pattern
description: William, a doctor, channels his self-sacrificing tendencies productively into his medical work but struggles in personal relationships. He is co-dependent, consistently seeking out needy people, particularly alcoholic women, to form self-sacrificing relationships with. He has improved through a support group for children of alcoholics and is now in a healthier relationship where he can assert his needs.
mechanism: Self-sacrifice becomes channeled into helping professions but maintains dysfunction in personal relationships through repeated selection of needy partners who cannot reciprocate care.
what_activates_it: encounters with needy or dependent individuals
opportunities to rescue or save others
behavioral_signature: difficulty asserting needs in personal relationships

intervention.primary: Support group participation and practicing need assertion
intervention.behavioral: Join support group for children of alcoholics; practice asserting needs in current relationship
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Learning to form relationships where your needs have a chance of being met is healthier than repeatedly sacrificing yourself for people who cannot give back.

exact_quotes: His pattern is to seek out needy people, particularly alcoholic women, and form self-sacrificing relationships with them.
William is learning that it is healthier for him to form relationships in which his needs have a chance of being met.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Danger Signals in Potential Partners for Subjugation
description: A comprehensive list of 13 warning signs that indicate a potential partner will trigger and reinforce the Subjugation lifetrap. These signals identify domineering, self-centered, needy, or emotionally volatile partners who would perpetuate subjugated relationship patterns.
mechanism: These partner characteristics create relationship dynamics that demand continued subjugation, either through submission to a controlling partner or self-sacrifice to a needy one.

intervention.verbal: Review these 13 danger signals before entering or deepening a relationship: 1) Partner is domineering and expects to have things their way. 2) Partner has a very strong sense of self and knows exactly what they want. 3) Partner becomes irritated or angry when you disagree or attend to your own needs. 4) Partner does not respect your opinions, needs, or rights. 5) Partner pouts or pulls away when you do things your way. 6) Partner is easily hurt or upset, so you feel you have to take care of them. 7) You have to watch what you do or say because partner drinks a lot or has a bad temper. 8) Partner is not very competent, so you end up doing most of the work. 9) Partner is irresponsible, so you have to be overly responsible. 10) You let partner make most choices because you don't feel strongly. 11) Partner makes you feel guilty or calls you selfish when you ask for your way. 12) Partner becomes sad, worried, or depressed easily, so you do most of the listening. 13) Partner is very needy and dependent on you.
intervention.behavioral: Create a checklist of these danger signals and review it when evaluating potential or current partners
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You have the strongest feelings of attraction to partners who trigger your lifetrap. These relationships become intense because they unlock the emotions of your childhood subjugation. Learning to recognize these warning signs can help you break the pattern.

exact_quotes: You have the strongest feelings of attraction and attachment to partners who trigger your lifetrap.
These relationships become intense for you because they unlock the emotions of your childhood subjugation.
Again and again, you turn relationships into reenactments of your childhood subjugation.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Shannon: Dependent Wife with Submissive Subjugation
description: Shannon was raised as 'the good girl' with her mother making all decisions for her. She married the kind of man her parents wanted and is now 'the perfect wife.' She feels totally dependent on her husband Anthony, experiences anxiety when apart from him, and does everything right to avoid his anger. She fears his anger most because she believes if he got angry he might abandon her, and she feels certain she could never survive alone.
mechanism: Dependence maintains subjugation by making autonomy feel impossible, while subjugation maintains dependence by preventing development of independent functioning. The fear of abandonment drives compliance.
behavioral_signature: avoiding any action that might provoke anger

intervention.primary: Address interaction between Subjugation and Dependence lifetraps
intervention.verbal: What is so bad about Anthony getting mad at you?
intervention.behavioral: Practice tolerating brief separations; gradually express small preferences
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your dependence maintains your subjugation, and your subjugation maintains your dependence. Breaking this cycle requires building tolerance for temporary discomfort.

exact_quotes: Shannon feels totally dependent upon her husband.
Her dependence maintains her subjugation, and her subjugation maintains her dependence.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Partner Selection Patterns in Subjugation
description: Three distinct partner selection patterns based on subjugation subtype: Submissive types seek aggressive, dominant leaders who make decisions for them. Self-sacrificing types are drawn to needy, dependent partners they can rescue, or narcissistic partners who are demanding but give little. Rebels pick partners who are even more subjugated, so they can be in control.
mechanism: Each partner type recreates the subjugated dynamic from childhood, either by playing the familiar subjugated role or by taking the opposite position (rebel) while still enacting the same relational template.
behavioral_signature: rushing to fill needy partners' needs
attempting to save or rescue partners
choosing someone more passive to control

intervention.verbal: Review past relationships and clarify your pattern of choosing controlling or needy partners. List the danger signals for you to avoid.
intervention.behavioral: Create written list of past partner characteristics; identify common patterns; develop explicit criteria for healthier partner selection
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding your pattern of partner selection is crucial because you repeatedly turn relationships into reenactments of your childhood subjugation.

exact_quotes: One common pattern among subjugated people who are submissive is to seek out relationships with aggressive, dominant figures—with leaders.
If you are self-sacrificing, you may be drawn to needy and dependent partners.
If you are a rebel, you might pick someone who is even more subjugated than you, so you can be the one in control.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation Lifetrap Assessment Questionnaire
description: A 26-item self-assessment questionnaire covering all manifestations of the Subjugation lifetrap including submission, self-sacrifice, passive-aggression, rebellion, and their impacts on work and relationships. Items address both behavioral patterns and emotional experiences associated with subjugation.
mechanism: Self-assessment identifies specific behavioral and emotional patterns that maintain subjugation, allowing targeted intervention planning.
behavioral_signature: anger at being told what to do

intervention.verbal: Rate yourself on each item: 1) You let other people have their own way most of the time. 2) You are too eager to please—you will do almost anything to be liked or accepted. 3) You do not like to disagree openly with other people's opinions. 4) You are more comfortable when other people are in positions of control. 5) You will do almost anything to avoid confrontation or anger. You always accommodate. 6) You do not know what you want or prefer in many situations. 7) You are not clear about your career decisions. 8) You always end up taking care of everyone else—almost no one listens to or takes care of you. 9) You are rebellious—you automatically say 'no' when other people tell you what to do. 10) You cannot stand to say or do anything that hurts other people's feelings. 11) You often stay in situations where you feel trapped or where your needs are not met. 12) You do not want other people to see you as selfish so you go to the other extreme. 13) You often sacrifice yourself for the sake of other people. 14) You often take on more than your share of responsibilities at home and/or at work. 15) When other people are troubled or in pain, you try very hard to make them feel better, even at your own expense. 16) You often feel angry at other people for telling you what to do. 17) You often feel cheated—that you are giving more than you are getting back. 18) You feel guilty when you ask for what you want. 19) You do not stand up for your rights. 20) You resist doing what other people want you to do in an indirect way. You procrastinate, make mistakes, and make excuses. 21) You cannot get along with authority figures. 22) You cannot ask for promotions or raises at work. 23) You feel that you lack integrity—you accommodate too much. 24) People tell you that you are not aggressive or ambitious enough. 25) You play down your accomplishments. 26) You have trouble being strong in negotiations.
intervention.behavioral: Complete questionnaire and identify highest-rated items as treatment targets
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These are pitfalls for you to avoid in love and work. Even if you find a partner who wants a relationship based on equality, you may still find ways to reinforce your lifetrap.

exact_quotes: These are pitfalls for you to avoid in love and work.
Even if you find a partner who wants a relationship based on equality, you may still find ways to reinforce your lifetrap.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Anger Build-Up Pattern in Subjugation
description: Whatever relationship subjugated individuals form, they are bound to have anger simmering below the surface. Early in relationships, they suppress anger and avoid conflict, which helps keep the relationship intact but is hard to maintain. After years, anger may build to a point where the person rebels completely, withdraws, retaliates, or experiences sexual difficulties. This threatens relationship stability.
mechanism: Chronic suppression of legitimate anger creates cumulative emotional pressure that eventually destabilizes the relationship through eruption, withdrawal, or passive expression.

intervention.primary: Express anger appropriately as soon as it arises
intervention.verbal: Practice confronting people instead of accommodating so much. Express your anger appropriately, as soon as you feel it.
intervention.behavioral: Identify early signs of anger; practice immediate, proportionate expression rather than suppression
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The build-up of anger threatens the stability of your relationships. Suppressing anger increases rather than decreases it, making inappropriate expression more likely later.

exact_quotes: Whatever relationship you form, you are bound to have anger simmering below the surface.
The build-up of anger is something that threatens the stability of subjugated relationships.
After a period of years, your anger may build up to such a point that you rebel, completely upsetting the balance of the relationship, or you may withdraw or retaliate.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation Patterns at Work
description: Subjugated people often work in helping professions if self-sacrificing (doctor, nurse, homemaker, teacher, minister, therapist). They may be the devoted right hand of a more powerful person. They are obedient, loyal, demand little, rarely ask for raises, try to please superiors, have trouble setting limits on sacrifice. The lifetrap gift is acute sensitivity to others' needs and pain.
mechanism: The work environment becomes another arena for reenacting subjugated patterns, with authority figures replacing parental figures and professional demands replacing family demands.

intervention.verbal: Be more aggressive at work. Take credit for what you do. Do not let other people take advantage of you. Ask for any promotions or raises you might be entitled to get. Delegate responsibilities to other people.
intervention.behavioral: Practice requesting appropriate compensation; set limits on work acceptance; delegate tasks to subordinates
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You are probably just the type of person a boss wants to hire—obedient, loyal, and demanding little. But this damages your chances for advancement and builds resentment.

exact_quotes: Subjugated people often work in one of the helping professions, particularly if they are self-sacrificing.
You are obedient, loyal, and demand little. It is probably a rare event for you to ask for a raise.
The Subjugation lifetrap takes a lot away from you, but one of its gifts is acute sensitivity to the needs and pain of others.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Carlton and Erica: Work Takes Priority Over Family
description: In a marital session, Erica expresses frustration that Carlton won't take vacation with her because he won't ask his father for time off. They haven't had a vacation together in six years. Carlton explains he can't let his father down, saying 'They need me too much at the office.' His work is always higher priority than his own desires or family needs.
mechanism: Subjugation to father/boss prevents meeting own needs and maintaining marital relationship, creating tension as spouse's legitimate needs go unmet.
what_activates_it: need to assert self with father/authority figure
behavioral_signature: refusing to ask for time off

intervention.behavioral: Practice making requests of authority figures starting with easier situations
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your subjugation to your father is damaging your marriage and preventing you from getting what you want.

exact_quotes: Another thing I'm mad about is that Carlton won't take a vacation with me. He won't ask his father for the time. We haven't gone on a vacation together in six years!
You just don't understand. They need me too much at the office. I know Dad would be disappointed. I just can't let him down that way.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Direct Communication in Assertiveness
description: A core assertiveness technique emphasizing brevity, use of 'I' statements, and speaking in terms of one's own feelings. Subjugated individuals are taught to avoid speeches and to own their feelings directly rather than generalizing.
mechanism: By speaking directly and owning feelings with 'I' statements, the person makes statements that cannot be argued with, validates their own experience, and communicates that their feelings matter. This counters the subjugation pattern of self-erasure.
what_activates_it: Opportunities to express preferences or feelings
behavioral_signature: Speaking briefly and to the point

intervention.verbal: Instead of saying, 'People feel angry when they are cut off that way,' say 'I felt angry when you cut me off.' By expressing what you feel, you make a statement that how you feel matters. No one can argue with your feelings.
intervention.behavioral: Practice converting generalized statements into 'I' statements. In each hierarchy item, express feelings directly using 'I'.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you say 'I feel' instead of 'people feel,' you're claiming your own experience. No one can tell you that you didn't feel something. This makes your communication more powerful and shows that your feelings matter.

exact_quotes: Interestingly, many subjugated people avoid the word 'I' whenever they talk about their feelings.
By expressing what you feel, you make a statement that how you feel matters.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 6: Practice Asking Others to Take Care of You
description: Treatment step teaching subjugated individuals to ask for help, discuss their problems, talk about themselves, and achieve balance between giving and receiving in relationships.
mechanism: Subjugated people habitually give but cannot receive, creating one-sided relationships. By practicing asking for help and talking about oneself, the person learns that their needs are legitimate and that sharing brings closeness rather than rejection.
what_activates_it: Situations where help could be requested
behavioral_signature: Getting anxious when talking about self too long
Switching focus back to other person

intervention.primary: Graduated practice of self-disclosure and help-seeking
intervention.verbal: When you feel anxious talking about yourself, understand that it is okay to talk about yourself. It is okay to talk about your problems and ask for help. You will find that it brings you closer to people.
intervention.behavioral: Practice talking about yourself longer in conversations even when anxious. Ask others for help with problems. Monitor the give-get ratio in relationships.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You've learned to always focus on others. But real closeness comes from mutual sharing. When you share your problems and ask for help, you'll find people want to be there for you—and those who don't may need to be reevaluated.

exact_quotes: Many subjugated patients tell us that when they talk about themselves for 'too long,' they start to get anxious, and they switch back to the other person.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 7: Pull Back from Self-Centered Relationships
description: Treatment step advising subjugated individuals to distance themselves from or end relationships with people who are too self-centered to consider their needs, including recognizing that some relationships cannot be preserved.
mechanism: Subjugated individuals often remain in exploitative relationships due to guilt, fear, or habit. Learning that not all relationships should be preserved breaks the cycle of subjugation and models healthy boundary-setting.
behavioral_signature: Feeling trapped but unable to leave

intervention.verbal: Some relationships are simply too damaging, and have too few prospects for change. If, in the final analysis, they will not change, then you have to pull back from the relationship. You may even have to end it.
intervention.behavioral: Give partners opportunities to change with clear communication. If they refuse to adjust, progressively pull back. Consider ending relationships that remain exploitative.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Not all relationships deserve to be saved. Some people won't change no matter what you do. Staying in those relationships only hurts you. You deserve relationships where your needs matter too.

exact_quotes: We no longer feel that relationships should be preserved at all costs. Some relationships are simply too damaging, and have too few prospects for change.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Carlton: Marriage Survived Through Assertiveness
description: Case example demonstrating positive outcome when a subjugated person becomes more assertive. Carlton's wife Erica initially resisted his changes but ultimately welcomed his increased strength, feeling more secure with limits set on her demandingness.
mechanism: When subjugated individuals become assertive, controlling partners may initially resist but can ultimately benefit from the change, feeling more secure and contained. This demonstrates that assertiveness can improve rather than destroy relationships.

intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Some partners will actually feel relieved when you become stronger, even if they resist at first. Setting limits can make them feel more secure.

exact_quotes: As he became more assertive, Erica at first fought it tooth and nail. But at some level she also welcomed it. In her heart she was glad to have him become stronger.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mary Ellen: Marriage Ended Due to Partner's Inability to Accept Change
description: Case example demonstrating that some relationships cannot survive when a subjugated person grows. Mary Ellen's husband Dennis could not accept her increased assertiveness, leading her to leave the marriage, pursue education, and begin dating.
mechanism: Some controlling partners are too invested in maintaining dominance to accept a partner's growth. In such cases, ending the relationship becomes necessary for the subjugated person's continued development.

intervention.primary: Relationship evaluation and decision to leave
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Some partners simply cannot tolerate you becoming your own person. When that happens, leaving may be the healthiest choice, even though it's painful.

exact_quotes: Dennis could not accept her growth. He was too invested in being the one in control. Eventually Mary Ellen left him.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 8: Confront People and Express Anger Appropriately
description: Treatment step teaching subjugated individuals to express anger constructively, confront rather than accommodate, and tolerate others' negative reactions. Includes specific guidelines for calm, effective confrontation.
mechanism: Subjugated individuals typically suppress anger, leading to passive-aggressive behavior or explosive outbursts. Learning to express anger calmly and constructively improves relationships and reduces internal pressure.

intervention.verbal: Whatever the other person does, keep calmly restating your position. Do not let the other person trick you into becoming defensive. Stick to your point. Stay calm. You are much more powerful when you are calm than when you are screaming. Screaming is a sign of psychological defeat.
intervention.behavioral: Practice confrontations starting with positive statements, directing criticism at behavior not person, requesting specific change, and ending positively. Use good timing and assertive body language.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You've learned to swallow your anger, but it comes out anyway in unhealthy ways. Learning to express anger calmly and directly actually improves your relationships and helps you feel better.

exact_quotes: You are much more powerful when you are calm than when you are screaming. Screaming is a sign of psychological defeat.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Constructive Confrontation Technique
description: A structured approach to confrontation for subjugated individuals: start with something positive, criticize behavior not person, request specific change, and end on a positive note. Emphasizes calm delivery, good timing, and assertive body language.
mechanism: The structure prevents defensiveness in the listener and psychological defeat in the speaker. By maintaining calm and using a positive framework, the message is more likely to be heard and the speaker maintains their power.
what_activates_it: Need to confront someone about their behavior
Situations requiring criticism or negative feedback

intervention.verbal: Carlton began one of his more difficult items ('Tell Erica not to reprimand me in front of other people') with the statement, 'Erica, I know you love me.' Then: 'You criticize me at times in front of other people.' He concluded by saying, 'I really appreciate that you were able to stand here and listen to me.'
intervention.behavioral: Practice confrontations in front of mirror. Plan timing carefully—not during highly emotional states. Maintain eye contact and assertive body language.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This structure helps the other person actually hear you instead of getting defensive. When you stay calm and frame things positively, you're much more likely to get what you need.

exact_quotes: Direct your criticism not at the person, but at the person's behavior.
People can only listen when they are in a receptive state. If you make people angry, they will become defensive and shut you off.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 9: Stop Rationalizing People-Pleasing
description: Treatment step targeting the cognitive distortion of minimizing one's own preferences. Subjugated individuals are taught to express preferences starting with trivial matters and progressing to important ones, rather than telling themselves their preferences don't matter.
mechanism: Rationalizing that preferences don't matter maintains the subjugation pattern by preventing the person from ever practicing asserting needs. By stopping this rationalization and expressing preferences, the person learns their preferences do matter.
what_activates_it: Moments of telling self 'it doesn't matter'
behavioral_signature: Saying 'it doesn't matter' or 'I don't care'

intervention.verbal: The moment I started to change in therapy was one night when Erica asked me what I wanted for dinner, steak or hamburger. I started to tell her that I didn't really care, and I stopped. And I picked steak.
intervention.behavioral: At every opportunity, weigh positives and negatives to decide preference. Make a choice and communicate it, starting with trivial matters and progressing to important ones.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Every time you say 'I don't care' or 'it doesn't matter,' you're erasing yourself a little more. Your preferences do matter. Start noticing them and expressing them.

exact_quotes: This may sound strange, but I really believe that the moment I started to change in therapy was one night when Erica asked me what I wanted for dinner, steak or hamburger.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 10: Review Relationship Patterns and Identify Danger Signals
description: Treatment step involving systematic review of past relationships to identify patterns of choosing controlling or needy partners, with emphasis on recognizing that high chemistry often accompanies unhealthy partner choices.
mechanism: Subjugated individuals are typically most attracted to exactly the type of partners who will exploit them. By making patterns conscious and identifying danger signals, the person can make healthier choices despite lower initial chemistry.
what_activates_it: Feeling strong chemistry with new partner
behavioral_signature: Attraction to bullying or guilt-tripping partners

intervention.verbal: Are you drawn to domineering partners? Do you melt into the lives of your partners so that you have no separate sense of self? Are you drawn to people who bully you with threats, or guilt-trip you? Or are you drawn to helpless, dependent people who need you to take care of them?
intervention.behavioral: Make list of important past relationships. Identify common patterns and danger signals. Consciously avoid partners who match these patterns even when chemistry is high.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You tend to be most attracted to exactly the partners who will exploit you. The chemistry is high, but the cost is too great. Learning to recognize your danger signals can help you choose healthier relationships.

exact_quotes: The patterns you identify are the ones for you to avoid. We know this will be hard for you because you tend to be most attracted to exactly these types of partners.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Step 11: Give Healthy Relationships a Chance Despite Lower Chemistry
description: Treatment step encouraging subjugated individuals to persist with partners who treat them as equals, even when initial chemistry is moderate, recognizing that chemistry may increase as the healthy pattern becomes more familiar.
mechanism: Subjugated individuals often abandon healthy relationships because they feel strange or lack the intense chemistry of exploitative relationships. By persisting despite strangeness, they can develop attraction to healthier dynamics.
what_activates_it: Feeling 'something is missing' in healthy relationship
behavioral_signature: Giving up on good relationships too soon

intervention.verbal: As long as you feel some chemistry—even a moderate amount—give the relationship a chance. As you become more accustomed to your new role, the chemistry might increase.
intervention.behavioral: When in relationship with respectful partner, persist despite feelings of strangeness. Give minimum time commitment before evaluating. Notice if chemistry increases over time.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Healthy relationships might feel boring or strange at first because you're used to the intensity of being exploited. Give these relationships time—the chemistry can grow as equality becomes more familiar.

exact_quotes: Subjugated people frequently give up too soon on good relationships, claiming they just are not interested, the relationship does not feel right, something is missing, or there is not enough chemistry.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards Origin: Parental Modeling
description: Parents who were themselves perfectionistic, orderly, status oriented, or high achieving model these attitudes and behaviors to their children. Often this origin is striking because no one in the family recognizes that the standards are unusually high - they feel normal to everybody. The child learns purely through observation without explicit statements about expectations.
mechanism: The child internalizes parental standards through observation and imitation. Because the entire family operates under these standards, they become normalized and invisible, making them resistant to later questioning or modification.
what_activates_it: household emphasis on order and achievement
immediate correction of any mess or imperfection
behavioral_signature: not recognizing own standards as high
describing perfectionistic parents as 'normal average people'
treating extremely high standards as baseline expectations

intervention.verbal: Until I started therapy, I never really thought of my standards as unrealistic. I never used to think of my parents as perfectionistic.
intervention.behavioral: Interview family members or reflect on specific memories about household rules, work habits, and reactions to imperfection
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When high standards are all you've ever known, they feel normal. Understanding that your parents modeled these patterns helps you see them as learned rather than natural or necessary.

exact_quotes: Your parents may have been models of unrelenting standards. They themselves were perfectionistic, orderly, status oriented, or high achieving. You learned their attitudes and behavior. Often this origin is striking because no one in the family knows that the standards are so high. They feel normal to everybody.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards Origin: Conditional Love and Withdrawal
description: Parents either fail to give praise when a child does well, treating high achievement as merely average, or actively withdraw love when the child falls short of expectations. This creates an environment where the child must constantly strive to earn affection that remains perpetually out of reach.
mechanism: When love is contingent on performance, the child learns that their worth depends on achievement. Because standards are impossibly high, the child rarely receives the love, respect, or attention they seek, creating an endless cycle of striving.
what_activates_it: achieving something good but receiving no acknowledgment
parental silence or withdrawal after imperfect performance
love withdrawn for grades below expectations
behavioral_signature: memories of high grades receiving no attention
feeling that extraordinary achievement was required for basic acknowledgment

intervention.verbal: I have so many memories of coming home from school with a top grade and not even getting any attention for it. It had to be something extraordinary for me to get attention.
intervention.behavioral: List specific childhood memories where achievement went unacknowledged or imperfection led to withdrawal of affection
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding that your drive for perfection developed because love felt conditional helps you recognize this as a childhood survival strategy, not an accurate measure of your worth.

exact_quotes: Parents can either fail to give praise when a child does well or actually withdraw love when the child falls short of their expectations.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards Origin: Compensation for Shame or Inferiority
description: Some individuals develop Unrelenting Standards as a reaction to feeling inferior relative to peers or ashamed of their family circumstances. High achievement or status becomes a strategy to rise above childhood environments and compensate for feelings of social exclusion or defectiveness.
mechanism: The drive for achievement functions as a counterattack against underlying shame or feelings of inadequacy. Success becomes a way to prove worth and escape the pain of feeling inferior, but because the underlying shame remains unaddressed, the striving is never satisfying.
what_activates_it: comparing self unfavorably to wealthier peers
parental feelings of having married beneath them
behavioral_signature: planning entire life around advancing social class
parent living vicariously through child's achievements

intervention.primary: Identify the underlying lifetrap driving compensation
intervention.verbal: I felt like where I came from wasn't good enough. I wanted to get to where the rich kids were. I wanted the things they had. I decided early on I was going to get the things the rich kids had.
intervention.behavioral: Explore whether achievement is compensating for Social Exclusion, Defectiveness, or Emotional Deprivation lifetraps
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Sometimes our drive for perfection is really an attempt to escape feelings of shame or not belonging. Understanding this helps us address the real wound rather than just running faster.

exact_quotes: Keith's Unrelenting Standards were a reaction to shame regarding his family's Social Exclusion.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards Origin: Parent Living Vicariously
description: A parent who feels unfulfilled or believes they married beneath them may spur the child on to achieve the status or success the parent desired for themselves. The parent fulfills their own desires vicariously through the child's achievements, creating constant pressure.
mechanism: The child internalizes the parent's voice as an ever-present taskmaster, making relaxation impossible. The child carries the burden of fulfilling the parent's unmet needs and desires, experiencing perpetual pressure to achieve.
what_activates_it: parent feeling they married beneath them
hearing parent's voice driving achievement even when alone
behavioral_signature: hearing parent's voice pushing for more
constant sense of obligations and tasks waiting

intervention.primary: Imagery work with internalized parental voice
intervention.verbal: I'm in bed trying to sleep, and I hear her voice going on and on. She's saying, 'You better get going, there are things you should be doing. Have you finished your homework, don't you need to practice your tennis, don't you have friends to call?'
intervention.behavioral: Practice responding to the internalized parental voice with healthy self-statements
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: That voice pushing you is not really you - it's your parent's unfulfilled desires that got planted in your head. You have the right to set your own standards.

exact_quotes: Keith's mother felt that she belonged in a higher class and had married beneath her. She fulfilled her desire for status vicariously through Keith. Consequently Keith could never relax.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards: Childhood Memories Focus on Failure
description: Although people with Unrelenting Standards are usually remarkably successful as adults, their childhood memories rarely focus on feelings of success. Instead, they are much more likely to remember feeling defective, excluded, or lonely. Regardless of how hard they tried, they rarely got the respect, admiration, attention, or love they wanted.
mechanism: The impossibly high bar for success means even genuine achievements failed to register as worthy of pride. What remains in memory is the gap between performance and the unattainable standard, reinforcing feelings of inadequacy despite objective success.
behavioral_signature: vivid memories of failures but not successes
recalling disappointment and pressure rather than pride
remembering striving hard yet failing to meet standards

intervention.verbal: What do you remember feeling as a child - success or failure? Despite your achievements, what emotions come up?
intervention.behavioral: List childhood achievements and examine why they didn't feel like successes at the time
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your memories of failure reflect impossible standards, not actual failure. Understanding this helps you see your achievements more accurately.

exact_quotes: Although people with Unrelenting Standards are usually remarkably successful as adults, their childhood memories rarely focus on feelings of success. In fact, they are much more likely to remember feeling defective, excluded, or lonely.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Link Between Unrelenting Standards and Defectiveness
description: If parents used shame or criticism when the child fell short of their expectations, the child almost certainly develops a Defectiveness lifetrap alongside Unrelenting Standards. The two lifetraps become intertwined, with achievement functioning as an attempt to counteract feelings of defectiveness.
mechanism: Shame and criticism for imperfection create a core sense of defectiveness. Unrelenting Standards develop as a counterattack strategy to prove worth and escape the shame, but because the underlying defectiveness remains, no achievement ever feels sufficient.
behavioral_signature: using achievement to counteract feelings of worthlessness

intervention.verbal: When you fall short of your standards, do you feel shame? Do you feel fundamentally flawed?
intervention.behavioral: Explore whether Defectiveness is the core lifetrap driving Unrelenting Standards
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your perfectionism may be trying to protect you from deeper feelings of shame or defectiveness. We need to address both issues.

exact_quotes: If your parents used shame or criticism when you fell short of their expectations, you almost certainly have a Defectiveness lifetrap as well.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards Danger Signals
description: Eleven warning signs indicating the Unrelenting Standards lifetrap is active and causing problems. These signals span health, relationships, pleasure, and functioning domains.
mechanism: These danger signals indicate that the relentless pursuit of high standards is creating negative consequences across multiple life domains while the person remains unaware of the connection between their standards and their distress.
behavioral_signature: life revolving around success and status

intervention.verbal: Do you recognize any of these patterns: health suffering from daily stresses, not from unavoidable events? Life feeling like constant pressure without fun? Losing touch with what really makes you happy?
intervention.behavioral: Rate yourself on each danger signal and track which appear most frequently
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These are warning signs that your standards are costing you more than they're giving you. Recognizing them is the first step to change.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards Lifetrap Indicators
description: A comprehensive list of 11 danger signals specifically for Unrelenting Standards: 1) Health suffering from daily stresses/overwork, 2) Lopsided work-pleasure balance, 3) Life revolving around success/status/material things, 4) Too much energy on keeping life in order, 5) Relationships suffering from time spent on standards, 6) Making others feel inadequate, 7) Rarely stopping to enjoy successes, 8) Feeling overwhelmed trying to accomplish too much, 9) Activities feeling like ordeals, 10) Procrastination due to overwhelming standards, 11) Chronic irritation at things not meeting standards.
mechanism: Provides specific behavioral indicators to identify when Unrelenting Standards are active and causing harm across health, relationships, emotional wellbeing, and functioning domains.

intervention.verbal: Review each item: 1) Your health is suffering because of daily stresses, such as overwork. 2) The balance between work and pleasure feels lopsided. 3) Your whole life seems to revolve around success, status, and material things. 4) Too much of your energy goes into keeping your life in order. 5) Your relationships are suffering because so much time goes into meeting your standards. 6) You make other people feel inadequate around you. 7) You rarely stop and enjoy successes. 8) You feel overwhelmed because you are trying to accomplish so much. 9) Your standards are so high that many activities feel like ordeals. 10) You procrastinate because your standards make tasks feel overwhelming. 11) You feel irritated a lot because things don't meet your standards.
intervention.behavioral: Check each item that applies and count total indicators
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This list helps you see the specific ways your standards may be hurting you. The more items you check, the more important it is to address this lifetrap.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Loss of Natural Self in Unrelenting Standards
description: The basic problem with Unrelenting Standards is losing touch with the natural self. The person becomes so focused on order, achievement, or status that they do not attend to basic physical, emotional, and social needs. Things like love, family, friendship, creativity, and fun take a back seat to the obsessive quest for perfection.
mechanism: The constant focus on meeting standards crowds out awareness of genuine needs and desires. The person functions on automatic, losing connection to what actually brings fulfillment and meaning to life.
what_activates_it: chronic focus on achievement or order
behavioral_signature: not knowing what really makes one happy

intervention.primary: Reconnecting with authentic needs and desires
intervention.verbal: Sometimes I feel like a machine, like I'm not really alive. Like I'm running on automatic.
intervention.behavioral: Practice identifying genuine preferences and desires separate from achievement goals
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your standards have disconnected you from your real self. Reconnecting with what genuinely makes you happy is essential for a fulfilling life.

exact_quotes: The basic problem with Unrelenting Standards is that you lose touch with your natural self. You are so focused on order, achievement, or status that you do not attend to your basic physical, emotional, and social needs.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards Impact on Relationships
description: Intimate relationships almost certainly suffer from Unrelenting Standards. The person may seek the 'perfect partner' and be unable to settle for less, be extremely critical and demanding within relationships, devalue partners for not meeting standards, or have no time left for loved ones due to work or maintaining order.
mechanism: High standards extend to partner selection and relationship expectations. The person applies the same impossible standards to others that they apply to themselves, leading to chronic criticism, devaluation, and emotional distance.
behavioral_signature: devaluing partner for not meeting standards

intervention.verbal: Do you expect your partner to meet the same impossible standards you set for yourself? How does this affect your relationship?
intervention.behavioral: Track critical thoughts and comments toward partner for one week
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your high standards are affecting the people you love most. Learning to accept imperfection in others is as important as accepting it in yourself.

exact_quotes: Your intimate relationships almost certainly suffer. You may want the perfect partner and be unable to settle for less.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependent Entitlement
description: A subtype of Entitlement where the person feels entitled to depend on other people. They place themselves in a weak, incompetent, needy role and expect others to be strong and take care of them. The entitlement manifests as a childlike expectation that others owe them care and support.
mechanism: The person adopts a helpless stance that obligates others to provide care, using passive-aggressive behaviors, pouting, whining, and occasional temper tantrums when needs are not met. This maintains dependence while avoiding personal responsibility.
what_activates_it: Being asked to complete tasks independently
behavioral_signature: Letting others handle everyday affairs and decisions
Passive-aggressive behavior when not cared for

intervention.primary: Gradual independence building with hierarchy of tasks
intervention.behavioral: Create hierarchy of tasks graded by difficulty; gradually do things you allow others to do for you
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You may not feel special, but you feel entitled to be dependent because you feel weak and vulnerable. Learning to do things yourself will prove you are more competent than you believe.

exact_quotes: If you are the dependent type, you feel entitled to depend on other people. You place yourself in the weak, incompetent, needy role, and expect other people to be strong and take care of you.
You feel entitled much in the same way as a child feels toward a parent. It is your right. People owe it to you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Impulsivity Pattern in Entitlement
description: A lifelong pattern of difficulty controlling behavior and feelings. Characterized by problems with impulse control, acting on desires without regard for consequences, difficulty tolerating frustration for long-term tasks, general lack of organization and structure, and undisciplined behavior.
mechanism: The person cannot insert thought between impulse and action, leading to immediate gratification at the expense of long-term goals. Problems with frustration tolerance prevent completion of boring or routine tasks, creating chronic underachievement.
behavioral_signature: Addictions (overeating, smoking, drinking, drugs, compulsive sex)
