intervention.verbal: When we asked him to write down his negative thoughts for homework, he did not do it. He claimed, 'Why think about things? They only make me feel worse.' When we asked him to close his eyes and picture himself as a child, he said, 'I can't see anything. My mind is blank.'
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: By escaping, you never overcome the lifetrap. Since you never confront the truth, you are stuck. You cannot change things that you do not admit are problems.

exact_quotes: When we asked him how he felt about himself, he denied having feelings of low self-esteem or shame.
When we asked him how he felt about his abusive father, he insisted that he felt no anger. 'My father was a good man,' he said.
We had to battle his Escape on every front.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Counterattack
description: Max counterattacks his Defectiveness lifetrap by maintaining an air of superiority, being critical of others while never acknowledging faults in himself. He spends his life trying to keep the worthless child at bay.
mechanism: Projects self-confidence and superiority, becomes a snob, criticizes others constantly, chooses flattering friends and employees, seeks prestige and status, manipulates people, stays one up in all relationships including therapy.
what_activates_it: being treated as equal rather than special

intervention.verbal: In feeling superior, Max experiences the opposite of what he felt as a child. He is trying to be as different as possible from the worthless child that his parents made him feel he was. We might say he spends his whole life trying to keep that child at bay.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your superiority is a defense against feeling worthless. Underneath, you are very fragile. When there is a major setback, the counterattack collapses and the original feelings return with enormous strength.

exact_quotes: Max is not in touch with his lifetrap yet it is very much with him.
He questioned our credentials, approach, competence, level of success, and age.
He felt he was not getting the special treatment he deserved.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Escape — General Patterns Across Lifetraps
description: Escape involves avoiding thinking about the lifetrap, dampening feelings when generated, and avoiding situations that might activate the lifetrap. Different lifetraps lead to different avoidance patterns.
mechanism: Push lifetrap out of mind, dampen feelings through substances or compulsive behaviors, avoid vulnerable situations entirely, create psychological distance from threatening stimuli.
what_it_maintains: lost opportunities for connection and growth
behavioral_signature: Failure: avoiding work, school tasks, promotions
Social Exclusion: avoiding groups, parties, meetings
Dependence: avoiding situations requiring independence, phobic about public places alone

intervention.verbal: If you have the Defectiveness lifetrap, you may avoid intimate relationships altogether, never letting anybody get too close. If you have the Failure lifetrap, you may avoid work, school tasks, promotions, or taking on new projects. If you have the Social Exclusion lifetrap, you may avoid groups, parties, meetings, conventions. If you have the Dependence lifetrap, you may avoid all situations that require you to be independent.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The disadvantage of Escape is that you never overcome the lifetrap. Since you never confront the truth, you are stuck.

exact_quotes: We take drugs, or overeat, or compulsively clean, or become a workaholic.
Many people escape whole areas of life where they feel vulnerable or sensitive.
In trying to coast through life without feeling pain, we rob ourselves of the chance to change the things that are causing us pain.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Counterattack — General Consequences
description: Counterattackers try to make up for the lifetrap by convincing themselves and others that the opposite is true. They can appear healthy and successful but are not at peace with themselves and feel defective underneath.
mechanism: Cling desperately to persona of being special, superior, perfect, infallible. Seek applause and validation to compensate for deep sense of worthlessness. Mask flaws with success before being discovered.
what_it_maintains: inability to trust or be vulnerable
behavioral_signature: putting self in situations for validation

intervention.verbal: Counterattackers can appear healthy. In fact, some of the people we admire most in society are counterattackers—some movie stars, rock stars, and political leaders. But although they fit in well with society and are successful in the eyes of other people, they are usually not at peace with themselves. They frequently feel defective underneath.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Underneath, counterattackers are usually very fragile. Their superiority is easily deflated. Eventually there is a crack in the armor, and the whole world feels as if it is collapsing.

exact_quotes: They deal with these feelings of inadequacy by putting themselves in situations where they will get the applause of an audience.
Our counterattacks isolate us. We become so invested in appearing to be perfect that we stop caring who gets hurt in the process.
We have met some patients who would rather lose everything—including their marriage, a relationship with someone they love—than risk becoming vulnerable.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment — Emotional Signature
description: The emotional pattern of the Abandonment lifetrap includes obsessive preoccupation with the partner, devastation during breakups, inability to become interested in stable partners, and attraction to commitment-avoidant partners.
mechanism: Fear of abandonment drives obsessive monitoring of relationship security, clinging behaviors, rage at perceived abandonment, and paradoxical attraction to unavailable partners.

intervention.verbal: This obsessiveness is characteristic of the Abandonment lifetrap. During breakups, Danielle occasionally dates other men, but she never becomes interested in anyone other than Robert. The stable, steady types bore her.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your obsessiveness and the way stable partners bore you are signs of your Abandonment lifetrap, not accurate assessments of those partners.

exact_quotes: I just want to stop feeling this way. I can't take it anymore. All I can think about is Robert. I'm obsessed with him. I've got to get him back.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment — Dual Parent Abandonment Message
description: Danielle received the message of abandonment from both parents: her mother abandoned her through alcoholism and unavailability, her father abandoned her literally by leaving the family. This dual abandonment became the central truth of her childhood.
mechanism: Mother's alcoholism meant emotional unavailability and inability to care for children. Father left family when Danielle was seven, then gradually drifted further away as he had children with new wife.
what_it_maintains: core belief that everyone she loves will abandon her
behavioral_signature: standing by couch trying to get drunk mother's attention
inability to make father stay no matter how hard she tried

intervention.verbal: I see myself. I'm standing by the living room couch. I'm trying to get my mother to pay attention to me, but she's drunk. I can't get her to pay attention to me.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your mother abandoned you through alcoholism, and your father abandoned you literally. Their abandonment was the central truth of your childhood.

exact_quotes: Danielle's mother was an alcoholic as far back as she could remember.
When Danielle was seven, her father left the family to marry another woman. He gradually drifted further away from the family as he had children with his new wife.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Self-Blame for Abandonment
description: Children commonly blame themselves when they are abused or neglected. Danielle thought it was her fault that she could not make her father stay and did not have a better mother.
mechanism: Children interpret parental failures as evidence of their own defectiveness rather than recognizing parental limitations.
behavioral_signature: believing 'if I were lovable, they would have stayed/treated me better'

intervention.verbal: It is very common for children to blame themselves when they are abused or neglected. Danielle thought it was her fault that she could not make her father stay and did not have a better mother.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: As a child, you blamed yourself for your parents' failures. This is common but not accurate—their behavior was about them, not about your worth.

exact_quotes: She thought it was her fault that she could not make her father stay and did not have a better mother.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Inner Child Dialogue — Comforting the Abandoned Child
description: Technique where adult self enters childhood imagery to comfort, guide, and offer empathy to the wounded inner child. Involves speaking directly to the child within the image.
mechanism: Adult self provides the nurturing, guidance, and validation the child never received, allowing emotional healing and unfreezing of the wounded child.

intervention.primary: Imagery rescripting with inner child dialogue
intervention.verbal: THERAPIST: I want you to talk to your inner child. Help her. DANIELLE: Well... (pause) I come into the image and take little Danielle onto my lap. I say, 'I'm so sorry this is happening to you. Your parents aren't able to be there for you the way you need. But I will be here for you. I will help you get through this and make sure you come out all right.'
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your inner child is frozen. We want to bring it back to life, where growth and change are possible. We want this child to heal.

exact_quotes: Giving comfort to your inner child, offering guidance and advice, and empathizing with how the child feels are some of the things we will ask you to do.
Even though these exercises may seem silly or uncomfortable to you at first, we have found that most people benefit enormously from them.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Childhood Memory Imagery — Accessing Early Memories
description: Technique to access childhood feelings by closing eyes and letting images come spontaneously. Patient allows memories to rise naturally rather than forcing them, and enters each image as deeply as possible.
mechanism: Bypasses blocking mechanisms to reconnect with childhood self and the emotions connected with the lifetrap.
what_activates_it: therapeutic direction to close eyes and allow images

intervention.verbal: To feel your lifetrap, you will need to remember your childhood. We will ask you to close your eyes and let the images come. Do not force the images—just let one rise to the top of your mind. Get into each one as deeply as you can. Try to picture these early memories as vividly as possible. If you do this a few times, you will begin to remember what you felt as a child. You will feel the pain or emotions connected with your lifetrap.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: We all have mechanisms for blocking pain. Unfortunately, by blocking the pain, we cannot get fully in touch with our lifetraps. This imagery helps you reconnect with what you felt as a child.

exact_quotes: This kind of imagery is painful. If you feel completely overwhelmed or frightened by the experience, that is a sign you need therapy. Your childhood was so painful that you should not remember it alone. You need a guide, an ally. A therapist can be this for you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Counterattack — General Pattern
description: A coping style where the person overcompensates for lifetrap pain by blaming others, demanding impossibly high standards, seeking constant excitement, or trying to impress people rather than acknowledging vulnerabilities.
mechanism: The person avoids feeling the pain of their flaws and childhood deprivation by projecting criticism outward, maintaining superiority, and refusing to acknowledge their own role in problems.
what_activates_it: situations that threaten to expose vulnerability
behavioral_signature: criticizing partners for not meeting impossibly high standards

intervention.verbal: Make a list of all the choices you have regretted in your life. What if they were your fault? How would you feel? What if the criticisms others have of you have some validity? What would that mean about you? Try to feel the pain of your flaws. Try to acknowledge the pain of your childhood—what you wanted but did not get.
intervention.behavioral: Gradually start working less or making a little less money. Try to refrain from deliberately impressing others. Feel what it is like to be the same as everyone else rather than special or superior.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Unless you can allow these feelings in, you will not make yourself vulnerable enough to change.

exact_quotes: Jed, the man who moved from woman to woman, had to struggle hard to stop Counterattacking. His criticalness of women, his demand that they meet his impossibly high standards for beauty and status, and his need for constant excitement were ways he made himself feel better about his loneliness.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Escape — General Pattern
description: A coping style where the person avoids experiencing their lifetrap by not allowing themselves to think about problems, cutting off feelings, or numbing through substances or behaviors.
mechanism: The person protects themselves from intense anxiety and pain by avoiding thoughts about problems, past, family, or life patterns, and by dulling feelings through drinking, drugs, or other escape behaviors.
what_activates_it: intimate situations that trigger the lifetrap
behavioral_signature: obsessing about other things to avoid core issues

intervention.verbal: You must allow yourself to think through your problems and feel your childhood pain before you can change. Force yourself to try some of the childhood memory exercises. Write down some criticisms of your parents or some of your flaws and vulnerabilities. Keep doing this every day.
intervention.behavioral: Stop escaping through drinking, overeating, using drugs, overworking, etc., for a few days. Keep a diary in which you write down what you feel. Try some of the imagery exercises during these days. Go to a twelve-step program.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: To stop avoiding means to lay yourself open to intense anxiety and pain, but it takes motivation to overcome Escape as a coping style. You have to see the rest of your life before you—either stuck in the lifetrap or finally free of it.

exact_quotes: Patrick had to stop avoiding the problems in his life by obsessing about Francine. Madeline had to stop escaping the pain that sexual intimacy evoked for her. Heather had to stop avoiding activities that she considered dangerous. Jed had to stop escaping from emotional closeness. And Carlton had to stop running away from his own needs and preferences.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Escape Coping — Advantages and Disadvantages List
description: A written exercise where patients list the pros and cons of avoiding their feelings, to be reread daily as a motivational tool for staying engaged with emotional work.
mechanism: By explicitly articulating both the short-term benefits and long-term costs of escape, patients build cognitive motivation to tolerate discomfort during therapeutic work.
what_activates_it: urges to use substances or distractions
behavioral_signature: conscious choice to engage rather than avoid

intervention.verbal: Make a list of the advantages and disadvantages of avoiding your feelings. Reread the list every day to remind yourself of why you are doing this.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This list helps you remember why you chose to face your feelings instead of running from them, especially when the urge to escape is strong.

exact_quotes: Make a list of the advantages and disadvantages of avoiding your feelings. Reread the list every day to remind yourself of why you are doing this.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Surrender — Continuing to Believe the Lifetrap is True
description: A form of surrender where the person accepts the lifetrap as valid on a rational level, failing to challenge or disprove it, thereby not attempting change.
mechanism: When a person accepts their lifetrap as fundamentally true, they maintain hypervigilance, continue to expect negative outcomes, and do not engage in experiments that could disconfirm the belief.
what_activates_it: situations that seem to confirm the lifetrap
fear of being wrong about the danger
behavioral_signature: assigning high probabilities to catastrophic outcomes

intervention.verbal: Return to the exercises on disproving your lifetrap. Go through them again. Make a commitment to fighting your beliefs. Look very carefully through your life for any evidence that challenges your lifetrap. Were there extenuating circumstances that invalidate the lifetrap? Were you abused? Did you avoid trying harder because you were afraid of rejection and failure? Did you choose friends, lovers, bosses, etc., who confirmed your lifetrap? Try to play devil's advocate in arguing against your lifetrap.
intervention.behavioral: Education about realistic appraisals, working continually to lower the odds assigned to danger, taking reasonable precautions, learning relaxation techniques.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: A lifetrap does not give way all at once. Rather, you must continually chip away at it, bit by bit, gradually weakening its pull.

exact_quotes: For a long time Heather felt extremely anxious in the situations she was entering. This was because she still believed the situations were dangerous and that catastrophe could strike at any moment. She maintained a hypervigilant state, ever alert for possible harm.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Flashcard — Daily Review Protocol
description: Instructions for using schema flashcards as a daily practice to counter lifetrap beliefs and maintain progress.
mechanism: Regular exposure to corrective cognitive content gradually weakens the automatic activation of lifetrap beliefs and strengthens the healthy voice.

intervention.verbal: Write your flashcard and read it several times a day. Set aside a few minutes every day to review your progress. Reread your flashcards. Was your lifetrap activated today? Did you do anything to surrender to your pattern? Push yourself to think, feel, or act differently each day.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your healthy side will become stronger and stronger, and your lifetrap side will become weaker. Be patient. Your feelings will change.

exact_quotes: Write your flashcard and read it several times a day.
Set aside a few minutes every day to review your progress. Reread your flashcards. Was your lifetrap activated today?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Surrender — Starting with Task Too Difficult
description: A form of surrender where the person attempts change at too high a difficulty level, fails, and confirms the lifetrap through repeated unsuccessful attempts.
mechanism: When tasks exceed current capacity, failure reinforces the sense of helplessness and confirms lifetrap beliefs, leading to abandonment of change efforts.

intervention.verbal: Break your plan into smaller steps. Start with the easier steps. Slowly build a sense of mastery. Work your way up to the difficult steps.
intervention.behavioral: Start with strangers (salespeople, waitresses), gradually move to acquaintances and coworkers, then focus on intimate relationships.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This is one of the most important rules: Always attempt manageable tasks. Trying to confront your father right off the bat was setting yourself up for failure.

exact_quotes: When Carlton first started doing assertiveness exercises, he began by attempting to be assertive with his father. This was a mistake: it was too hard. Carlton would become very fearful and unable to express himself. Trying to confront his father right off the bat was setting himself up for failure.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Head-Gut Split — Knowing vs. Feeling
description: A common emotional pattern where patients recognize their lifetrap is irrational on a cognitive level but continue to feel its truth emotionally, creating a disconnect between insight and felt experience.
mechanism: Early emotional learning is stored differently than cognitive insight; intellectual understanding does not automatically change the emotional schemas formed in childhood.
what_activates_it: partner behavior that resembles early caregiver patterns
what_it_maintains: behavioral patterns driven by feeling rather than cognition
behavioral_signature: stating 'I know it's not true but I still feel it'
clinging behaviors despite knowing they push people away
not allowing partner space despite intellectual understanding

intervention.verbal: Remind yourself that insight comes quickly, but change comes slowly. Your healthy side will become stronger and stronger, and your lifetrap side will become weaker. Be patient. Your feelings will change.
intervention.behavioral: Write dialogues between your healthy side and your lifetrap. Get angry at your lifetrap. Cry about the way you were treated as a child. Let yourself feel the injustice. Work harder to change the behaviors that reinforce your lifetrap.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You can speed up the process by doing more experiential exercises and by working harder to change old patterns. As you change old patterns, you will see new evidence that contradicts your lifetrap. This new evidence will have a powerful effect on the way that you feel.

exact_quotes: Most patients tell us that for many months they still feel deep down that the lifetrap is true, regardless of what logic and evidence tell them.
Even when he was in a healthy relationship with a stable woman who could be there for him, he would still feel like she was going to abandon him.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Written Dialogue Between Healthy Side and Lifetrap
description: An experiential technique where patients write out a dialogue between their healthy voice and their lifetrap voice to strengthen the healthy perspective and process emotions.
mechanism: Externalizing the internal conflict through written dialogue helps patients identify with their healthy side, access suppressed emotions, and weaken the lifetrap's hold.
what_activates_it: gap between intellectual understanding and emotional experience

intervention.verbal: Write dialogues between your healthy side and your lifetrap. Get angry at your lifetrap. Cry about the way you were treated as a child. Let yourself feel the injustice.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This exercise helps you speed up the process of emotional change by letting you express feelings that have been held back and by strengthening the part of you that knows the lifetrap isn't true.

exact_quotes: Write dialogues between your healthy side and your lifetrap. Get angry at your lifetrap. Cry about the way you were treated as a child. Let yourself feel the injustice.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Surrender — Subjugation Missing Element (Not Communicating Needs)
description: A specific form of surrender in Subjugation where the person becomes assertive about anger and boundaries but fails to communicate their positive needs and preferences, remaining unfulfilled.
mechanism: Partial assertion without communicating desires keeps the person in a subjugated position because others cannot meet needs they don't know about, perpetuating anger and resentment.
what_activates_it: opportunities to ask for what one wants
behavioral_signature: asking others to change disturbing behavior
BUT not telling others what is wanted

intervention.verbal: Review the list of behaviors that are typical of your lifetrap more carefully. Did you overlook part of the pattern that really does apply to you? Ask someone you trust to review your lifetrap and plan with you. Perhaps this person will notice something in your life pattern that you have missed.
intervention.behavioral: Practice communicating what you want—your opinions and preferences—to other people.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Communicating your needs is an important element. You were not telling other people what you wanted from them, and then were angry you were not getting it.

exact_quotes: He was not communicating what he wanted—his opinions and preferences. He was not telling other people what he wanted from them, and then was angry he was not getting it. Communicating his needs was an important element for Carlton.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment — 'I will be left alone forever'
description: The core childhood message internalized by those with Abandonment lifetrap: that they will lose the people they love and be left emotionally isolated forever, with the loss being permanent.
mechanism: Early experiences of loss or instability create a fundamental belief that relationships are doomed, that people will leave, and that the child will ultimately end up completely alone.
behavioral_signature: standing at window waiting for person to return
inability to accept that person is gone
feeling of aloneness that doesn't go away

intervention.verbal: You have a fundamental belief that you will lose the people you love and be left emotionally isolated forever. Whether people will die, send you away, or leave you, somehow you feel that you will be left alone. You expect to be abandoned, and you expect it to last forever.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding this core message helps you see where your intense reactions come from and why brief separations feel so devastating.

exact_quotes: I'm all alone
No one is there for me
In the end, I will be alone

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment Emotional Cycle — Anxiety, Grief, and Anger
description: People with the Abandonment lifetrap experience an intense emotional cycle when separation occurs. The anxiety, grief, and anger are so intense they cannot soothe themselves and feel totally disconnected and desperate.
mechanism: When separation occurs, the person experiences overwhelming anxiety about loss, followed by deep grief, then anger at being abandoned. These emotions cycle repeatedly and the person cannot self-soothe.
behavioral_signature: inability to focus on anything else

intervention.verbal: Allow yourself to experience all the feelings in the cycle. Become aware of the cycle whenever you ride it.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: By recognizing the cycle when it happens, you can begin to understand your reactions and eventually gain more control over them.

exact_quotes: The anxiety, grief, and anger that they feel are so intense that they are unable to soothe themselves, and they feel totally disconnected and desperate. They can distract themselves from the feeling for only a short time.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment Core Message — You Will Be Left Alone Forever
description: The child internalizes a deep belief that connections are unstable and people will leave. The core message is that being alone is inevitable and catastrophic.
mechanism: Through experiences of loss, parental instability, or unpredictable caregiving, the child learns that emotional connections cannot be trusted to last.
behavioral_signature: keeping parent in view at all times

intervention.verbal: Consider whether you have a biological predisposition to develop the lifetrap. When you have some quiet, peaceful time, let images of your childhood float to the top of your mind.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding where your feelings come from helps you see them more clearly and begin to challenge their validity in your current life.

exact_quotes: To the child, it can seem like living in a world of strangers.
She feels that if her connection to the loved person were lost, she would be plunged into utter aloneness.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Imagery — Falling Into the Dark Cellar of Aloneness
description: A guided imagery technique where the patient closes their eyes and generates an image of how they feel during abandonment triggers. This reveals the core emotional experience of the lifetrap.
mechanism: By accessing spontaneous imagery, the patient connects with the raw emotional experience of abandonment, which can then be processed and linked to childhood origins.

intervention.verbal: Close your eyes and give me an image of how you felt. [Patient responds: 'I see myself falling backwards. It's like falling backwards into this dark cellar, where I'll be all alone forever. Greg is pushing me backwards into the cellar, and the door is going to shut, and I'm going to be all alone.'] What are you feeling? [Patient: 'Terrified.']
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Images help you access feelings that are hard to put into words. They show us the emotional truth of your experience.

exact_quotes: I see myself falling backwards. It's like falling backwards into this dark cellar, where I'll be all alone forever.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Imagery — Linking Present Triggers to Childhood Origins
description: A guided imagery technique that begins with a current abandonment trigger and traces back to the earliest memory of similar feelings. This forges emotional links between present reactions and childhood experiences.
mechanism: Starting with current feelings of abandonment, the patient is guided to access early memories where they first experienced these feelings, creating insight into the origins of their lifetrap.
what_activates_it: intense emotional reaction to relationship threat

intervention.verbal: Close your eyes and get an image of [partner]. What do you see? [Patient describes current image] What are you feeling? [Patient describes feeling] Give me an image of when you felt this way before, as far back as you can. [Patient accesses childhood memory]
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Forge these links between the present and the past with imagery. Try to remember the beginnings of your feelings of abandonment.

exact_quotes: Give me an image of when you felt this way before, as far back as you can.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Socratic Question — Getting an Image of Current Feelings
description: A therapist question designed to elicit spontaneous imagery that captures the emotional experience of abandonment.
mechanism: By asking for an image rather than a verbal description, the question bypasses intellectual defenses and accesses the raw emotional experience.

intervention.verbal: Close your eyes and give me an image of how you felt.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Images often capture feelings more accurately than words.

exact_quotes: Close your eyes and give me an image of how you felt.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Socratic Question — Earliest Memory of This Feeling
description: A therapist question that prompts the patient to trace current feelings back to their earliest childhood occurrence.
mechanism: This question bridges present emotional reactions to childhood origins, creating insight into the developmental roots of the lifetrap.

intervention.verbal: Give me an image of when you felt this way before, as far back as you can.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding where these feelings first came from helps you see why they are so powerful now.

exact_quotes: Give me an image of when you felt this way before, as far back as you can.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment — Escape (Avoiding Intimate Relationships)
description: Some people with the Abandonment lifetrap cope by avoiding intimate relationships altogether. They would rather remain alone than go through the process of loss again.
mechanism: By avoiding intimate connections entirely, the person prevents the possibility of experiencing the devastating pain of abandonment again. This provides short-term relief but maintains loneliness.

intervention.verbal: You avoid intimate relationships even with appropriate partners because you are afraid of losing the person or getting too close and being hurt.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Avoiding relationships may protect you from pain, but it also prevents you from having the stable connection you need.

exact_quotes: I just couldn't keep going through it. It was too painful. I could never find someone who would be there for me. I was better off alone. At least I had some peace.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment — Counterattack (Detachment)
description: Detachment is the Counterattack for Abandonment. When detached, the person denies their need for connection with a defiant 'I don't need you.' There is usually anger mixed in, and it is partly punitive.
mechanism: By emotionally withdrawing and becoming cold, the person denies their vulnerability and punishes their partner for not meeting their needs. This provides temporary protection but creates emotional numbness.

intervention.verbal: You sometimes detach, leave, or withdraw to punish your partner for leaving you alone.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Although detachment helps you cope with feelings of abandonment, you pay a price: you give up your feelings and exist in a chilly emotional numbness.

exact_quotes: Detachment is the Counterattack for Abandonment. When you are detached, you are denying the need for connection. It is a defiant, 'I don't need you.' There is usually some anger mixed in with your detachment, and it is partly punitive. You punish your partner for withdrawing from you, for not giving you what you need.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment — Surrender (Clinging and Obsessive Attachment)
description: Early in relationships, the person becomes excessively clingy with a desperate quality. Their whole life becomes obsessed with keeping the connection because it feels so important. Their absorption is total.
mechanism: Clinging reinforces the lifetrap because it reinforces the idea that the person is going to lose their partner. It keeps the possibility of abandonment alive in the relationship.
behavioral_signature: constant need to be near partner

intervention.verbal: Do not cling, become jealous, or overreact to the normal separations of a healthy relationship.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Clinging actually reinforces your fear that you will lose the person. It keeps the feeling of potential abandonment constantly alive.

exact_quotes: Her connection with her boyfriend never feels strong enough. She feels lonely and lost, so she pours her whole life into the relationship. Her absorption is total. As she says, she becomes obsessed, forgetting about the outside world. All her energy becomes invested in keeping the connection because it is so important.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment — Surrender (Choosing Unstable Partners)
description: The person is attracted to partners who present some hope for stability but not complete hope—a mixture of hope and doubt. Living in an unstable relationship feels comfortable and familiar.
mechanism: The instability keeps activating the lifetrap, generating chemistry and passionate feelings. Choosing unavailable partners ensures continued reenactment of childhood abandonment.
behavioral_signature: attraction to married or unavailable partners

intervention.verbal: Avoid uncommitted, unstable, or ambivalent partners even though they generate high chemistry. Remember that we are not saying that you should go out with people you find unattractive, but an intense sexual attraction may be a sign that your partner is triggering your Abandonment lifetrap.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You are attracted most to partners who show some commitment but not enough to feel secure. This keeps your lifetrap active and your relationships turbulent.

exact_quotes: You are attracted most to partners who show some degree of commitment and connection, but not so much that you are absolutely sure that they will stay. Living in an unstable love relationship feels comfortable and familiar to you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment Emotional Pattern — Running From Disconnection
description: The person constantly tries to distract themselves from the underlying feeling of being disconnected. From the outside they may appear to be enjoying solitude, but internally they feel like someone running to escape bad feelings.
mechanism: Distraction provides temporary relief but the feeling of disconnection lurks in the background waiting to engulf them. Everyone has a limit to how long they can distract before the feelings catch up.
what_it_maintains: return of intense feelings when distraction fails
behavioral_signature: appearing calm externally while anxious internally

intervention.verbal: It is important for you to start spending time alone if you are not doing so. Choose to spend time alone instead of running away.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: If you keep running from these feelings, they never go away. By choosing to spend time alone and face the feelings, you can eventually pass through the fears into a space of peace.

exact_quotes: I was gardening, trying to forget about Kurt being gone... But I wasn't enjoying myself. I felt more like someone who was running and running, and when I was too tired to run anymore, the bad feelings would catch up with me again.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment Emotional Pattern — Roller Coaster Relationships
description: Romantic relationships are seldom calm and steady but feel like roller coaster rides. The person experiences the relationship as perpetually on the brink of catastrophe.
mechanism: Hypersensitivity to any sign of withdrawal leads to constant emotional reactivity. Minor disruptions feel like major threats, creating cycles of distress and relief.
behavioral_signature: interpreting minor signs as evidence relationship will end

intervention.verbal: You overreact to minor things your partner says or does, and interpret them as signs that he/she wants to leave you.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your partner almost invariably feels you are overreacting. Learning to recognize when you are doing this can help you respond more proportionately.

exact_quotes: Your romantic relationships are seldom calm and steady. Rather, they often feel like roller coaster rides. This is because you experience the relationship as perpetually on the brink of catastrophe.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment Flashcard — Managing Withdrawal Triggers
description: A complete flashcard designed to help Lindsay manage her abandonment lifetrap when triggered by perceived withdrawal from her partner Richard. The flashcard addresses emotional regulation, cognitive reframing, and behavioral redirection.
mechanism: Provides structured self-talk that interrupts automatic lifetrap activation, validates current feelings while offering reality testing, and redirects attention to adaptive coping behaviors

intervention.verbal: Right now I feel devastated because Richard is withdrawing from me, and I am about to become angry and needy. However, I know that this is my Abandonment lifetrap, and that my lifetrap is triggered by just the slightest evidence of withdrawal. I need to remember that people in good relationships withdraw, and that withdrawal is part of the natural rhythm of good relationships. If I start behaving in an angry and clingy way, I will push Richard even further away. Richard has a right to pull away at times. What I should do instead is work with my thoughts to try to take a longer view of the relationship as a whole. My feelings are way out of proportion to reality. I can tolerate my feelings and remember that in the big picture Richard and I are still connected, and the relationship is good. To best help myself, I should turn my attention to my own life, and ways of developing myself. The better able I am to be on my own, the better I will be in relationships.
intervention.behavioral: Use flashcard each time abandonment lifetrap is triggered
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Using a flashcard each time your lifetrap is triggered chips away at the lifetrap, weakening it over time

exact_quotes: Using a flashcard each time your lifetrap is triggered chips away at the lifetrap, weakening it.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment — Surrender (Clinging and Accusations)
description: A surrender response to the abandonment lifetrap characterized by clinging behavior and making accusations of infidelity when perceiving partner withdrawal.
mechanism: The person gives in to abandonment fears by becoming angry and needy, making accusations, which paradoxically pushes the partner away and confirms abandonment expectations

intervention.verbal: If I start behaving in an angry and clingy way, I will push Richard even further away
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Clinging and accusations push partners away, creating the very abandonment you fear

exact_quotes: Lindsay felt less vulnerable, and she did not cling as much nor make her usual accusations of infidelity.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Abandonment — Counterattack (Giving Self Entirely Away)
description: A maladaptive coping pattern where the person overcompensates for abandonment fears by giving themselves entirely to the partner, relinquishing all personal power in an attempt to prevent loss.
mechanism: By giving everything to the other person, losing that person becomes catastrophic, intensifying abandonment fears and dependence

intervention.primary: Learning to hold on to power in relationships
intervention.verbal: I always thought the whole point of a relationship was to hold on to the other person. I would do anything to keep her. But now I see that I can let people go and survive.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: If you give everything to the other person, it is a catastrophe to lose that person

exact_quotes: Patrick has always given himself entirely away, so that he had nothing and the other person had everything. If you give everything to the other person, it is a catastrophe to lose that person.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust/Abuse — Volatile Emotional Signature
description: The characteristic emotional experience of the mistrust and abuse lifetrap involving intense feelings of pain, fear, rage, and grief that simmer near the surface and can suddenly burst forth.
mechanism: Abuse creates a complex mixture of intense emotions that remain close to the surface, leading to sudden emotional outbursts that surprise others

intervention.verbal: Abuse is a complex mixture of feelings—pain, fear, rage, and grief. The feelings are intense, and they simmer near the surface.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding your intense emotional reactions helps you recognize when your lifetrap is triggered

exact_quotes: You may have volatile moods. You suddenly become very upset—either crying or enraged. It often surprises other people.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust/Abuse — Escape (Dissociation)
description: An escape response to abuse trauma where the person psychologically removes themselves from the situation by dissociating, appearing spaced out, feeling things are unreal, or becoming emotionally numb.
mechanism: Dissociation developed as a psychological escape from abuse, allowing the child to remove themselves emotionally and survive overwhelming experiences
what_activates_it: topics person doesn't want to address

intervention.verbal: When Frank doesn't want to talk about something, it's like he can click off. Like snap, he's gone. I don't even exist.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Dissociating was an adaptive response as a child that helped you survive, but it can interfere with connection now

exact_quotes: At other times you may be spaced out—what we call dissociated. You seem to be somewhere else. Things seem unreal to you. Your emotions are numb. This is a habit you developed as a kind of psychological escape from the abuse.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust/Abuse — Counterattack (Becoming the Abuser)
description: A counterattack response where the abused person copes by imitating the behavior of their abuser, abusing others to feel more powerful and avoid feeling like the victim.
mechanism: The child imitates abuser behavior as a way to feel more powerful, perpetuating the chain of abuse across generations

intervention.verbal: I used to beat up on my younger brother. Man, I feel so bad about it now. I would beat up on him just like my father beat up on me.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Most victims of child abuse do not grow up to become child abusers - you can break the chain

exact_quotes: One of the most common Counterattacks for the Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap is to abuse somebody else. This is what perpetuates the chain of abuse.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust/Abuse — Counterattack (Explosive Anger)
description: A counterattack response where the person reacts to perceived abuse or betrayal with explosive anger, attacking preemptively to avoid being hurt.
mechanism: Expecting abuse triggers defensive rage, leading to attacking others before they can hurt you

intervention.verbal: At one point he grabbed me and started shaking me. He was shouting at the top of his lungs. I felt really scared of him.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your anger comes from expecting abuse, but it frightens the people who love you

exact_quotes: Where was I, who was I with, what was I doing.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust/Abuse — Escape (Promiscuity and Substances)
description: An escape response where the abuse survivor avoids emotional intimacy through promiscuous sex with strangers and substance use to numb feelings.
mechanism: Alcohol and casual sex allow avoidance of genuine intimacy and vulnerability while still meeting needs for connection, numbing painful abuse memories

intervention.verbal: In my early twenties I used to drink a lot and have sex with guys I barely knew. I was really promiscuous. But two years ago I stopped drinking. I haven't had a boyfriend since.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Substances and casual encounters help avoid real intimacy and the vulnerability it requires

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust/Abuse — Surrender (Allowing Mistreatment)
description: A surrender response where the person allows continued mistreatment in relationships, believing it is all they deserve or fearing being alone.
mechanism: Low self-worth from abuse leads to accepting mistreatment as deserved, and fear of abandonment makes any connection seem better than none

intervention.verbal: These were the guys I fell in love with. Besides, it was better than being alone.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: At least alone you have the chance to heal and rebuild your sense of self-esteem, to find a partner who will treat you differently

exact_quotes: We do not agree that it is better than being alone. At least alone you have the chance to heal and rebuild your sense of self-esteem, to find a partner who will treat you differently.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You Deserve the Abuse Because You Are Bad
description: The internalized childhood belief that abuse occurs because the child is inherently bad, worthless, or deserving of punishment. The abuser blames the child, who accepts that blame.
mechanism: The child lacks the cognitive capacity to blame the adult and instead blames self, creating deep feelings of defectiveness that persist into adulthood

intervention.verbal: At the time I thought it was happening because I was so bad. I was clumsy and would get into trouble. My father used to tell me I would rot in hell. I believed him. I just thought it was happening because I was such a lousy person.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: No matter how you feel, you were innocent. Your innocence and your trust were betrayed.

exact_quotes: In every instance of abuse we have encountered as therapists, the abuser makes the child feel worthless. The abuser blames the child, and the child accepts that blame.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Sexual Abuse — Your Innocence and Trust Were Betrayed
description: The core message for sexual abuse survivors that their innocence was violated and their natural trust in caregivers was betrayed, regardless of any participation, response, or secrecy.
mechanism: Sexual abuse violates both body and spirit, creating guilt and shame that must be reframed as betrayal of trust by the adult, not fault of the child

intervention.verbal: Sexual abuse is a violation of your spirit as well as your body. No matter how you feel, you were innocent. Your innocence and your trust were betrayed.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The fact that you were a child absolves you. Your family was supposed to be protecting you.

exact_quotes: Sexual abuse is a violation of your spirit as well as your body. No matter how you feel, you were innocent. Your innocence and your trust were betrayed.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Nobody Cared Enough to Protect Me
description: The painful internalized message that both the abuser and the non-abusing parent failed to protect the child, indicating the child was not valued or loved enough to be kept safe.
mechanism: The dual failure of one parent abusing and the other failing to prevent it creates deep feelings of worthlessness and unimportance

intervention.primary: Validating the pain of not being protected
intervention.verbal: They didn't care enough what happened to me. Neither one of them. They were my mother and my stepfather, and they didn't care what happened to me.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The feeling of not being protected is part of most forms of abuse. They both let you down.

exact_quotes: The feeling of not being protected is part of most forms of abuse. One parent abused you, and the other failed to prevent or stop it. They both let you down.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust and Abuse — Surrender
description: The surrender response to the Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap involves allowing oneself to remain in abusive relationships, tolerating mistreatment, and sending messages that one is not worth treating well.
mechanism: The person lowers their value in relationships by giving in too easily, putting themselves down, and allowing partners to take advantage of them, unconsciously recreating the childhood dynamic of abuse.
what_it_maintains: the belief that abuse is normal in relationships
behavioral_signature: tolerating physical, verbal, or sexual abuse
giving in to whatever partner wants

intervention.primary: Stop tolerating abuse and assert boundaries
intervention.verbal: Stop tolerating abuse in your current relationships. Look at your current relationships. Write down the ways in which you still permit yourself to be abused—hit, manipulated, put down, humiliated, raped. This all must stop. You cannot heal while your lifetrap is being reinforced.
intervention.behavioral: Document abuse, assert rights, leave if partner won't change
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You cannot heal while continuing to allow abuse. You need to protect yourself now as the adults in your childhood failed to do.

exact_quotes: You do this by lowering your value in the relationship: you give in too easily to whatever the person wants, put yourself down, allow your partner to take advantage of you, send out messages that you are not worth treating well.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust and Abuse — Escape
description: The escape response to the Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap involves avoiding intimate relationships altogether based on the belief that all potential partners will inevitably hurt or betray you.
mechanism: The person over-generalizes from childhood abuse, expecting all people to be like their abusive family members. Rather than risk another painful relationship, they avoid relationships entirely.
what_it_maintains: the belief that no one can be trusted
behavioral_signature: refusing to trust even kind people

intervention.verbal: Go through a period of just dating first. Work your way into relationships slowly. Keep a feeling of control. As you get into relationships, make sure you stand up for your rights.
intervention.behavioral: Date casually first, evaluate evidence of trustworthiness objectively
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: There are many people in the world who are kinder than the people in your family. You are over-generalizing when you expect everyone to hurt you.

exact_quotes: Madeline believed that the best she could hope for was another painful relationship. With that logic, it made sense to avoid relationships altogether. This is an example of Escape as a coping style.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mistrust and Abuse — Counterattack
description: The counterattack response to the Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap involves adopting the strategy that 'the best defense is a good offense' by attacking partners preemptively before they can attack you.
mechanism: Expecting attack from others, the person strikes first. They become aggressive, accusatory, or abusive themselves, not noticing they are the only one attacking. This often provokes retaliation and pushes partners away.
what_activates_it: partner's neutral behaviors misinterpreted as threats

intervention.primary: Stop abusive behavior and make amends
intervention.verbal: If you have been abusing someone you love, stop right now. If you cannot stop, get help right away. We want you to make amends to the people you have hurt. Tell them you realize you have been wrong, and ask their forgiveness.
intervention.behavioral: Identify aggressive behaviors, stop immediately, apologize and outline changes
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your outbursts of anger bring about the very situation you fear—driving people away or making them retaliate. Stopping will help you more than continuing the cycle.

exact_quotes: This is when you believe the saying, 'The best defense is a good offense.' Since you expect the other person to attack, you make sure you attack first. You do not notice that time passes and you are the only one attacking.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Core Message: People Cannot Be Trusted
description: The internalized belief from childhood abuse that all people are fundamentally untrustworthy, have hidden motives, and secretly enjoy causing suffering.
mechanism: Pervasive abuse shapes the child's entire worldview so that mistrust becomes the basic stance toward all relationships. This becomes the emotional tone surrounding any intimacy.

intervention.verbal: For each person in your life that is not obviously abusive, write down all the evidence that he/she can be trusted. Next write down all the evidence that he/she cannot be trusted. If you do not really have much evidence of bad treatment, try trusting more.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your basic sense that people want to hurt you comes from your childhood, not from objective evidence about the people currently in your life.

exact_quotes: Depending upon the pervasiveness of your abuse, your whole world view may be based upon the idea that people cannot be trusted. Your basic sense of people is that they are out to hurt you and secretly enjoy your suffering.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Core Message: You Are Damaged Goods
description: The internalized message from abuse that the victim is dirty, defective, and only worthy of being used and discarded, leading to self-blame for the abuse.
mechanism: Abusers devalue their victims, and the child internalizes this devaluation as truth about themselves rather than recognizing it as the abuser's distortion.
what_activates_it: being valued or wanted by others

intervention.verbal: Stop blaming yourself. You did not deserve the abuse. No matter what you were made to feel, the abuse did not happen because you were bad. That was just a convenient excuse. Victimizers always devalue their victims. Awake from your feelings of defectiveness.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: It was your abuser who was dirty, not you. The devaluation you feel was put there by someone who needed to justify their own cruel behavior.

exact_quotes: I feel dirty anyway, like I'm damaged goods. Who could really want me, except for a one-night stand?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Core Message: Men/Women Want to Use You
description: The internalized belief that all members of a gender are fundamentally exploitative, only pretending to care in order to get what they want before discarding you.
mechanism: Abuse by a specific person generalizes to an entire gender, creating a deep conviction that genuine care from that gender is impossible.
behavioral_signature: testing potential partners to expose hidden motives

intervention.verbal: The truth is that there are many people in the world who are kinder than the people in your family. You expect the whole world to be like your family, and you are wrong. You are over-generalizing.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your belief about all men/women comes from specific abusers in your past. Not everyone is like them.

exact_quotes: Deep inside they all want to use you and then throw you away. They just pretend to care to get what they want.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Challenging Mistrust Evidence
description: A Socratic question designed to help the patient evaluate whether their partner has actually set out to hurt them, separating past abuse from current relationship dynamics.
mechanism: By asking for specific evidence of deliberate harm, the therapist helps the patient distinguish between legitimate concerns and lifetrap-driven distortions.

intervention.verbal: Can you name times she deliberately set out to hurt you?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Let's examine the actual evidence rather than the feelings.

exact_quotes: THERAPIST: Can you name times she deliberately set out to hurt you?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Following the Sequence
description: Simple follow-up questions to help patients trace the consequences of their behaviors in relationships.
mechanism: By tracking what happened next, patients can see how their own behaviors may contribute to relationship problems.

intervention.verbal: What happened?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Let's trace what actually occurred.

exact_quotes: THERAPIST: What happened?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Growing Up in the Image
description: An imagery technique where the patient is instructed to age themselves in a childhood abuse memory until they are adult-sized, enabling them to confront their abuser from a position of power.
mechanism: By becoming adult-sized in the image, the patient can access their adult power and resources to stand up to the abuser, breaking the pattern of childhood helplessness.
what_activates_it: patient feeling too helpless or frightened to confront abuser in imagery

intervention.verbal: Close your eyes and give us an image from your childhood. [After image established] I want you to freeze the image for a moment. Now turn to your father and tell him what he's doing that's wrong. [If patient says they can't] I understand. You're not powerful enough. Let's do something to help you. I want you to grow up in the image, until you are as big as you are now. Now tell him. Tell him what he's doing wrong. You can hit the couch with your fists as you talk.
intervention.behavioral: Patient can punch pillows or telephone books while expressing anger
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This exercise is about empowering yourself. It will help free you from the domination of your abuser. At some level you are still operating in the world as a frightened child. We want you to claim the power of an adult.

exact_quotes: I want you to grow up in the image, until you are as big as you are now.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Rescuing the Inner Child from Abuse
description: An imagery technique where the adult patient enters a childhood abuse memory and physically removes the child from the abusive situation, providing comfort and protection.
mechanism: The patient's healthy adult self provides the protection and nurturing that was missing in childhood, helping to heal the wounded inner child and redirect blame away from self.

intervention.verbal: I want you to bring yourself into the image as an adult, and help the child. [Patient responds:] I bring myself in. The child Madeline is lying on the couch with him. Her eyes look dead. I pick her up and carry her out of the room. I take her outside, far away. I sit down with her in my arms, and I just rock her.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Find the good child within you. Feel sympathy for this wounded child. You did the best you could under the circumstances.

exact_quotes: I pick her up and carry her out of the room. I take her outside, far away. I sit down with her in my arms, and I just rock her.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Collapse with Abuser
description: The characteristic emotional response when someone with the Mistrust and Abuse lifetrap encounters their abuser, characterized by immediate regression to feeling small, helpless, and worthless.
mechanism: The abuser's presence or criticism triggers automatic emotional regression, collapsing the person's adult functioning and reinstating childhood feelings of helplessness and defectiveness.
behavioral_signature: inability to respond or defend oneself

intervention.primary: Reduce or stop contact with abuser
intervention.verbal: Your abuser is a powerful reinforcer of your lifetrap. He or she gives you all the wrong messages—that you are helpless, a victim, defective, at fault.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: It is hard to heal in that toxic environment. At least temporarily, you may need distance from your abuser.

exact_quotes: When Adrienne and I had dinner with my parents, I felt like such a jerk. Right away. We sat down at the table, and I immediately knocked over my glass of water. My pants got soaked. My father started calling me spastic and laughing at me. I felt like a worm.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Sexual Shutdown After Abuse
description: The emotional disconnection and deadness experienced during sex by survivors of sexual abuse, often accompanied by anger and disturbing fantasies.
mechanism: Sexual abuse damages the ability to experience sexuality as positive, leading to emotional shutdown during intimate moments as a protective response.
behavioral_signature: anger during or after sexual activity

intervention.verbal: If you were sexually abused, the damage to your sexuality is bound to be an issue in romantic relationships. You are prone to feeling angry or emotionally dead during sex.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The joy of your sexuality was taken from you. With therapy, you can reclaim what is rightfully yours.

exact_quotes: Sometimes I think I wouldn't mind if sex weren't a part of life. I don't look forward to sleeping with men again. I just feel so shut-down during sex it makes me upset.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Core Message of Aloneness
description: The internalized childhood message that nobody is there emotionally, leading to a belief that one is destined to be alone and that certain emotional needs will never be fulfilled.
mechanism: The absence of adequate maternal nurturance in early childhood creates a template of emotional emptiness that becomes the prototype for future relationships.
behavioral_signature: feeling disconnected even from close people

intervention.verbal: What a neglected child feels. It is a feeling of aloneness, of nobody there. It is a sad and heavy sense of knowledge that you are destined to be alone.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding the core message you internalized as a child helps you recognize it as a belief formed in childhood circumstances, not an eternal truth about your life.

exact_quotes: Emotional deprivation is what a neglected child feels. It is a feeling of aloneness, of nobody there. It is a sad and heavy sense of knowledge that you are destined to be alone.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Message of Being Unwanted
description: The internalized belief that one's parents never should have had them, reflecting a sense of being a burden or unwanted presence rather than a cherished child.
mechanism: When parents show no interest or treat the child as an inconvenience, the child internalizes this as evidence of their own unworthiness of care.
behavioral_signature: not asking for what one needs

intervention.verbal: I don't know why she had me. They never should have had me.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These phrases reflect what you concluded as a child, not the truth about your worth or right to exist and have needs met.

exact_quotes: We often hear phrases like these from patients with Emotional Deprivation: 'I don't know why she had me,' or 'They never should have had me.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Escape (Avoiding Romantic Relationships)
description: Coping with the Emotional Deprivation lifetrap by avoiding romantic relationships altogether or only briefly engaging in them, thereby preventing the triggering of deep emotional needs.
mechanism: By avoiding the most intimate type of relationship, the person prevents activation of their deprivation schema and the associated painful feelings of emptiness and disappointment.
behavioral_signature: ending relationships when they become too close

intervention.verbal: Some people who have the Emotional Deprivation lifetrap avoid romantic relationships altogether, or only get into them for a short time. This is typical of the Escape coping style.
intervention.behavioral: Gradually allow relationships to progress past the point of typical termination
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Avoiding relationships protects you from pain but also keeps you from ever getting your needs met. The goal is to gradually allow closeness while building skills to choose and maintain healthy relationships.

exact_quotes: some people who have the Emotional Deprivation lifetrap avoid romantic relationships altogether, or only get into them for a short time. This is typical of the Escape coping style.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Escape (Breaking Off When Partner Gets Close)
description: A pattern of conveniently finding reasons to end relationships when the other person starts to get emotionally close, protecting oneself from vulnerability and potential disappointment.
mechanism: As intimacy increases, anxiety about potential deprivation or abandonment increases, triggering escape behavior before the person can be hurt.
behavioral_signature: finding fault with partner as closeness increases

intervention.verbal: You, like Jed, have a history of breaking off relationships when the person starts to get too close. You conveniently find reasons to end the relationship.
intervention.behavioral: Stay in the relationship through the discomfort of increasing closeness
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The urge to escape is your lifetrap trying to protect you from pain you experienced as a child. But escaping also prevents you from ever having the close relationship you actually need.

exact_quotes: Perhaps you, like Jed, have a history of breaking off relationships when the person starts to get too close. You conveniently find reasons to end the relationship.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Surrender (Choosing Unavailable Partners)
description: Surrendering to the lifetrap by repeatedly choosing romantic partners who are emotionally unavailable, cold, or unable to meet one's needs, thus recreating childhood deprivation.
mechanism: High chemistry with unavailable partners reflects the familiarity of the childhood dynamic; the person is drawn to what feels 'normal' even though it perpetuates deprivation.
behavioral_signature: attraction to married or unavailable people

intervention.verbal: Like Dustin, you protect yourself from closeness by choosing partners who are unavailable. Or, like Elizabeth, you choose someone who is there, but is cold and ungiving. No matter what path you take, the final outcome is the same. You wind up in a situation that is emotionally depriving.
intervention.behavioral: Recognize and avoid partners who generate high chemistry but show signs of unavailability
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The strong chemistry you feel with unavailable people is your lifetrap being triggered. It feels like love, but it's actually the familiarity of your childhood deprivation.

exact_quotes: like Dustin, you protect yourself from closeness by choosing partners who are unavailable. Or, like Elizabeth, you choose someone who is there, but is cold and ungiving.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Surrender (Not Asking for What You Need)
description: Surrendering to the lifetrap by remaining silent about emotional needs, not asking for what one wants, and then feeling hurt when needs are not met.
mechanism: Having learned in childhood that expressing needs does not result in fulfillment, the person stops asking, perpetuating the deprivation cycle.
behavioral_signature: not telling partner what you feel

intervention.verbal: You don't tell your partner what you need, then feel disappointed when your needs are not met. You don't tell your partner how you feel, and then feel disappointed when you are not understood.
intervention.behavioral: Practice explicitly stating emotional needs to partner
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You learned as a child that asking doesn't work. But with the right partner, asking is exactly what will get your needs met. Your partner isn't a mind reader.

exact_quotes: You don't tell your partner what you need, then feel disappointed when your needs are not met. You don't tell your partner how you feel, and then feel disappointed when you are not understood.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Surrender (Always Being the Giver)
description: Surrendering to the lifetrap by consistently giving nurturance to others while receiving little in return, often choosing helping professions or being 'the listener' in relationships.
mechanism: By giving to others what one never received, the person compensates for their own unmet needs while avoiding the vulnerability of receiving.

intervention.verbal: I am always the listener. Other people tell me their problems, and I help them as best I can, but I don't tell anyone my problems.
intervention.behavioral: Practice receiving care and sharing your own problems with others
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Giving to others feels safe because it's familiar. But relationships need to go both ways. Learning to receive is part of healing your deprivation.

exact_quotes: I am always the listener. Other people tell me their problems, and I help them as best I can, but I don't tell anyone my problems.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Surrender (Silence About Deprivation)
description: A surrender coping style characterized by feeling deprived but saying nothing, harboring resentment instead of expressing needs or seeking fulfillment.
mechanism: The person accepts deprivation as inevitable, maintaining silence because they learned in childhood that expressing needs was futile or unwelcome.

intervention.verbal: You feel deprived, but you don't say anything. You harbor resentment. Elizabeth's silence is characteristic of Surrender as a way of coping.
intervention.behavioral: Practice verbalizing feelings of deprivation in the moment
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Staying silent keeps your partner in the dark and keeps you trapped in resentment. Learning to speak up, even when it feels uncomfortable, is essential for getting your needs met.

exact_quotes: You feel deprived, but you don't say anything. You harbor resentment.
Elizabeth's silence is characteristic of Surrender as a way of coping.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Surrender (Learning to Expect Nothing)
description: Complete surrender to the lifetrap, occurring when children were deprived both emotionally and materially, leading them to give up and expect nothing from others.
mechanism: Total neglect in childhood leads to learned helplessness about having any needs met, resulting in passive acceptance of deprivation as normal.
what_activates_it: any opportunity for needs to be met
what_it_maintains: inability to recognize or accept care

intervention.verbal: Some children are neglected in both domains, emotionally and materially. No matter where they turn, they encounter deprivation. These children usually just give up and learn to expect nothing.
intervention.behavioral: Gradually practice accepting small offers of care
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You learned as a child that nothing was available, so you stopped hoping. But as an adult, care is available if you can learn to let it in.

exact_quotes: some children are neglected in both domains, emotionally and materially. No matter where they turn, they encounter deprivation. These children usually just give up and learn to expect nothing (the Surrender coping style).

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Counterattack (Hostility and Demandingness)
description: Counterattacking against feelings of deprivation by becoming hostile and demanding in relationships, acting entitled to have all needs met while feeling chronically unsatisfied.
mechanism: Instead of feeling hurt by deprivation, anger is used as a defense. Demands for material or superficial needs substitute for the true craving for emotional nurturance.

intervention.verbal: Some people with the Emotional Deprivation lifetrap counterattack; they compensate for their feelings of deprivation by becoming hostile and demanding. Jed's anger and demandingness are typical of the Counterattack coping style.
intervention.behavioral: Identify and express the underlying hurt rather than anger
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your anger is a defense against the hurt of feeling deprived. But demanding things won't fill the emptiness because you're not asking for what you really need—emotional connection.

exact_quotes: Some people with the Emotional Deprivation lifetrap counterattack; they compensate for their feelings of deprivation by becoming hostile and demanding.
Jed's anger and demandingness are typical of the Counterattack coping style

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Counterattack (Narcissistic Entitlement)
description: A counterattack style combining Emotional Deprivation and Entitlement lifetraps, where the person becomes very demanding about superficial needs as a substitute for unmet emotional needs.
mechanism: When emotional needs were not allowed to be expressed but material demands were tolerated, the person learns to fight deprivation by demanding tangible rewards instead of emotional nurturance.
what_activates_it: situations where demands can be made
behavioral_signature: entitlement about everything except emotional needs

intervention.verbal: You might be very demanding about what you eat, or how you dress, or whom you are with, or where you go. You might be very demanding about material things. You might be demanding about anything except the true object of your craving, which is emotional nurturance.
intervention.behavioral: Redirect demands toward emotional needs; practice asking for emotional connection
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: All those demands are a poor substitute for what you really want—love and understanding. That's why getting what you demand never satisfies you.

exact_quotes: You might be very demanding about what you eat, or how you dress, or whom you are with, or where you go. You might be very demanding about material things. You might be demanding about anything except the true object of your craving, which is emotional nurturance.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Surrender (Keeping Needs Secret)
description: Surrendering to Emotional Deprivation by not telling your partner what you need, then becoming angry when needs are not met. This pattern ensures that even with a warm partner, emotional needs remain unmet.
mechanism: The person maintains invulnerability as a childhood protective strategy, refusing to communicate needs directly, which guarantees continued deprivation even in potentially nurturing relationships.
what_activates_it: Being in a relationship with a caring partner
behavioral_signature: Not expressing emotional needs to partner
Getting angry when needs are not met

intervention.verbal: If you are with a loving partner, tell the person what you need. Allow your partner to take care of you, protect you, and understand you. Ask yourself, 'This time, is it different? Can I trust this person?' If the answer is 'yes,' perhaps you should take a chance.
intervention.behavioral: Practice expressing needs directly to partner
