intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Keeping your needs secret is a way of surrendering to your lifetrap. You make sure that even though your partner is a warm person, your needs still will not get met.

exact_quotes: Keeping your needs secret is a way of surrendering to your lifetrap. You make sure that even though your partner is a warm person, your needs still will not get met.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Counterattack (Angry and Demanding)
description: Counterattacking against Emotional Deprivation by becoming angry and demanding that needs be met. The person shows only the angry surface while hiding the underlying hurt and vulnerability.
mechanism: Anger builds from unmet needs until all the person feels is resentment. The angry, demanding behavior drives partners away and makes it less likely needs will be met, creating a self-defeating cycle.
behavioral_signature: Expressing only anger, not underlying hurt

intervention.verbal: Below the anger is a feeling of hurt, of vulnerability. Share that with your partner. If you show only the angry, demanding surface, you will drive your partner away and make it less likely that he or she can meet your needs. Becoming angry and demanding is self-defeating. It rarely works. You almost never end up feeling better—things just get worse and worse.
intervention.behavioral: Express needs calmly without blame; share vulnerability
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you are angry, tell your partner how you feel. Do it calmly, without blame. The angry demanding approach rarely works and usually makes things worse.

exact_quotes: My anger builds. I get to the point that all I feel is resentment, and all I'm doing is telling Michelle off.
If you show only the angry, demanding surface, you will drive your partner away and make it less likely that he or she can meet your needs.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Deprivation — Escape (Feeling Bored and Wanting to Leave)
description: Escaping from Emotional Deprivation by feeling bored and dissatisfied in healthy relationships and wanting to walk away, particularly when the relationship seems unexciting.
mechanism: When emotional needs begin to be met in a healthy relationship, the unfamiliar sensation creates discomfort interpreted as boredom. The person wants to escape rather than experience the strange feeling of having needs met.
what_activates_it: Being in a healthy, nurturing relationship
Unfamiliar experience of needs being met
behavioral_signature: Wanting to walk away from nurturing partners
Questioning if relationship is a mistake

intervention.verbal: Do not walk away so fast, even if the relationship seems unexciting. Maybe you just need to get used to the strange sensation of having your emotional needs met.
intervention.behavioral: Stay in healthy relationship despite boredom feelings
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Many people with this lifetrap feel bored and dissatisfied in healthy relationships. The boredom may actually be discomfort with finally getting what you need.

exact_quotes: Many times people with this lifetrap feel bored and dissatisfied in healthy relationships, and they want to walk away.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion — Escape (Avoiding Social Situations)
description: Escaping from Social Exclusion by avoiding most social situations, group activities, and community involvement because they generate too much anxiety.
mechanism: Social situations trigger intense anxiety about being scrutinized and judged negatively. To avoid this discomfort, the person avoids socializing, which prevents disconfirmation of negative beliefs and maintains isolation.
behavioral_signature: Only spending time with immediate family or one or two close friends

intervention.verbal: Socially, you avoid joining groups or being part of the community. You only spend time with your immediate family or with one or two close friends.
intervention.behavioral: Gradually increase participation in social activities
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Avoiding social situations prevents you from learning that you can handle them and that others may accept you.

exact_quotes: They make me too anxious.
I would rather avoid than attend most social functions.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion — Surrender (Staying on the Periphery)
description: Surrendering to Social Exclusion by remaining on the outskirts of groups, keeping to oneself at work and social situations, and not attempting to connect with others.
mechanism: The person accepts their outsider status as inevitable and does not try to change it. They stay detached, go through motions of social interchange while feeling alone inside, confirming their belief they do not belong.

intervention.verbal: At work you are on the periphery. You keep to yourself. You do not get promoted or included in projects because you do not fit in.
intervention.behavioral: Make efforts to connect with colleagues and join groups
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Staying on the periphery confirms your belief that you don't belong, but it also prevents you from finding out that you could belong if you tried.

exact_quotes: I always feel on the outside of groups.
I'm always on the outside, looking in.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion — Counterattack (Overcompensating Through Status)
description: Counterattacking against Social Exclusion by putting excessive emphasis on gaining status, material possessions, physical attractiveness, or other markers of belonging to overcome perceived family or personal deficiencies.
mechanism: The person tries to prove they belong by acquiring external markers of acceptability, working inordinately hard on appearance, career success, or social status to compensate for underlying feelings of not fitting in.
behavioral_signature: Trying to obscure ethnic or family differences
Striving for career success to compensate

intervention.verbal: You put a lot of emphasis on overcoming your own family's deficiencies: to gain status, have material possessions, sound highly educated, obscure ethnic differences, etc. You put too much emphasis on compensating for what you feel are your social inadequacies.
intervention.behavioral: Reduce compensatory behaviors; build genuine connections
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Overcompensating keeps you focused on proving yourself rather than simply being yourself and allowing real connections to form.

exact_quotes: You put a lot of emphasis on overcoming your own family's deficiencies: to gain status, have material possessions, sound highly educated, obscure ethnic differences, etc.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You Are Different From Others
description: Core childhood message internalized when a child's family is different from neighbors, when parents explicitly communicate that the family is above or separate from others, or when the child feels fundamentally different from siblings and peers.
mechanism: Parents may convey that neighbors are bad influences or that the family doesn't belong with those around them. The child internalizes that being different is their identity and that they will never fit in anywhere.
behavioral_signature: Keeping home and social life separate

intervention.verbal: My parents always considered us above everyone around us. They acted as though we were really different—as though we still belonged in a fancy house in a fancy neighborhood. In fact they would make it seem like our neighbors were kind of bad to be around, a bad influence, so I was encouraged to be kind of different and to stay away from the other kids.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The message that you are fundamentally different may have come from your family's circumstances or explicit messages, not from any real inability to connect with others.

exact_quotes: I felt like another species of life entirely.
My parents always considered us above everyone around us.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You Are Socially Inadequate
description: Core childhood message internalized from being teased, rejected, or humiliated by other children for observable qualities like appearance, weight, stuttering, or social awkwardness.
mechanism: Repeated experiences of peer rejection and humiliation lead the child to believe they are fundamentally undesirable and socially incompetent. This belief persists into adulthood despite changed circumstances.

intervention.verbal: I was fat as a child. I was disgusting. In the playground other kids made fun of me. They would chase me and try to get me to fall down. When I got older, none of the boys wanted to go out with me.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The messages you received as a child about being undesirable were specific to that time and those circumstances. They do not define who you are now.

exact_quotes: I was fat as a child. I was disgusting. In the playground other kids made fun of me.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Exploring Anticipated Catastrophe
description: Socratic question used to explore the specific feared outcomes and catastrophic expectations that drive social anxiety before attending a social event.
mechanism: By asking what the patient imagined would happen, the therapist helps uncover the specific fears and negative predictions that fuel anticipatory anxiety and avoidance.

intervention.verbal: What were you imagining would happen?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding your specific fears helps us examine whether they are realistic and develop ways to cope with them.

exact_quotes: THERAPIST: What were you imagining would happen?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Assessing Social Behavior Patterns
description: Socratic question to assess the patient's current behavioral patterns and identify avoidance or limitations in social contact opportunities.
mechanism: This question helps identify whether the patient's social difficulties stem from lack of opportunity, active avoidance, or interpersonal skill deficits.

intervention.verbal: Where do you usually meet people?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding where and how you currently try to meet people helps us identify what might be getting in the way.

exact_quotes: THERAPIST: Where do you usually meet people?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Evaluating Partner Trustworthiness
description: Self-directed Socratic question to help patients with Emotional Deprivation evaluate whether their current partner is safe for vulnerability, distinguishing present relationships from past patterns.
mechanism: By asking whether this situation is different and whether the partner can be trusted, the patient learns to make conscious evaluations rather than automatically applying past protective strategies.
what_activates_it: Being in a potentially healthy relationship

intervention.verbal: This time, is it different? Can I trust this person?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: As a child you had good reason to protect yourself. But now you can evaluate each relationship individually rather than assuming everyone will disappoint you.

exact_quotes: Ask yourself, 'This time, is it different? Can I trust this person?' If the answer is 'yes,' perhaps you should take a chance.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion — Escape
description: The primary coping strategy for Social Exclusion lifetrap involves avoiding social situations entirely to prevent anxiety and potential rejection.
mechanism: Avoidance prevents disconfirmation of negative beliefs about social competence and desirability, keeping skills from improving and maintaining the lifetrap.
what_activates_it: anxiety about being exposed as different
behavioral_signature: relying on partner for social interactions

intervention.verbal: Escape is your primary means for coping with your lifetrap. It is the rock upon which your Social Exclusion is built. Your avoidance of social situations ensures that nothing can change for you. Your skills cannot improve. Your beliefs cannot be disconfirmed. You are more comfortable, but you are stuck. Changing requires a basic shift from a stance of Escape to one of confrontation and mastery. The people who overcome social exclusion are the ones who make this shift.
intervention.behavioral: Create hierarchy of avoided situations and gradually move up
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: As long as you keep running away, your lifetrap cannot change. By the time we reach adulthood, the likelihood of people rejecting us is lower than it was in childhood. But you do not see this because you avoid situations where you might get exactly the positive feedback that you need.

exact_quotes: Escape is your primary means for coping with your lifetrap. It is the rock upon which your Social Exclusion is built.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion — Counterattack
description: Overcompensating for feeling different or inferior by trying to prove the opposite is true, such as impressing others with accomplishments or hiding true self.
mechanism: The person combats the lifetrap by doing everything possible to show they are not different or undesirable, but this creates a false front that is transparent to others.
what_activates_it: fear of being seen as inferior
social situations where deficiencies might be exposed
behavioral_signature: pretending to be like others to fit in
trying to impress people with popularity

intervention.verbal: Not only is counterattacking like this a burden, but your behavior is transparent as well. Other people see right through you. They know you feel ashamed inside. The showing off is false. You know it, and they know it too. Replace the person that you pretend to be with the person you really are. People will like you better, and you will like yourself better as well.
intervention.behavioral: Stop trying to overcompensate; be yourself in groups
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Allow yourself to see that most people will accept you for who you are—not for trying to impress them with your accomplishments or possessions. Giving up this pressure is a relief.

exact_quotes: Having to prove all the time that I was a popular guy was a burden. I was always lying about who I was. I really don't want to have to do it anymore.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion Flashcard — Anxiety at Social Occasions
description: A coping card to help counter anxiety and feelings of being watched at social situations, grounding the person in reality and providing behavioral guidance.
mechanism: Cognitive restructuring through prepared responses that challenge automatic negative thoughts in the moment of activation.
behavioral_signature: using card to interrupt negative thinking

intervention.verbal: I know that right now I feel anxious, as if everyone is looking at me. I feel like I can't talk to anyone. But it is just my lifetrap being triggered. If I look around, I will see that people are not looking at me. And even if someone is, it is probably a friendly look. If I start talking to people, in a little while my anxiety will grow less. People can't really tell I'm anxious. Besides, other people are anxious too. Everyone is a little anxious in social situations. I can start by relaxing my body, looking around the room, and finding one person to talk to.
intervention.behavioral: Relax body, look around room, find one person to talk to
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: A flashcard can help break the spell of the lifetrap, and get you back on the right track.

exact_quotes: I know that right now I feel anxious, as if everyone is looking at me. I feel like I can't talk to anyone. But it is just my lifetrap being triggered.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion Flashcard — Aloofness and Feeling Different
description: A coping card to address feelings of being an outsider and becoming aloof, redirecting toward connection and recognizing commonalities.
mechanism: Cognitive restructuring that interrupts the automatic process of maximizing differences and withdrawing.
what_activates_it: starting to feel like an outsider
behavioral_signature: using card to prompt friendlier behavior

intervention.verbal: I'm starting to feel different from the people I'm with. I'm feeling like an outsider, alone in the crowd. I am holding myself back, becoming aloof. But this is my lifetrap kicking in. In fact I'm exaggerating how different I am. If I become friendlier, I will find that we have things in common. I just have to give myself a chance to connect.
intervention.behavioral: Become friendlier, look for commonalities
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: A flashcard can help break the spell of the lifetrap, and get you back on the right track.

exact_quotes: I'm starting to feel different from the people I'm with. I'm feeling like an outsider, alone in the crowd.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Social Exclusion Emotional Signature
description: The characteristic emotional experience of those with Social Exclusion lifetrap includes loneliness, anxiety in social situations, feelings of being different or inferior, and cold isolation.
mechanism: Early experiences of exclusion create an emotional template that is reactivated in social situations, leading to anticipatory anxiety and withdrawal.
behavioral_signature: high anxiety before and during social events
feeling like an outsider looking in
experiencing relief when leaving social situations
feeling pressure and nervousness when talking to people

intervention.verbal: Social exclusion is a cold, lonely feeling. Do not leave your inner child in that cold place. Before you leave the image, bring yourself in as an adult, and give support to your inner child.
intervention.behavioral: Comfort the excluded inner child through imagery
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The journey out of Social Exclusion is a journey from loneliness to connection.

exact_quotes: I half expect people to start ganging up on me and start chanting songs about how fat I am.
I had the thought that it was always like this, with everyone coming at me from far away. And I'm watching, like through a window.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Childhood Message — I Am Different and Don't Belong
description: The internalized message from childhood experiences of being excluded, laughed at, or not fitting in with peers, creating a core belief of being fundamentally different.
mechanism: Experiences of being humiliated, teased, bullied, or simply being alone and apart from others create an internalized sense of being undesirable or fundamentally different.
what_activates_it: current social situations that mirror past experiences
behavioral_signature: magnifying differences between self and others

intervention.verbal: Remember when you felt different or inferior. Often the memories that come are of being laughed at, humiliated, teased, or bullied, or of being alone, apart from the others, not fitting in.
intervention.behavioral: Comfort the excluded child through adult imagery intervention
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Adults are usually much more tolerant of differences than children. It is only children—or immature adults—who feel that pressure to be exactly the same.

exact_quotes: I'm at my friend Gina's house. There are a bunch of kids there. We're picking sides for a game of kickball, and no one is picking me. I'm the last kid to be picked, and, when the team gets me, they all start to groan.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Childhood Message — I Am Always on the Outside Looking In
description: The internalized message of being perpetually separate from others, watching life through a window rather than participating.
mechanism: Repeated experiences of social exclusion create a persistent sense of separation and disconnection from the social world.
what_activates_it: moments of social connection observed in others

intervention.verbal: Bring yourself in as an adult, and dive into the falls where your childhood self is standing, and tell him that he is not alone anymore. I am here, and I will help him connect with other people.
intervention.behavioral: Adult self comforts and supports excluded child self
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Do not leave your inner child in that cold place.

exact_quotes: I had the thought that it was always like this, with everyone coming at me from far away. And I'm watching, like through a window. Everyone else is out there playing and being normal with each other, and I'm outside looking in.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Imagery for Childhood Social Exclusion
description: A technique to access and process childhood memories of social exclusion by starting with current triggering situations and allowing earlier memories to emerge.
mechanism: Accessing emotional memories allows for processing and rescripting, while adult self-comfort provides corrective emotional experience.
behavioral_signature: allowing images to come without forcing
bridging from current to childhood situations

intervention.verbal: When you have some time alone, darken the room and sit back in a comfortable place. Remember not to force the images. Just close your eyes and let them come. Remember when you felt different or inferior. You can start with an image of a current situation that triggers feelings of Social Exclusion. Stay with it, and let an image come of when you felt the same way as a child. Before you leave the image, bring yourself in as an adult, and give support to your inner child.
intervention.behavioral: 1) Relax in darkened room 2) Get image of current trigger 3) Let childhood memory emerge 4) Adult self enters image to comfort child
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Memories of social exclusion are painful. We want you to comfort that excluded child. Social exclusion is a cold, lonely feeling. Do not leave your inner child in that cold place.

exact_quotes: darken the room and sit back in a comfortable place. Remember not to force the images. Just close your eyes and let them come.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Positive Imagery Rehearsal for Social Situations
description: Using visualization to practice successful social performance before events, replacing catastrophic imagery with images of competent functioning.
mechanism: Positive mental rehearsal reduces anticipatory anxiety and prepares neural pathways for actual performance.
behavioral_signature: lying down and relaxing before events

intervention.verbal: Instead of spending time before an event imagining catastrophic situations and making yourself even more afraid—spend time imagining yourself performing well. Imagine yourself performing exactly as you wish. Have dress rehearsals of success rather than failure. Before I went to the company Christmas party, I lay down and relaxed myself, and I imagined the party going very well. I pictured myself walking in and looking around, smiling, and picking one person to go up to and talk to. I imagined walking up and saying, 'hi,' and feeling composed. I imagined starting to talk, the things I would say.
intervention.behavioral: 1) Lie down and relax 2) Imagine entering situation confidently 3) Visualize specific successful interactions 4) Practice what you would say
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Imagery can play a large role in your preparation. Planning in advance how to handle various scenarios will reduce your anxiety.

exact_quotes: Have dress rehearsals of success rather than failure.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Challenging Maximized Differences
description: Questions used to challenge the tendency to exaggerate differences and minimize similarities between self and others.
mechanism: Socratic questioning exposes the cognitive distortion of selectively attending to differences while ignoring commonalities.
what_activates_it: patient reporting feeling different from others

intervention.verbal: Why didn't you feel you could talk to that new manager at work? But you are in the same field. That is something already. But didn't you hear that he's a foreign film buff too?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The very way you see the world—in terms of differences rather than similarities—reinforces your lifetrap.

exact_quotes: But you are in the same field. That is something already.
But didn't you hear that he's a foreign film buff too?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Identifying Problematic First Impression Behaviors
description: Questions designed to help identify specific behaviors contributing to social difficulties through self-observation and feedback.
mechanism: Systematic analysis of behavior through multiple sources of information leads to concrete targets for change.
what_activates_it: patient unable to identify specific problems

intervention.verbal: So what can we conclude from all this? What aspects of your behavior are problematic?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Once you identify specific problematic behaviors, it becomes fairly easy to change them.

exact_quotes: So what can we conclude from all this?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Exploring Fear Content in Panic
description: A series of Socratic questions used to help the patient explore and articulate the specific content of their fears underlying panic and avoidance behaviors.
mechanism: By asking progressively specific questions about what the patient fears, the therapist helps the patient identify the core catastrophic belief driving the anxiety.
behavioral_signature: movement from vague fear to concrete belief

intervention.verbal: THERAPIST: What are you afraid of? [Patient responds] THERAPIST: What might happen to you?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: By understanding exactly what you're afraid will happen, we can examine whether that fear is realistic and find ways to cope with it.

exact_quotes: THERAPIST: What are you afraid of?
THERAPIST: What might happen to you?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You Cannot Survive Without Help
description: The core childhood message internalized by those with the Dependence lifetrap that they are incapable of taking care of themselves in the world and must rely on others to survive.
mechanism: Through overprotective parenting that prevented independent functioning, the child internalizes the belief that they lack the fundamental capacity to handle adult responsibilities.

intervention.verbal: The message you learned as a child was that you cannot survive on your own. But this belief was never tested because others always did things for you.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The belief that you can't cope alone was formed before you had a chance to prove otherwise. Your dependence is one large untested hypothesis.

exact_quotes: What if something happens to me and there is no one to take care of me?
There are so many things I can't do. I have to have someone there to do them for me.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You Cannot Trust Your Own Judgment
description: The childhood message that one's own opinions and decisions are unreliable and that guidance from others is necessary for making good choices.
mechanism: Parents who made decisions for the child, criticized the child's opinions, or undermined the child's competence created the belief that independent judgment leads to failure.

intervention.verbal: The belief that you can't trust your judgment came from having your decisions criticized or made for you as a child.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You never learned to trust yourself because you were never given the chance to make decisions and learn from them.

exact_quotes: I wish I could make up my mind about Carol. I don't know why I waver back and forth so much. It's like I believe that I can't possibly make the right choice.

The statement I heard most often from my mother was, 'If only you had listened to me.' She was really into, 'I told you so's.' To this day I still feel that if I don't take her advice, I'm going to fail.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: The World Is Dangerous — Don't Go Out
description: The childhood message transmitted by anxious parents that the outside world is full of dangers and that safety lies only within the protected family environment.
mechanism: Anxious or agoraphobic parents communicate their fears to children, who internalize both the belief that the world is dangerous and the associated anxiety response.

intervention.verbal: Your mother's fears became your fears. Her warnings taught you that the world was dangerous before you had a chance to discover otherwise.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You inherited your parent's view of the world as dangerous. But their fears were their own, not an accurate picture of reality.

exact_quotes: 'Don't go!' she would say. 'Don't go out, it's too cold. You'll get sick. Don't go out, it's too dangerous. Don't go out, it's too dark.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: If You Don't Listen to Me, You'll Fail
description: The childhood message that disobeying or not following parental guidance will inevitably lead to failure or negative consequences.
mechanism: Through repeated 'I told you so' responses and criticism of independent choices, parents condition the child to believe that only parental guidance leads to success.

intervention.verbal: You learned that not following advice leads to failure. But this kept you from developing confidence in your own abilities.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your parent taught you that your own choices lead to failure. This prevented you from learning that you can make good decisions.

exact_quotes: The statement I heard most often from my mother was, 'If only you had listened to me.' She was really into, 'I told you so's.' To this day I still feel that if I don't take her advice, I'm going to fail.
'Fine. Just don't come crying to me when you can't think of anything good to say.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence — Surrender (Having Others Handle Tasks)
description: The surrender response to the Dependence lifetrap where the person consistently gets others to perform tasks and make decisions for them, reinforcing the belief in their own incompetence.
mechanism: By allowing others to handle responsibilities, the person avoids the anxiety of independent functioning but confirms the schema that they cannot cope alone.
behavioral_signature: relying on others for daily tasks

intervention.verbal: When you consistently have others do things for you, you reinforce the idea that you cannot do these things yourself and prevent yourself from developing competence.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Each time someone else handles a task for you, it confirms your belief that you can't do it. You've never tested whether you actually can function alone.

exact_quotes: When you consistently act in ways designed to keep people doing things for you, you are surrendering to your lifetrap.
Having people do things for you reinforces the idea that you are not capable of doing these things on your own and keeps you from developing a sense of competence.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence — Escape (Avoiding Adult Responsibilities)
description: The escape response to the Dependence lifetrap where the person avoids tasks they believe are too difficult, confirming their sense of incompetence.
mechanism: By avoiding challenging tasks, the person never learns they can accomplish them independently, perpetuating the belief in their inadequacy.
behavioral_signature: not going to movies or restaurants alone

intervention.verbal: By continually running away from these tasks, you confirm your sense that you cannot accomplish them on your own.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Every time you avoid a task because you think it's too hard, you miss the chance to prove to yourself that you can do it.

exact_quotes: Escape is another way of reinforcing your lifetrap. You avoid the tasks you believe are too difficult for you.

There are certain tasks dependent people commonly avoid. These include driving, attending to financial matters, making decisions, taking on new responsibilities, and learning new areas of expertise. You avoid breaking apart from a parent or partner. You rarely live alone or travel alone. You rarely go to a movie alone or out to eat alone.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence — Counterattack (Counterdependence)
description: The counterattack response to the Dependence lifetrap where the person overcompensates by achieving at high levels and refusing to accept any help, even when reasonable.
mechanism: By constantly proving independence and refusing assistance, the person fights against core feelings of incompetence while remaining anxious underneath.
behavioral_signature: forcing oneself to face everything alone

intervention.verbal: Your extreme independence is actually a sign of the Dependence lifetrap. Underneath, you feel the same as other dependent people, but you cope by refusing all help.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Being unable to accept help is the flip side of being too dependent. Both come from the same fear that you cannot cope on your own.

exact_quotes: This tendency to go to the other extreme—to act as though she does not need anybody for anything—is called counterdependence, and is a strong indication of the presence of the Dependence lifetrap.

Counterdependent people refuse to turn to others for help, even when it is reasonable to do so. They refuse to ask for advice, assistance, or guidance. They cannot allow themselves to get a normal amount of help from other people, because it makes them feel too vulnerable.
It is like if I turn to anyone at all I might become totally dependent.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence — Surrender (Tolerating Abuse to Maintain Dependence)
description: The surrender response where dependent people allow themselves to be abused, subjugated, or deprived in order to maintain the dependent relationship.
mechanism: The fear of being alone is so overwhelming that the person will endure mistreatment rather than risk losing the person they depend on.

intervention.verbal: You are allowing mistreatment because your fear of being alone is greater than your need for respect. This keeps you trapped.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The price of dependence is accepting treatment that causes you pain. You feel trapped because you believe you cannot survive alone.

exact_quotes: Dependent people often allow themselves to be abused, subjugated, or deprived in order to maintain the dependence. They will do almost anything to keep the person with them.
You probably accept a subordinate role in your relationships with family members, lovers, and friends.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence — Counterattack (Dependent Entitlement)
description: A counterattack response where the dependent person feels entitled to have their dependence needs met and becomes angry or punishing when others fail to meet them.
mechanism: The combination of Dependence and Entitlement lifetraps creates a pattern where the person demands care and punishes others for not providing it.
what_activates_it: being required to do things alone
behavioral_signature: getting angry when needs aren't met
criticizing partner who doesn't meet needs

intervention.verbal: You may have a combination of Dependence and Entitlement. You get angry when people don't meet your needs and punish them for it.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you feel entitled to have others take care of you, you become angry at them for not meeting needs you could meet yourself.

exact_quotes: Some dependent people feel entitled to have their dependence needs met.

It may be that you have a combination of the Dependence and Entitlement lifetraps. In this case you get angry when people do not meet your needs. You punish them by sulking, acting irritated, or getting overtly angry.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence Emotional Signature — Trapped and Claustrophobic
description: The emotional experience of feeling trapped in dependent relationships, characterized by claustrophobia, suppressed hatred, and inability to escape despite wanting to.
mechanism: The dependent person feels simultaneously secure and imprisoned, creating intense internal conflict between needing the relationship and hating the constraint.

intervention.verbal: Close your eyes and give me an image of your marriage.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: By examining how you feel in the relationship, we can understand both why you stay and what it costs you.

exact_quotes: I'm in a dark place that I can't get out of, and there is no air, and I can't breathe. It's claustrophobic. Anthony is yelling at me about something. I hear him going on and on, and I hate him so much that I feel like I'm going to explode.
I apologize to him, and I promise I'll never do it again.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence — Surrender
description: The person gives in to the Dependence lifetrap by relying on others for everyday tasks, decisions, and guidance rather than attempting to function independently. This perpetuates the belief that they cannot cope on their own.
mechanism: By consistently turning to others for help with routine tasks and decisions, the person never builds competence or confidence. Each instance of relying on others reinforces the belief that independent functioning is impossible, maintaining the dependent state.
what_activates_it: facing any decision or task alone
behavioral_signature: turning to wiser or stronger people all the time for advice and guidance
avoiding new challenges on your own
not taking care of financial records or decisions
being much more dependent on parents than most people your age
avoiding being alone or traveling alone
remaining ignorant about practical functioning and daily survival skills
not having lived on your own for any significant period of time

intervention.primary: behavioral change through systematic task mastery
intervention.verbal: Use the two lists you have just constructed to map out a plan for yourself. Sit down and rate how difficult each item would be for you to complete. Begin with the easy items. Make sure that you have included items that will be relatively easy for you to complete. In addition, it is important to do a lot of planning before you actually attempt an item, even an easy one. We want you to be ready.
intervention.behavioral: systematically tackle everyday tasks and decisions without asking for help
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: By starting with easy tasks and gradually building up, you can prove to yourself that you are capable of handling things on your own. Each success builds your confidence for the next challenge.

exact_quotes: You turn to wiser or stronger people all the time for advice and guidance
You minimize your successes and magnify your shortcomings
You avoid new challenges on your own
You do not make your own decisions

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence — Escape
description: The person avoids situations that would require independent functioning, including new challenges, changes, and confronting fears or phobias. This avoidance protects them from anxiety but prevents growth.
mechanism: By avoiding challenges and staying within the protective bubble of dependence, the person never experiences evidence that they could cope independently. The avoidance reduces short-term anxiety but strengthens long-term beliefs about incompetence.
behavioral_signature: avoiding new challenges on your own
avoiding being alone or traveling alone
remaining ignorant in many areas of practical functioning

intervention.verbal: List challenges, changes, or phobias that you have avoided because you are afraid of them. Rate how difficult each item would be for you to complete using a 0 to 8 scale. Begin with the easy items. Examine every possibility, and plan what you would do if it occurred.
intervention.behavioral: systematically confront avoided tasks starting with easiest
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Each time you face something you've been avoiding, you prove to yourself that you can handle it. The anxiety will pass, and you'll build confidence that transfers to other challenges.

exact_quotes: You avoid new challenges on your own
You have fears and phobias that you do not confront
You avoid being alone or traveling alone

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dependence — Counterattack (Counterdependence)
description: The person overcompensates for underlying dependency needs by refusing all help, taking on excessive challenges, and appearing completely self-sufficient while feeling chronically anxious and overwhelmed inside.
mechanism: To avoid the vulnerability of dependence, the person swings to the opposite extreme—never asking for help, taking on more than they can handle, and partnering with dependent people they must care for. This creates chronic pressure while the underlying need for support remains unmet.
behavioral_signature: never seeming to be able to turn to anyone for guidance or advice
having to do everything on your own
always taking on new challenges and confronting fears but feeling under constant pressure
partner is very dependent and you end up doing everything and making all decisions

intervention.verbal: If you are counterdependent, acknowledge your need for guidance. Ask others for help. Do not take on more challenges than you can handle. Use your anxiety level as a gauge of how much you are comfortable taking on.
intervention.behavioral: practice asking for help and accepting support
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: There's nothing wrong with healthy dependence—it's normal to want help and support sometimes. By allowing yourself to receive help, you can bring balance into your life and stop feeling constantly overwhelmed.

exact_quotes: You never seem to be able to turn to anyone for guidance or advice. You have to do everything on your own
You are always taking on new challenges and confronting your fears, but you feel under constant pressure while doing it
Your partner is very dependent on you, and you end up doing everything and making all the decisions

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You are an empty hole inside without someone else
description: The internalized message from childhood overprotection and enmeshment that the child has no independent sense of self and must rely on others to define their identity and provide direction.
mechanism: When a parent forces their image onto a child and subjugates the child's natural inclinations, the child loses their sense of self. This creates an internal emptiness that feels like it can only be filled by depending on someone else who has a sense of self.
behavioral_signature: feeling empty when not with attachment figures

intervention.verbal: Your father forced his own image onto you, and forced you to fit it, no matter what your natural inclinations might have been. What you wanted was of no importance, and you gradually lost your sense of self.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You were never allowed to develop your own identity because your parent's wishes overshadowed yours. Learning who you are and what you want is part of recovering from this.

exact_quotes: He once described himself as an empty hole inside. If you have no sense of self, you are totally dependent. There is an emptiness inside, and the only way to fill it is to rely on someone else, someone who has a sense of self.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: I am always swimming out of my depth
description: The internalized message from childhood underprotection that one is constantly facing challenges beyond their capacity, that decisions are unreliable, and that there should be someone to depend on but there is no one.
mechanism: When parents fail to provide adequate guidance and protection, children must make decisions beyond their developmental capacity. This creates chronic anxiety, a sense of being overwhelmed, and a yearning for someone to depend on.

intervention.verbal: Children such as this long for someone upon whom they can depend, to relieve the weight of responsibility. They question their decisions, feel a lot of anxiety about their competence, and yet have no choice but to continue making them.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You had to grow up too fast and handle too much alone. It makes sense that part of you still longs for the support you never got. Learning to accept help from trustworthy people is healthy.

exact_quotes: I always had the feeling that I was swimming out of my depth. I always had the feeling that my decisions weren't good ones, that I wished there was someone I could ask.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Exploring reassurance-seeking behavior
description: Questions designed to help the patient recognize their pattern of seeking safe people and reassurance, even when they have never actually needed to use this safety net.
mechanism: By exploring the specific behavior of looking for potential helpers, the therapist helps the patient recognize the automatic nature of their dependence and the fact that they have never actually needed the help they seek.
what_activates_it: patient describes dependent behavior in public

intervention.verbal: Is that usually the first thing you do, look for someone to take care of you? Have you ever used such a person to help you?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding your automatic patterns is the first step to changing them.

exact_quotes: Is that usually the first thing you do, look for someone to take care of you?
Have you ever used such a person to help you?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Accessing childhood dependence through current emotional trigger
description: Using a current situation that triggers feelings of dependence as a bridge to access childhood imagery and understand the origins of the lifetrap.
mechanism: By starting with a present-day emotional experience and then asking for an earlier image of the same feeling, the patient connects current patterns to their developmental origins, creating insight and emotional processing.

intervention.verbal: Close your eyes and bring that moment back with imagery. What are you feeling? Now give me an image of when you felt this way before, as a child.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Any time that you feel your Dependence lifetrap in your current life can serve as an opportunity for an imagery exercise. Find a quiet place to summon that feeling back.

exact_quotes: Close your eyes and bring that moment back with imagery
What are you feeling?
Now give me an image of when you felt this way before, as a child

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Adult self intervening to help the parentified child
description: An imagery exercise where the patient brings their adult self into a childhood memory to provide the guidance, support, and nurturing that was missing from the original experience.
mechanism: By having the adult self enter the childhood scene and provide what was missing, the patient practices self-nurturing and begins to meet their own dependency needs internally rather than seeking them externally.
what_activates_it: imagery of childhood overwhelm or neglect

intervention.verbal: I want you to bring yourself into the image as an adult and intercede to help that child. What would you say? 'Here, I'll show you how to iron, it's not so hard. I'm sorry that you have to do everything yourself. I know it's too much for you. But I'm here for you when the going gets tough. You can come to me when you need help.'
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You can learn to nurture that part of yourself with imagery exercises. The dependent child within you needs a certain type of help that you can give.

exact_quotes: I want you to bring yourself into the image as an adult and intercede to help that child

Here, I'll show you how to iron, it's not so hard. I'm sorry that you have to do everything yourself. I know it's too much for you. But I'm here for you when the going gets tough. You can come to me when you need help.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Imagery expressing yearning for normal dependence
description: Spontaneous imagery that reveals the counterdependent person's hidden longing for healthy dependence and nurturing that was absent in childhood.
mechanism: The imagery bypasses defenses to reveal the underlying emotional need that the counterdependent coping style has been covering up. This helps the patient acknowledge legitimate dependency needs.

intervention.verbal: What image do you see? 'I see myself as a child. My mother's sitting on the couch, and all I want to do is go across the room, sit down, and lay my head in my mother's lap.'
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This imagery shows the part of you that just wants to stop coping for a while and rest. Acknowledging this need is the first step to meeting it in healthy ways.

exact_quotes: I see myself as a child. My mother's sitting on the couch, and all I want to do is go across the room, sit down, and lay my head in my mother's lap.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Dream imagery of parents letting go on steepening stairs
description: A dream that captures the dependent child's fear of separation and the terrifying sense that one cannot function without parental support.
mechanism: The dream symbolically represents the core fear of the Dependence lifetrap—that without someone holding on, the challenges of life become impossibly difficult.
what_activates_it: contemplating separation from partner or family

intervention.verbal: I was walking up the stairs with my parents. My parents were walking, holding my hand on either side of me. I was a little boy. But then they were letting go of my hand and moving away, and the stairs started getting steeper and steeper, and I was having trouble climbing them alone.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Dreams can show us our deepest fears. Understanding what triggers your fear of being alone helps you work through it.

exact_quotes: I was walking up the stairs with my parents. My parents were walking, holding my hand on either side of me. I was a little boy. But then they were letting go of my hand and moving away, and the stairs started getting steeper and steeper, and I was having trouble climbing them alone.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional signature of Dependence lifetrap
description: The characteristic emotional experience of those with the Dependence lifetrap, including fear of independent functioning, relief when protected, anxiety about competence, and emptiness without attachment figures.
mechanism: The emotional pattern reflects the underlying belief that one cannot cope alone. Proximity to protective figures brings relief and calm, while separation or independence triggers anxiety, fear, and a sense of emptiness or doom.
behavioral_signature: panic when separated from safe people

intervention.verbal: That child is still alive in you. It is a frightened child. Try to comfort that child. Encourage that child. Support that child's efforts to tackle things alone.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding your emotional reactions helps you recognize when the dependent child part of you is activated. You can learn to comfort yourself through these feelings rather than immediately seeking rescue.

exact_quotes: Like I always do. Like I hate him, but at the same time I'm so relieved he's there
That child is still alive in you. It is a frightened child.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability — Escape (Avoiding Activities)
description: The person avoids activities that produce physical sensations similar to panic, such as exercise or sex. This avoidance severely restricts lifestyle and prevents learning that sensations are harmless.
mechanism: Physical sensations from exercise, sex, or excitement are misinterpreted as dangerous, triggering anticipatory anxiety. By avoiding these activities entirely, the person never learns the sensations are benign, reinforcing the belief that they must be avoided.
what_activates_it: any activity that mimics panic symptoms

intervention.verbal: Robert was avoiding all forms of exercise, including sex, when he first came to therapy. The sensations made him too anxious—they were too similar to panic. He had given up playing tennis, which he loves. By trying to Escape his Vulnerability lifetrap, Robert was putting a serious dent in his lifestyle.
intervention.behavioral: Create hierarchy of avoided activities and systematically expose to each
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: By avoiding activities that produce sensations, you never learn they are harmless. Your avoidance is actually maintaining your fear.

exact_quotes: Robert was avoiding all forms of exercise, including sex, when he first came to therapy. The sensations made him too anxious—they were too similar to panic. He had given up playing tennis, which he loves.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability — Escape (Medical Avoidance)
description: Some people with vulnerability avoid doctors entirely because they fear finding out something is wrong, while others run to doctors constantly. Both represent escape from uncertainty about health.
mechanism: The underlying fear of illness creates intolerable uncertainty. Avoiding doctors provides temporary relief from confirmation of feared illness, but maintains hypervigilance and health preoccupation.

intervention.verbal: Similarly, you may run to the doctor continuously, or you may avoid doctors altogether because you are afraid of finding out that something is wrong. Either way, you are constantly preoccupied with thoughts of illness.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Whether you run to doctors or avoid them completely, both keep you focused on illness. We need to find a middle ground of appropriate medical care.

exact_quotes: Similarly, you may run to the doctor continuously, or you may avoid doctors altogether because you are afraid of finding out that something is wrong.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability — Escape (Avoiding Travel and Public Spaces)
description: The person avoids traveling, public transportation, elevators, crowded places, and situations perceived as dangerous. This severely restricts life and family activities.
mechanism: Catastrophic beliefs about danger lead to avoidance of any situation where danger seems possible. Each successful avoidance provides temporary relief but reinforces the belief that the world is dangerous.

intervention.verbal: She won't do anything. We can't go on vacation because she won't get on the plane, and she won't go in the water, and she won't get in the elevator. We can't go into the city on a weekend night because it's too dangerous.
intervention.behavioral: Build hierarchy from least to most feared travel situations and systematically expose
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your avoidance is like a prison. Each situation you avoid makes the prison walls higher. We need to gradually expand your world.

exact_quotes: She won't do anything. We can't go on vacation because she won't get on the plane, and she won't go in the water, and she won't get in the elevator.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability — Surrender (Seeking Overprotective Partners)
description: The person selects partners who are willing to protect, reassure, and take care of them. This surrenders to the lifetrap by maintaining the belief that they cannot cope alone.
mechanism: By choosing strong, protective partners, the person avoids facing fears independently. The partner's reassurance and protection prevent learning that the person can cope, reinforcing dependence and vulnerability.

intervention.verbal: You are most attracted to people who can take care of you. By selecting a partner who protects you, you Surrender to your Vulnerability lifetrap and thus reinforce it.
intervention.behavioral: Ask partner to gradually reduce reassurance and accompaniment
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When your partner constantly protects and reassures you, you never learn that you can handle things yourself. This keeps you feeling vulnerable.

exact_quotes: You are most attracted to people who can take care of you. By selecting a partner who protects you, you Surrender to your Vulnerability lifetrap and thus reinforce it.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability — Surrender (Excessive Safety Measures)
description: The person takes extreme safety precautions including expensive alarm systems, excessive checking, insurance policies, and rigid safety routines. These measures surrender to the belief that catastrophe is imminent.
mechanism: Excessive safety measures temporarily reduce anxiety but reinforce the belief that danger is real and imminent. The safety behaviors become compulsive and never provide lasting reassurance.

intervention.verbal: We have this really expensive burglar alarm system, which she made me get, but she still worries about people breaking in. People who know what they're doing can get by burglar alarms. I keep telling him to put window bars on all the downstairs windows.
intervention.behavioral: Gradually eliminate one safety behavior at a time while observing nothing bad happens
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: No amount of safety measures will ever feel like enough. The more precautions you take, the more dangerous the world seems. We need to break this cycle.

exact_quotes: We have this really expensive burglar alarm system, which she made me get, but she still worries about people breaking in.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability — Surrender (Obsessive-Compulsive Rituals)
description: The person uses magic rituals, counting, checking, washing, and cleaning to ward off perceived dangers. These rituals drain life energy and reinforce the sense that danger is real.
mechanism: Ritualistic behaviors provide temporary anxiety relief through a sense of magical control. The rituals must be performed 'correctly' or anxiety returns. This creates an exhausting cycle that maintains vulnerability beliefs.

intervention.verbal: Before I go to sleep I have to go around the house and check everything five times. I check the iron, the stove, the microwave, the toaster oven, the hair dryer, the children's room, the car, and the garage. It's what I have to do to be able to relax and go to sleep.
intervention.behavioral: Gradually reduce number of checks while tolerating anxiety
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These rituals make you feel temporarily safe, but they actually feed your anxiety. The more you check, the more you need to check.

exact_quotes: Before I go to sleep I have to go around the house and check everything five times. I check the iron, the stove, the microwave, the toaster oven, the hair dryer, the children's room, the car, and the garage.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability — Surrender (Financial Hoarding and Restriction)
description: The person maintains rigid financial safety cushions, refuses to spend money, and goes to extreme lengths to save. This surrenders to the belief that financial ruin is imminent.
mechanism: No amount of money ever feels safe enough. The person maintains anxious vigilance about finances, controls money tightly, and cannot enjoy spending. This prevents career risks and life enjoyment.

intervention.verbal: I have to laugh at myself. The other day I went all the way out on Long Island to buy pants because I had a coupon for ten dollars off. Of course I got there and they didn't have my size. Meanwhile, I had taken a bus and a cab to get there, costing four dollars each way.
intervention.behavioral: Create hierarchy of spending behaviors from least to most anxiety-provoking
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You're spending your life preparing for a catastrophe that may never come, while missing the life happening right now.

exact_quotes: I have to laugh at myself. The other day I went all the way out on Long Island to buy pants because I had a coupon for ten dollars off.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability — Escape (Career Risk Avoidance)
description: The person stays in unfulfilling, boring jobs because they feel safe and secure. Fear of taking risks prevents career advancement or change.
mechanism: The overwhelming priority of safety and security in weighing decisions prevents any risk-taking. Life becomes about containing danger rather than seeking fulfillment, leading to depression and stagnation.
what_it_maintains: belief that safety must come first

intervention.verbal: My job is really dull and boring, way below my level. I really could be an analyst. It's really depressing for me to go to work. The money's good and the job is really secure. I mean, they're not about to fire me.
intervention.behavioral: Identify small career risks and take them systematically
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You're trading your life satisfaction for an illusion of complete safety. No job is truly risk-free, but you can learn to tolerate reasonable uncertainty.

exact_quotes: My job is really dull and boring, way below my level. I really could be an analyst. It's really depressing for me to go to work. The money's good and the job is really secure.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Childhood Message — You Are Too Fragile to Cope
description: The child learns from overprotective parents that they are too weak, fragile, or incompetent to handle everyday challenges without protection.
mechanism: Parents' constant warnings and protection communicate that the child cannot cope independently. The child internalizes this as a core belief about personal fragility and need for protection.

intervention.primary: Build evidence of coping ability through graded challenges
intervention.verbal: Your parent was overprotective of you, particularly around issues of danger or illness. Your parent continuously warned you of specific dangers. You were made to feel that you were too fragile or incompetent to handle these everyday issues.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You were taught you couldn't cope, but that was never true. You can learn that you are more capable than you were led to believe.

exact_quotes: You were made to feel that you were too fragile or incompetent to handle these everyday issues.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Childhood Message — People Cannot Be Trusted
description: Holocaust survivor parents transmitted the message that people, especially outsiders, cannot be trusted and may turn dangerous at any moment.
mechanism: Through direct warnings and stories of betrayal, the child learns that even friendly neighbors can become enemies. This creates pervasive mistrust and fear of others.
what_it_maintains: inability to feel safe with people

intervention.primary: Distinguish past context from present reality
intervention.verbal: They would always tell me not to trust people who weren't Jewish, even friends and neighbors. She told me that when she was a girl in Germany, she saw neighbors turn against her family and suddenly become enemies.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your parents' experiences were real and terrifying, but the danger they faced is not your reality. We can examine whether people today are truly as dangerous as you were taught.

exact_quotes: They would always tell me not to trust people who weren't Jewish, even friends and neighbors. She told me that when she was a girl in Germany, she saw neighbors turn against her family and suddenly become enemies.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability — Surrender (Seeking Excessive Reassurance)
description: People with the Vulnerability lifetrap surrender by repeatedly seeking reassurance from others and allowing loved ones to overprotect them. They rely on partners to be the 'strong one' and avoid making decisions about safety independently.
mechanism: The person maintains their sense of vulnerability by creating dependency on others for safety, which prevents them from developing their own sense of mastery and reinforces the belief that they cannot handle threats alone.
behavioral_signature: relying on partner to be strong
allowing others to make safety decisions
appearing helpless in face of perceived threats

intervention.verbal: Ask them to start protecting you less and reassuring you less. You can ask them to gradually phase these things out.
intervention.behavioral: Meet with loved ones and enlist their support in helping face fears while reducing overprotection
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The people around you reinforce your lifetrap by overprotecting you. Getting them to reduce this to a normal level helps you build your own sense of safety.

exact_quotes: Most partners will jump at the opportunity to give up such an overprotective role. Usually partners are weary of being so solicitous.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability — Escape (Avoiding Feared Situations)
description: People with the Vulnerability lifetrap escape by avoiding situations that trigger their fears entirely, such as staying home rather than going places, avoiding travel, or refusing to enter elevators or airplanes.
mechanism: By avoiding situations where catastrophic events could theoretically occur, the person never learns that their fears are exaggerated and never develops mastery over anxiety. Each avoided situation reinforces the belief that the world is dangerous.

intervention.verbal: The hierarchies involve gradually stopping both your escapes (e.g., going places you usually avoid) and your overprotections (e.g., taking more risks alone). Be sure to include both.
intervention.behavioral: Create step-by-step hierarchies and systematically confront avoided situations
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: There is so much that you miss because of your fears. The journey out of the Vulnerability lifetrap is a journey back to life.

exact_quotes: I think the thing that really got me moving was realizing how much I was missing out. I mean, I was really depriving myself of so much. It was a life devoted to anxiety.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Surrender (Accepting Criticism and Rejection)
description: People who surrender to the Defectiveness lifetrap are in touch with their feelings of being inherently flawed. They are too vulnerable in relationships, allowing others to criticize and reject them without defending themselves.
mechanism: The person accepts others' criticism as valid because it confirms their core belief about themselves. They do not protect or defend themselves because they believe the criticism is deserved.

intervention.verbal: Alison's Defectiveness lifetrap makes her much too vulnerable in relationships. The other person has so much power to hurt her. She does not protect herself or defend herself.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your critical inner voice came from your parent, not from reality. Learning to defend yourself against unfair criticism helps heal the lifetrap.

exact_quotes: She also magnifies evidence that people dislike and reject her
She constantly discounts evidence that people like her and want to be with her

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Escape (Hiding the True Self)
description: People escape their Defectiveness lifetrap by hiding their true self and presenting a false self to others. They may look normal on the surface but keep their real thoughts and feelings secret to avoid exposure and shame.
mechanism: By never revealing the true self, the person avoids the risk of being exposed and rejected. However, this prevents genuine intimacy and healing, as the hidden true self cannot receive acceptance or love.

intervention.verbal: The person constructs a false self. This false self is harder, less easily wounded. But no matter how hard the exterior, deep inside there is pain about losing one's true self.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: A true self that stays hidden cannot heal. You have to stop settling for illusion and start going for the real thing.

exact_quotes: Eliot learned to hide his true thoughts and feelings. His true self became a secret, known only to him. This way, he felt less vulnerable.
Drinking, drugs, overworking, and overeating are all ways of numbing yourself to avoid the pain of feeling worthless.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Counterattack (Narcissistic Compensation)
description: People counterattack against the Defectiveness lifetrap by developing narcissistic traits—acting superior, demanding special treatment, lacking empathy, and blaming others. They become invulnerable so no one can hurt them.
mechanism: By becoming superior and entitled, the person reverses the original dynamic of being criticized and devalued. They make others feel defective instead, which temporarily alleviates their own shame but prevents true healing.

intervention.verbal: It is as if they are saying to the world: 'I will be so demanding, act so superior, and become so special that you will never be able to ignore or criticize me again.'
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The sense that it all might collapse is always with you. Your persona can collapse, suddenly exposing the worthless person you believe you are underneath. True healing requires risking vulnerability.

exact_quotes: He has developed this narcissism to fight back against their underlying feelings that no one will ever love or respect them.
Narcissists will hold onto their self-centeredness at almost all costs. Eliot was watching his marriage to a woman he loved deeply disintegrate, yet he was unable to admit that he had problems.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Vulnerability Flashcard — Elevator Fear
description: A complete flashcard example written by Heather to address her fear of riding elevators, demonstrating how to counter catastrophizing thoughts and encourage facing avoided situations.
mechanism: The flashcard helps interrupt automatic catastrophizing by providing a rational counter-perspective at the moment the lifetrap is triggered, reminding the person of the true source of their fears.

intervention.verbal: I know that right now I am afraid to ride the elevator. I am afraid of some catastrophe, like the building catching fire, and the elevator getting stuck. I feel this is very likely to happen. But what really is happening is that my Vulnerability lifetrap is getting triggered. I'm probably exaggerating the degree of risk. Therefore, I will force myself to go into this situation anyway, in spite of my fears, to see that it's not really dangerous. I know I want to count the floors five times before I get on the elevator. I feel that the checking will make me feel safer. But I don't need to do the checking. I am safe enough without it. The checking is just a superstition. Besides, it's a burden and I want to give it up.
intervention.behavioral: Read the flashcard whenever the lifetrap is triggered before entering the feared situation
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The flashcard counters your tendency to catastrophize and reminds you that your feelings of danger are exaggerated. Eventually, your anxiety will pass and you will feel comfortable.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Challenging Probability Estimation — Airplane Fear
description: A Socratic questioning sequence used to examine the exaggerated probability estimates that people with the Vulnerability lifetrap have about feared events occurring.
mechanism: By asking the patient to articulate their felt probability and then comparing it to objective statistics, the therapist reveals how emotional reasoning distorts risk assessment under the sway of the lifetrap.

intervention.verbal: What do you think the odds are of your airplane crashing? ... And when you are on an airplane, what do you think the odds are then? ... Do you know that the odds really are closer to one in a million?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Right now you use intuition to judge the odds of feared events occurring. The problem is that your intuition is simply wrong because it is under the sway of your lifetrap.

exact_quotes: HEATHER: I don't know. I guess about one in a thousand.
HEATHER: When I'm on the plane, the odds seem higher. Maybe six out of ten.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Identifying the Critical Voice Origin
description: A Socratic question used to help patients recognize that their harsh self-critical voice is actually an internalized voice from a critical parent, not their own authentic evaluation of themselves.
mechanism: By asking whose voice is speaking the criticism, the therapist helps externalize the self-attack and trace it to its developmental origin, creating distance between the patient's true self and the internalized critic.

intervention.verbal: Whose voice is this? Whose voice is it in your head that's saying all this?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You internalized the voice of your critical parent and it became part of you. In a sense, the voice of your critical parent is your lifetrap—this voice that constantly criticizes, punishes, and rejects you in your mind.

exact_quotes: ALISON: Well, it's Eric. My old boyfriend.
ALISON: (Pause.) It's my father's voice.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Imagery Reparenting — Comforting the Vulnerable Child
description: An imagery exercise where the patient accesses a childhood memory of feeling vulnerable, then brings themselves into the image as an adult to comfort and reassure their frightened inner child.
mechanism: By revisiting the original scene of vulnerability and introducing a protective adult figure (the patient's current self), the patient can provide the safety and reassurance that was missing in childhood, gradually healing the wounded child part.
what_activates_it: current triggers of the Vulnerability lifetrap

intervention.verbal: Get an image of a time as a child that you felt vulnerable. Don't force it. Just tell me the first one that comes into your mind. ... Once you are in touch with the image and the feeling of vulnerability, bring yourself into the image as an adult to comfort your frightened child. Try to make the vulnerable child feel safer.
intervention.behavioral: Practice bringing in the adult self whenever the lifetrap is triggered in daily life
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The feelings connected to your lifetrap are child feelings. They are the feelings of your vulnerable inner child. You need to develop an inner parent to help this inner child.

exact_quotes: I bring myself into the image as an adult. I sit down at the table with the child Heather. I say: 'You don't have to be afraid. You're safe. You're in your own house and I am here and you're safe. No one will hurt you. There are no Nazis here. If you want to go out and play, I will come with you. I'll protect you. I'll help you face what you fear.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Imagery Rehearsal — Positive Outcome for Feared Situations
description: An imagery technique where the patient visualizes successfully entering feared situations, resisting safety behaviors, and coping well, as a rehearsal before attempting the real-life exposure.
mechanism: By repeatedly imagining successful outcomes, the patient counteracts the automatic catastrophic imagery that maintains the lifetrap and builds a sense of mastery and confidence before real-world practice.
what_activates_it: preparing for exposure to feared situations

intervention.verbal: Use the hierarchies you developed. Start with the easier steps first. Sit down in a comfortable chair, and relax yourself with your breathing meditation. Once you are relaxed, get an image of the feared situation. Imagine yourself going through the situation exactly as you would wish.
intervention.behavioral: Practice imagery rehearsal before each real-life exposure attempt
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Imagery plays a major role in the triggering of this lifetrap. You are vividly picturing the worst possible outcome. We want you to start using imagery to make things better, not worse. You have had many imagery rehearsals of bad outcomes. It is time to have some imagery rehearsals of success and safety.

exact_quotes: I'm standing at the elevator. Walt is with me, and the adult Heather is there too. It makes me feel safe to have them there. I want to count all the floors five times, but I resist. Once I decide not to do the counting, I get this wave of anxiety, but it passes through me and leaves. I feel fine. I feel strong and confident. The elevator comes and the doors open. We get in. I stand calmly and do my relaxation. Before you know it, the elevator stops and we get out. We have gone five floors and I feel fine.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Escape (Avoiding Intimate Relationships)
description: Coping with the Defectiveness lifetrap by avoiding long-term, intimate relationships altogether. This includes having no relationships, only short relationships, or multiple superficial relationships to prevent anyone from getting close enough to see inner flaws.
mechanism: By avoiding long-term commitment and intimacy, the person ensures that no one gets close enough to discover their perceived defects. This maintains the belief that they are fundamentally flawed while protecting against the anticipated pain of rejection.
behavioral_signature: having series of short intense affairs
choosing partners who don't want intimacy
only dating people felt to be 'below' oneself
