intervention.verbal: List signs that you might be coping with Defectiveness through Escape. Do you abuse alcohol or drugs? Do you overeat or overwork? Do you avoid getting close to people? Are you very closed about discussing personal feelings? Try to stop these behaviors designed to Escape. This will allow your feelings of defectiveness to surface more readily. You cannot deal with your lifetrap until you are in touch with it.
intervention.behavioral: Stop engaging in avoidance patterns for one month
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: By always running away from your feelings of defectiveness—through drinking, avoiding close relationships, or hiding your real thoughts and feelings—your lifetrap cannot change. Your feelings of defectiveness remain frozen.

exact_quotes: You may cope with your Defectiveness lifetrap by avoiding long-term, intimate relationships altogether. You may have no relationships at all, only short relationships, or multiple relationships. By avoiding long-term commitment, you make certain that no one gets close enough to see your inner flaws.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Escape (Substance Use and Numbing)
description: Coping with defectiveness feelings through substance abuse, overeating, overworking, or other numbing behaviors to avoid experiencing the painful core feelings of shame and worthlessness.
mechanism: Substances and compulsive behaviors provide temporary relief from painful defectiveness feelings, but they prevent the person from processing and healing these feelings, keeping them frozen in place.

intervention.verbal: Do you abuse alcohol or drugs? Do you overeat or overwork? Make explicit the ways you Escape to cope with your feelings of defectiveness. Watch yourself and write them down. Try to stop these behaviors for one month.
intervention.behavioral: Stop escape behaviors for one month to allow defectiveness feelings to surface
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: If you are always drinking, avoiding close relationships, or hiding your real thoughts and feelings—your lifetrap cannot change. Your feelings of defectiveness remain frozen.

exact_quotes: On days that he was home, he smoked marijuana and drank beer. Most of the time, he stayed isolated in his bedroom watching television.
We made an agreement that he would stop trying to escape in these ways for one month.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Escape (Hiding True Self)
description: Coping with defectiveness by developing a false self and hiding one's true thoughts, feelings, and characteristics from others. The person believes only their false self is worthy of love.
mechanism: By hiding the true self, the person avoids the anticipated rejection that would come from being fully known. However, this reinforces the belief that the real self is shameful and unlovable, and prevents genuine connection.
what_it_maintains: belief that real self is unlovable
behavioral_signature: putting on an act with partner
maintaining emotional distance while appearing close

intervention.verbal: Are you very closed about discussing personal feelings? By not being completely open, you reinforce your sense that the real self is shameful and unlovable. Your greatest terror may be of being exposed: that eventually your partner will see through your act to the defective person beneath.
intervention.behavioral: Try to be more genuine in close relationships
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: A true self that stays hidden cannot heal. You have to stop settling for illusion and start going for the real thing. By hiding your true self, you never believe that your partner loves the real you.

exact_quotes: I always feel like I'm putting on an act with Matthew.
The failure to be genuine is a common pattern with this lifetrap. You believe that only your false self is worthy of love.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Counterattack (Criticizing Others)
description: Coping with defectiveness by becoming hypercritical of partners, children, and others. This includes devaluing loved ones, picking apart their flaws, and maintaining impossibly high standards for partners.
mechanism: Criticizing others allows the person to feel temporarily superior and distract from their own perceived defects. By keeping partners 'one-down,' they reduce the threat of being judged or rejected. Devaluing loved ones prevents caring too much about their opinions.
behavioral_signature: having exact specifications for perfect partner
criticizing most the person loved most

intervention.verbal: Are you hypercritical of other people? Do you devalue the people you love? Make explicit the ways you Counterattack to cope with your feelings of defectiveness. If you are in a relationship where you are the critical partner, try to stop putting your partner down. Do the same in other close relationships.
intervention.behavioral: Stop criticizing loved ones
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You may be more comfortable with a partner you can see as one-down. Then you do not have to worry so much about being found out, judged, or rejected. But this pattern damages your relationships and prevents true intimacy.

exact_quotes: Eliot picks apart everything I do. When I'm with him, I feel like I'm always doing something wrong.
Eliot criticizes his partners for failing to meet his specifications. In this way, he keeps himself from caring too much about how they feel toward him.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Counterattack (Success and Status Seeking)
description: Compensating for feelings of defectiveness by overemphasizing success, status, and achievement. The person's entire sense of worth becomes built on external accomplishments rather than intrinsic value.
mechanism: Success provides temporary relief from defectiveness feelings by proving worth through external validation. However, this becomes addictive—the person can never get enough success to feel genuinely good, and any failure triggers collapse into feelings of worthlessness.
what_activates_it: fear of being seen as defective
behavioral_signature: devotion to career at expense of family
operating only at extremes of success/failure

intervention.verbal: Do you overemphasize status or success? Do you try to impress people? Success and status often become addictions. You try to get more and more, but you can never get enough to make you feel good. Success is a pale substitute for finding one person who really knows and loves you.
intervention.behavioral: Reduce emphasis on success; focus on authentic relationships
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: If you use success in your career to make up for feelings of defectiveness, then your sense of well-being may be quite fragile. Your whole sense of worth becomes built on your success. Any small deflation or failure may be enough to make you nervous.

exact_quotes: Eliot's life is devoted to gaining success and status. He does it to impress people. When he is with a woman, it is all he talks about. This is his way of proving himself worthy of love. The end result, though, is that he has status and success, but he still does not have love. He is looking for love but settling for admiration.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Counterattack (Defensiveness to Criticism)
description: Coping with defectiveness by vehemently denying any flaws, mistakes, or errors. The person becomes hypersensitive to criticism and responds with denial or hostility to protect against the flood of shame that acknowledging any flaw would trigger.
mechanism: Acknowledging any flaw is experienced as opening the floodgates to overwhelming shame feelings. By denying all criticism, the person attempts to maintain their protective shell and keep defectiveness feelings at bay.

intervention.verbal: Are you defensive about criticism? To acknowledge any flaw is to let in a flood of painful feelings related to Defectiveness. Thus, you protect yourself by denying any flaw, mistake, or error. Your defensiveness and inability to take criticism can be a serious problem.
intervention.behavioral: Practice accepting minor criticism without defending
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You may find it difficult to tolerate criticism because you are hypersensitive to it. Even a slight criticism can lead you to feel enormous shame. Learning to accept valid feedback is an important step in healing.

exact_quotes: You may also find it difficult to tolerate criticism. You are probably hypersensitive to it. Even a slight criticism can lead you to feel enormous shame. You may vehemently deny that you have done anything wrong, or put down the person who is criticizing you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Surrender (Accepting Mistreatment)
description: Surrendering to the defectiveness lifetrap by tolerating being badly mistreated in relationships because the person believes this is all they deserve. They stay with critical, rejecting, or abusive partners.
mechanism: The person accepts mistreatment as confirmation of their perceived defectiveness. Critical and rejecting partners feel familiar because they echo the childhood environment, and the person believes they are 'lucky' to have anyone who wants to be with them.
behavioral_signature: staying with nasty and insulting partners
being most attracted to rejecting partners

intervention.verbal: Stop allowing people to treat you badly. We strongly recommend that you stop dating partners who do not treat you well rather than try to win them over and gain their love. You basically feel that this is all you deserve. When asked why you stayed so long, you might say 'I felt I was lucky to have someone who wanted to be with me at all.'
intervention.behavioral: End relationships where you are mistreated; set boundaries
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Many people who get into masochistic relationships—in which they tolerate being badly mistreated—have Defectiveness lifetraps. You do not deserve to be treated this way.

exact_quotes: Many people who get into masochistic relationships—in which they tolerate being badly mistreated—have Defectiveness lifetraps. They basically feel that this is all they deserve.
I felt I was lucky to have someone who wanted to be with me at all.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Surrender (Pursuing Rejecting Partners)
description: Surrendering to defectiveness by being most attracted to partners who are critical, rejecting, or uninterested. Strong chemistry is felt with partners who reinforce feelings of defectiveness.
mechanism: Partners who criticize and reject feel familiar because they echo the childhood environment. The person experiences the most chemistry in situations that reinforce defectiveness because it is consistent with their self-image. Conversely, they get bored with partners who love them.
behavioral_signature: losing interest when partner shows love
feeling alien when valued by someone valuable

intervention.verbal: If you have the Defectiveness lifetrap, be careful when there is very strong chemistry. You probably have the most powerful attraction to partners who criticize and reject you. They reinforce your feelings of defectiveness. Critical partners will feel familiar because they echo your childhood environment.
intervention.behavioral: Recognize chemistry as a warning sign; choose partners who treat you well despite lower initial chemistry
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You have the most chemistry in situations that reinforce your defectiveness. It somehow feels alien to have someone you value value you. This is your paradox: you want love so much, but the more your partner gives you love, the less attracted you feel.

exact_quotes: You probably have the most powerful attraction to partners who criticize and reject you. They reinforce your feelings of defectiveness.
You want love so much, but the more your partner gives you love, the less attracted you feel.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Surrender (Self-Deprecation)
description: Surrendering to defectiveness by putting oneself down around partners and in relationships, constantly comparing oneself unfavorably to others, and feeling envious and inadequate.
mechanism: The person actively reinforces their defectiveness schema by verbalizing self-criticism, idealizing others while exaggerating their own flaws, and seeking constant reassurance that paradoxically undermines their perceived value.
what_it_maintains: needy behavior that drives partners away

intervention.verbal: When I'm with Matthew, and I feel all needy and jealous, I tell myself it won't help at all to start bugging him about it. I tell myself it will just make me feel worse—weak and kind of worthless. Plus it gets him mad at me. Instead, I talk to myself. I tell myself it's okay, he loves me. I tell myself I'm worthy of his love.
intervention.behavioral: Stop needy behaviors; self-soothe instead of seeking external reassurance
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your attempts to insulate your partner from rivals usually backfire. You come across as needy and insecure, which drives your partner away. This tends to lower your value in their eyes.

exact_quotes: Whenever we go out, like to a bar or a party, I always feel like he wants to be with the other women more than with me.
She comes across as needy and insecure, which drives Matthew away.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Surrender
description: Giving in to the Defectiveness lifetrap by accepting criticism passively, allowing others to treat you badly, and internalizing the critical voice without fighting back.
mechanism: The person surrenders to feelings of shame and worthlessness by not defending themselves, tolerating abusive criticism from friends or partners, and failing to assert their rights or demand respectful treatment.
what_activates_it: presence of critical friends or partners

intervention.verbal: Start standing up for yourself. Assert your rights. Tell the person that you will no longer tolerate abusive criticism. Demand that you be accepted as you are. Remember the principles of assertiveness. Do not talk to the person in an angry and aggressive manner. You will be much more effective if you remain calm. Stand up straight and look the person in the eye. Be direct. Be specific. And above all, do not get into defending yourself. Just keep restating your point in a calm and controlled manner.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you stay silent and accept mistreatment, you reinforce your belief that you deserve to be treated badly. Standing up for yourself, even in small ways, begins to challenge that belief.

exact_quotes: To this day she puts me down. Like the other day she said to me, 'You better let Matthew get you that ring before he changes his mind.' That was such a bitchy thing to say.
What did you do when she said it? Nothing. I got upset.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness — Counterattack (Eliot's Pattern)
description: Eliot's specific counterattack behaviors for his Defectiveness lifetrap included criticalness, need to impress people, workaholism, and infidelity as ways of overcompensating for deep feelings of shame.
mechanism: By being critical of others, constantly trying to impress, working obsessively, and seeking validation through affairs, Eliot avoids confronting his core feelings of defectiveness while maintaining a facade of superiority and success.
what_activates_it: fear of being seen as inadequate
what_it_maintains: emotional distance from wife and children

intervention.verbal: Eliot agreed to stop these behaviors for one month.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you stop the behaviors that help you avoid your feelings, you can finally face what's underneath and begin to heal.

exact_quotes: Similarly, Eliot's flaws were mostly forms of counterattack or ways he overcompensated: his criticalness, his need to impress people, his workaholism, his infidelity. As we noted before, Eliot agreed to stop these behaviors for one month.
The odd thing is that I feel more relaxed and more in control. Especially at work. Nobody's throwing me off at all.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure — Escape
description: Avoiding taking steps necessary to advance career, develop skills, or tackle new challenges as a way of escaping the possibility of failure and the painful feelings associated with it.
mechanism: The person reasons that there is no point in trying when they are doomed to fail anyway. This massive avoidance prevents skill development, opportunity seizure, and career advancement, creating a self-fulfilling prophecy of failure.

intervention.verbal: Your avoidance is what holds you back. You avoid taking the steps necessary to widen your knowledge and advance your career. You let opportunities for success pass you by. You are afraid that if you try you will fail.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The expectation of failure becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy when you avoid trying. Your avoidance, not your ability, is what's actually holding you back.

exact_quotes: You reinforce the Failure lifetrap primarily through Escape. Your avoidance is what holds you back. You avoid taking the steps necessary to widen your knowledge and advance your career. You let opportunities for success pass you by. You are afraid that if you try you will fail.
With the Failure lifetrap, the degree to which you use Escape as a coping style is often massive.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure — Escape (Kathleen's Proposal)
description: Kathleen's specific escape pattern involved not completing a proposal her boss requested, allowing a career opportunity to pass by due to avoidance.
mechanism: Despite taking the rare step of asking for a promotion opportunity, Kathleen's escape pattern emerged when she failed to follow through on writing the proposal, ensuring the opportunity would be lost.
what_activates_it: fear of being exposed as inadequate
behavioral_signature: asking for opportunity but not following through

intervention.verbal: A few weeks ago I went and talked to my boss about letting me be in charge of scheduling for this one project. It's really rare for me to make that kind of move, but I just felt like I had to do it. Anyway, my boss told me to write up a proposal. You know, a plan, whatever. Well, three weeks have gone by and I still haven't done it. The project is starting tomorrow and now it's really too late.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Notice how you took a brave first step but then your escape pattern took over. The pattern, not your ability, is what stopped you.

exact_quotes: A few weeks ago I went and talked to my boss about letting me be in charge of scheduling for this one project. It's really rare for me to make that kind of move, but I just felt like I had to do it. Anyway, my boss told me to write up a proposal. You know, a plan, whatever. Well, three weeks have gone by and I still haven't done it. The project is starting tomorrow and now it's really too late.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure — Escape (Brian's Subtle Avoidance)
description: Brian's subtle escape pattern involves appearing to tackle work while actually procrastinating due to anxiety, ensuring he cannot do the job properly.
mechanism: Anxiety about potential failure leads to delayed start on projects, which then creates time pressure that makes quality work impossible, creating a self-fulfilling prophecy of inadequate performance.
what_activates_it: anxiety about being exposed as fraudulent

intervention.verbal: Your avoidance may be subtle. You may appear to tackle your work but still do things to avoid. You procrastinate, you get distracted, you do the work improperly, or you mishandle the tasks you take on. These are all forms of self-sabotage.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Even though you look like you're working, the anxiety-driven delay is a form of avoidance that guarantees you can't do your best work.

exact_quotes: This last project my boss gave me to do, I've been so anxious about it that I didn't get started until this week. I'm really under pressure now. I can't possibly do the job the way it should be done at this point. The whole thing has got me so frazzled.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure — Surrender
description: Surrendering to the Failure lifetrap by constantly twisting events and circumstances to reinforce the view of oneself as a failure, exaggerating negatives and minimizing positives.
mechanism: The person selectively attends to any criticism or negative feedback while dismissing or minimizing positive feedback, thereby maintaining their self-image as a failure regardless of actual performance.
what_activates_it: positive feedback that doesn't match self-image

intervention.verbal: Another way you surrender to your lifetrap is by constantly twisting events and circumstances to reinforce your view of yourself as a failure. You exaggerate the negative and minimize the positive.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your mind is filtering out the evidence of your competence and amplifying any hint of failure. This isn't accurate perception—it's the lifetrap distorting reality.

exact_quotes: I know I blow things out of proportion. Like yesterday, my boss gave me this really positive feedback about a press release I had written. But he criticized one tiny detail about it. And of course I went home and fretted about that detail all night.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: A Defectiveness Flashcard (Alison)
description: A complete flashcard example for challenging the Defectiveness lifetrap, demonstrating how to acknowledge current feelings, identify the lifetrap trigger, and affirm true positive qualities and evidence of being loved.
mechanism: The flashcard serves as a weapon against the critical parent voice, keeping awareness that there are two sides—the critical parent and the vulnerable child who deserves love. It helps push out the critical voice so the healthy side can give the child what they need.
what_activates_it: social situations with seemingly superior people

intervention.verbal: Right now I feel humiliated and inadequate. I feel surrounded by people, especially women, who seem superior to me in every way—looks, brains, personality. I feel their presence diminishes me totally. But this is not true. What is really going on is that my lifetrap is being triggered. The truth is that I am worthy too. I am sensitive, intelligent, loving, and good. The truth is that many people have found me to be worthy of love: [lists their names]. Generally I have not given people a chance to get close enough to really know and appreciate me. But believing what I say on this card will help me move in this direction.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The flashcard is a weapon against the voice of the lifetrap. It keeps you aware that there are two sides. It helps you remember to give yourself love.

exact_quotes: Make a flashcard that you can take out and read whenever your Defectiveness lifetrap is triggered—whenever, as Alison says, 'the voice of my father starts yammering in my head.'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You Had Nothing Worthwhile About You
description: Core message internalized when a parent acted as though the child had nothing special or great about them, that they didn't matter and didn't deserve love or attention.
mechanism: When a parent consistently devalues and ignores a child, the child internalizes the message that they are fundamentally worthless and undeserving of love, leading to chronic shame.

intervention.verbal: You were cruel to me when I was a child. You acted as though I had nothing worthwhile about me, nothing special or great. I just didn't matter to you. You didn't care that I had feelings, that I could feel pain or get hurt. You couldn't be bothered to give me love.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Writing out what happened and how it made you feel helps you externalize the critical voice and recognize that this was something done to you, not a truth about who you are.

exact_quotes: You were cruel to me when I was a child. You acted as though I had nothing worthwhile about me, nothing special or great. I just didn't matter to you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You Are Nothing Compared to Your Sibling
description: Core message internalized when constantly compared unfavorably to a sibling, leading to feeling like nothing in comparison and deep shame about one's own qualities.
mechanism: The parent's differential treatment—showing happiness and excitement with one child while being mean and disgusted with another—communicates that the unfavored child is fundamentally defective and disappointing.
what_activates_it: witnessing parent's positive treatment of sibling

intervention.verbal: What hurt most was how you always compared me to [my brother] Rick. You made me feel like nothing next to him. When you were around Rick, you'd act all happy and excited. But when you were around me, you were mean and disgusted. Like I was such a disappointment.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The comparison wasn't fair or accurate—it said more about your parent's limitations than about your true worth.

exact_quotes: What hurt most was how you always compared me to [my brother] Rick. You made me feel like nothing next to him. When you were around Rick, you'd act all happy and excited. But when you were around me, you were mean and disgusted. Like I was such a disappointment.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure Lifetrap — Escape
description: Kathleen demonstrates the escape response mode by avoiding school through playing sick, not attempting to learn material, and watching television instead of facing academic challenges. This avoidance pattern became lifelong.
mechanism: When faced with potential failure or humiliation, the person avoids the triggering situation entirely rather than confronting it, which prevents skill development and reinforces feelings of incompetence.

intervention.verbal: I would try to get out of going to school by playing sick. If there was a test or a paper due, I would get sick that day. I just couldn't face the humiliation of failing again.
intervention.behavioral: Face challenges instead of running away
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your avoidance developed as a way to cope with failure as a child, but it's no longer helping you. Running away leads only back to failure.

exact_quotes: I would try to get out of going to school by playing sick. If there was a test or a paper due, I would get sick that day. I just couldn't face the humiliation of failing again.
Nah. I would just watch television. I spent my childhood watching television.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure Lifetrap — Counterattack
description: Brian compensated for his failure lifetrap by adopting the image of a rebel as a teenager—dressing outlandishly, riding motorcycles, and becoming skilled at chasing women. This created an area of success without tackling the core issue.
mechanism: The person develops excellence in alternative domains (sports, seduction, appearance, rebellion) to feel good about themselves while avoiding direct confrontation with feelings of failure in achievement.

intervention.verbal: These compensations are fragile. They easily collapse, and yield to the feeling of failure. You need to deal with the issue of achievement more directly.
intervention.behavioral: Address achievement directly rather than through compensatory behaviors
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: While these alternative successes feel good, they don't address your core feelings about achievement and can collapse easily.

exact_quotes: As a teenager Brian compensated for his lifetrap by adopting the image of a rebel. He dressed outlandishly and rode motorcycles. He became skilled at chasing women. He found a way to feel good about himself without tackling the prime issue.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure Lifetrap — Escape via Successful Partner
description: Kathleen compensates for her failure lifetrap by choosing a successful partner (Wayne, head writer on top-rated TV show), living vicariously through his success while not accomplishing much herself.
mechanism: By attaching to a successful partner, the person gains access to high-status social circles and vicarious achievement without having to face their own challenges with achievement.
behavioral_signature: moving in high circles through partner

intervention.verbal: You may be drawn to other roles or to partners who are successful to compensate for your feelings of failure. This is really another avoidance strategy on your part. It is another way for you to escape facing the challenges of achievement.
intervention.behavioral: Emphasize importance of succeeding for yourself
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Living through your partner's success doesn't address your own feelings of failure and keeps you from developing your own potential.

exact_quotes: One way Kathleen compensates is by choosing a partner who is successful. Wayne, her husband, is the head writer on a top-rated television show. When she goes to job functions, such as cocktail parties and conventions, she moves in the highest circles.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Failure Lifetrap — Surrender Patterns
description: Twelve specific ways people surrender to and reinforce the Failure lifetrap through behavioral patterns that ensure continued failure.
mechanism: Through various self-sabotaging behaviors, the person maintains their identity as a failure and confirms their negative beliefs about their competence.
behavioral_signature: not developing solid skills in career
refusing to work way up ladder
selecting impossible career without knowing when to give up

intervention.verbal: The Failure lifetrap feeds on itself in such a way that the entire arena of work becomes a disaster for you. Your expectation of failure becomes a self-fulfilling prophesy.
intervention.behavioral: Identify which patterns apply and create specific plan to change
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: These patterns boil down to escape—you avoid taking the steps necessary to advance yourself. Through avoidance, you constantly twist events to reinforce your view of yourself as stupid, untalented, and incompetent.

exact_quotes: Many of these patterns boil down to the issue of Escape: you avoid taking the steps necessary to advance yourself. Through your avoidance, you constantly twist events to reinforce your view of yourself as stupid, untalented, and incompetent.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness Message — You Cannot Trust Your Success
description: Brian's father constantly criticized him about everything, making Brian feel so generally defective that he could never believe in his own achievements, even when he did well in school.
mechanism: Chronic criticism from a parent creates a global sense of defectiveness that contaminates even areas of objective success, leading to impostor syndrome and fraudulent feelings.

intervention.verbal: My father was always criticizing me about everything, not just about school. Actually achievement was the one area where I could sort of do okay. But I never trusted it, I never trusted that I really knew what I was doing.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your father's criticism damaged your ability to believe in yourself—this was about his issues, not your actual competence.

exact_quotes: My father was always criticizing me about everything, not just about school. Actually achievement was the one area where I could sort of do okay. But I never trusted it, I never trusted that I really knew what I was doing.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Competitive Father Message — Success Will Be Punished
description: Brian's father felt competitive with him and put Brian down to feel better about himself, especially after losing his job. He punished Brian for succeeding because he was threatened by Brian's potential to surpass him.
mechanism: A parent who feels threatened by their child's success undermines the child's self-confidence by punishing achievement, teaching the child that success brings negative consequences.
what_it_maintains: feeling like a failure despite achievements
behavioral_signature: comparing to others who chose 'better' careers

intervention.verbal: Particularly after he lost his job and we had to move to a smaller house, when I was eight, he would really light into me. He would boost himself up by humiliating me.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your father was threatened by your success. He punished you for succeeding as a way to feel better about himself—this was about his needs, not your worth.

exact_quotes: Particularly after he lost his job and we had to move to a smaller house, when I was eight, he would really light into me. He would boost himself up by humiliating me.
His father was threatened by Brian's success at school. He was afraid that Brian would surpass him, so he punished Brian for succeeding.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Wrong Direction Message — Your Talents Are Worthless
description: Brian's father dismissed his creative talents in art and writing, insisting he become a doctor. When Brian struggled in pre-med (not his natural aptitude), his father blamed him rather than recognizing the mismatch.
mechanism: Parents impose their own agenda regardless of the child's natural talents, causing failure in imposed domains while the child's actual abilities remain undeveloped and devalued.
what_it_maintains: conflict between authentic self and parental expectations

intervention.verbal: The trouble was that science and math were not my thing. I was into more creative things—art and writing. My father always looked down on my interest in art. 'That and fifty cents will get you a ride on the subway,' he used to say.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Many times when we fail as a child, it is because we are being pushed in a direction that is not naturally ours. Your father's expectations didn't match your real abilities.

exact_quotes: The trouble was that science and math were not my thing. I was into more creative things—art and writing. My father always looked down on my interest in art. 'That and fifty cents will get you a ride on the subway,' he used to say.
When I switched to creative writing, my father was furious at me. He stopped paying for my education.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Current Event to Childhood Image Bridge
description: An imagery technique where the patient starts with a current triggering event, makes it vivid and emotional, then allows a childhood image of the same feeling to emerge spontaneously.
mechanism: By connecting current emotional reactions to their childhood origins through imagery, patients can understand the source of their lifetrap and access the wounded child part that needs healing.

intervention.verbal: When something happens in your current life to trigger your Failure lifetrap, take some time to explore the event through imagery. Sit in a dark, quiet room and close your eyes. Get an image of the current event. Make the image as vivid and emotional as possible. Then let an image come of when you felt the same way as a child. Do not force the image. Just let it float to the top of your mind.
intervention.behavioral: Practice imagery exercises when triggered
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your images can help you understand the origins of your lifetrap. That child who felt like a failure is still very much alive in you.

exact_quotes: Sit in a dark, quiet room and close your eyes. Get an image of the current event. Make the image as vivid and emotional as possible. Then let an image come of when you felt the same way as a child. Do not force the image. Just let it float to the top of your mind.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Imagery Confrontation with Parent
description: An imagery exercise where the patient visualizes a parent or peer and tells them how their behavior affected them, expressing anger and standing up for themselves.
mechanism: By confronting the person in imagery, patients can express emotions safely, develop assertiveness, and begin to reject the internalized critical messages.
what_activates_it: need to stand up for self
behavioral_signature: stating what you wish they could be

intervention.verbal: Get an image of a parent or a peer, and tell the person how you feel. Get angry at the people who made you feel like a failure. Talk back to your lifetrap. Stand up for yourself. You can do this by writing letters, talking to people directly, or through imagery.
intervention.behavioral: Practice confrontation in imagery before attempting in real life
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Regardless of whether you confront people directly, it is vital for you to confront them in your own heart. Give a voice to the strong part of you that can reject the label of failure.

exact_quotes: Get angry at the people who made you feel like a failure. Talk back to your lifetrap. Stand up for yourself. You can do this by writing letters, talking to people directly, or through imagery.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Peer Comparison Assessment for Failure
description: A structured questioning approach to assess the accuracy of the patient's sense of failure by systematically comparing achievements to a range of peers.
mechanism: By objectively comparing to peers from bottom, middle, and top of class, patients can assess whether their failure perception is accurate or distorted.

intervention.verbal: List a range of people with whom you went to high school, college, or graduate school. Be sure to choose people from the bottom, middle, and top of your class. Write down what each person has accomplished within their chosen fields. How far have they progressed? How much are they paid? How much responsibility do they have at work? Where do you stand in comparison?
intervention.behavioral: Complete peer comparison exercise
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Most of the time you will have a lot of realistic evidence of failure. But sometimes your perception is inaccurate and you will find little evidence to support your view.

exact_quotes: List a range of people with whom you went to high school, college, or graduate school. Be sure to choose people from the bottom, middle, and top of your class. Write down what each person has accomplished within their chosen fields. How far have they progressed? How much are they paid? How much responsibility do they have at work? Where do you stand in comparison?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Surrender
description: The subjugated person gives in to the lifetrap by constantly meeting others' needs, suppressing their own desires, and allowing others to control their choices. They remain passive, letting life happen to them rather than actively shaping events.
mechanism: The person prioritizes what others want over their own needs, becoming agreeable and eager to please to avoid conflict. They say yes to unreasonable demands, fail to set limits, and lose a clear sense of who they are as the boundary between self and subjugator becomes blurred.
behavioral_signature: not asking others to change behavior
not sending back cold food at restaurant

intervention.verbal: The feeling of subjugation is oppressive. It is a burden to live life under the weight of that feeling. Constantly meeting the needs of other people is so much responsibility. It is exhausting. Life loses much of its joy and freedom.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Recognizing that your passivity is a coping style rather than your true nature helps you understand you have choices you weren't aware of.

exact_quotes: It is not, 'What I want and feel,' but rather, 'What you want and what I can do to make you happy with me.'
Rather than an actor, you are a reactor.
You feel there is little you can do to solve your problems. You merely wait and hope that suddenly, miraculously, everything will get better.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Escape
description: The subjugated person escapes by losing themselves in the subjugator, merging identities to avoid the discomfort of having separate needs. They may also be drawn to groups with charismatic leaders or cults.
mechanism: By becoming so immersed in meeting others' needs, the boundary between self and other becomes blurred. The person adopts others' goals, opinions, and values as their own, essentially escaping the burden of having a separate self with separate needs.
what_activates_it: lack of strong sense of self
behavioral_signature: adopting other people's goals as own
losing sense of where self ends and other begins

intervention.verbal: You can become so immersed in trying to meet the needs of other people that you begin to blend or merge into these people. The boundary between who you are and who they are becomes blurred.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding that you've lost yourself in others helps you recognize you have a separate identity worth discovering.

exact_quotes: You might adopt other people's goals and opinions as your own. You might adopt other people's values. You might lose yourself in the other.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Counterattack (The Rebel)
description: Instead of submitting, the person becomes aggressive, domineering, defiant, and self-centered. They overcompensate for feelings of subjugation by taking the opposite role, constantly battling authority and refusing external control.
mechanism: The rebel copes with subjugation by adopting the role of someone aggressive and defiant. However, this is a mask—underneath they feel the same as other subjugated people, that they are less important and others are in control. Their choices remain dictated by what they're rebelling against.
behavioral_signature: pursuing careers or relationships opposite to parents' wishes

intervention.verbal: Rebels are not actually any more free than other subjugated people. They do not freely choose their interests or relationships; choices are made for them by the people they are rebelling against. Through an insistence on disregarding the rules, they are just as bound to them as the person who obeys them.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: True freedom comes from making choices based on what you genuinely want, not from doing the opposite of what others expect.

exact_quotes: They fit the punch line of that joke, 'Why did the teenagers cross the road?'—'Because somebody told them not to.'
Your aggression is just a mask, and you wear it with a sense of falseness.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Counterattack (Excessive Self-Control)
description: Some subjugated people compensate by seizing rigid control over some aspect of themselves, such as food intake in anorexia. Because they feel out of control of most life areas, they exert extreme control over one domain.
mechanism: The person feels powerless over most aspects of life but rebels by maintaining rigid control over one area. This becomes a battleground for control, unknowingly reenacting the Subjugation lifetrap while appearing to fight against it.
what_activates_it: lack of autonomy in life decisions
behavioral_signature: battleground dynamics with parent over eating

intervention.verbal: The one area in which Rose feels in charge—the one area in which she exerts control—is her weight. She controls her weight with a vengeance. Through the symptoms of anorexia, she rebels against her mother, and unknowingly reenacts her Subjugation lifetrap.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your need for control in this one area reflects how little control you feel elsewhere—addressing the underlying subjugation can help you feel genuinely in charge of your life.

exact_quotes: Food becomes the battleground for control between Rose and her mother.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Your Needs Don't Matter
description: The subjugated child learns that their own needs, desires, and opinions are less important than those of others. They internalize the message that everyone has rights except them.
mechanism: Through repeated experiences of having needs dismissed, criticized, or punished, the child develops the core belief that their needs are trivial and not worth fighting for. This becomes the foundation for adult subjugation patterns.
what_activates_it: withdrawal of love when child disagrees
behavioral_signature: not fighting for what one wants because desires seem trivial

intervention.verbal: Everyone has rights except you. We hear this argument often from subjugated patients: that they do not fight for what they want because their desires seem so trivial. But in the end, when you add all the trivial desires together, you are left with a life in which few of your needs are met.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your needs are not trivial—they add up to the quality of your entire life.

exact_quotes: It is what the other person wants that comes first.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Being Yourself Is Wrong/Weak/Selfish
description: The child learns that expressing individuality, having personal interests, or asserting needs is wrong, weak, or selfish. This is internalized into intense self-criticism whenever they want to assert themselves.
mechanism: When the child made demands or expressed individuality, the parent criticized, ridiculed, or called them weak and selfish. The child learns that having a self is bad, carrying this into adulthood as self-criticism whenever they want to assert themselves.
what_activates_it: parental criticism when child expressed needs
demands for conformity to parent's image
behavioral_signature: giving up personal interests like piano

intervention.verbal: Whenever Carlton made demands, his father called him weak and selfish. After a while, Carlton learned that he was bad to have needs of his own. He carried this feeling with him so that later, as an adult, he became intensely self-critical whenever he wanted to assert himself.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The self-criticism you feel when asserting yourself comes from childhood messages, not from reality.

exact_quotes: His father didn't like it. He thought it wasn't macho enough. He made fun of me. He wouldn't let me take lessons.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You Are Responsible for Others' Well-Being
description: The child learned that they are responsible for taking care of others—a parent who was sick, depressed, or alcoholic—leading to excessive self-sacrifice and neglect of their own needs in adulthood.
mechanism: By taking on caretaking responsibilities inappropriate for a child, they developed the belief that their role is to fix things and make everyone better. This becomes an excessive virtue in adulthood.
behavioral_signature: staying home to care for parent instead of playing

intervention.verbal: As a very young child, he developed self-sacrifice as a means of preserving an attachment to his mother—by keeping her intact, he could insure that she would be available to him. He took care of his mother. He was the parentified child.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: As a child, you took on adult responsibilities to survive. As an adult, you no longer need to sacrifice yourself to maintain relationships.

exact_quotes: I tried to keep her company, keep her happy. She was so down all the time. I would stay with her instead of going outside to play.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Exploring Passivity in Getting What You Want
description: A Socratic exchange exploring how the patient's subjugation leads to passivity and waiting for others to give them what they want rather than pursuing it directly.
mechanism: By reflecting the patient's experience back to them, the therapist helps them see the pattern of passivity and waiting rather than acting.
what_activates_it: patient expressing helplessness about getting what they want

intervention.verbal: CARLTON: I just feel like I can't get what I want in life. I don't know how to get it. THERAPIST: You feel like all you can get is what other people deign to give you. You don't go after what you want.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding this pattern is the first step toward becoming an active agent in your own life.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Exploring Lost Childhood Interests
description: A Socratic question exploring what the patient genuinely enjoyed as a child before subjugation caused them to abandon their authentic interests.
mechanism: By asking about childhood interests, the therapist helps reconnect the patient with their authentic self that was suppressed.
what_activates_it: patient uncertainty about what they want in life

intervention.verbal: Was there anything you liked as a child, something that was special to you?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Reconnecting with what you genuinely enjoyed before you learned to suppress your needs can help you rediscover who you really are.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Exploring Consequences of Disobedience
description: A Socratic question exploring what happened when the patient disobeyed as a child, revealing the fear-based origins of submissive subjugation.
mechanism: By asking about consequences of childhood disobedience, the therapist helps the patient understand the fear-based origins of their current submission.

intervention.verbal: What happened when you disobeyed?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding the real consequences you faced as a child helps you see why you learned to submit, and that those consequences no longer apply.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Exploring Whether Child Received Help
description: A Socratic question exploring whether anyone helped the parentified child who was taking care of an alcoholic or incapacitated parent.
mechanism: By asking if anyone helped, the therapist validates the child's isolation and excessive burden, while also exploring family dynamics around denial.

intervention.verbal: Didn't anyone try to help you?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding how alone you were as a child helps you recognize you deserved support and don't have to carry everything alone now.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Exploring True Source of Disproportionate Anger
description: A Socratic question exploring why the patient became so angry at a particular moment, revealing displaced anger from subjugation.
mechanism: By asking why the anger occurred at that specific time, the therapist helps the patient trace disproportionate anger back to its true source—usually someone they are subjugated to.
what_activates_it: patient describing angry outburst at inappropriate target

intervention.verbal: Why did you get so angry at Kathy right then?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you explode at the wrong person, it usually means you're holding back anger at someone you feel you can't confront directly.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Surrender (Self-Sacrificing Relationships)
description: Surrender mode for Subjugation where the person seeks out needy, dependent, or alcoholic partners and forms self-sacrificing relationships. The person channels their talent for self-sacrifice into caregiving roles, becoming co-dependent and constantly rescuing others.
mechanism: The subjugated person gravitates toward needy individuals they can rescue, maintaining the familiar pattern of putting others' needs first while their own needs remain unmet.
what_activates_it: opportunities to help or rescue others
relationships with alcoholic or dysfunctional partners

intervention.verbal: His pattern is to seek out needy people, particularly alcoholic women, and form self-sacrificing relationships with them. He has helped himself to overcome this self-destructive pattern by joining a support group for children of alcoholics.
intervention.behavioral: joining support groups, forming relationships where needs have a chance of being met
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You learned to sacrifice yourself as a child, and now you seek out relationships that repeat this pattern. By recognizing this, you can choose healthier relationships where your needs also matter.

exact_quotes: His pattern is to seek out needy people, particularly alcoholic women, and form self-sacrificing relationships with them

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Surrender (Submission to Dominant Partners)
description: Surrender mode where submissive subjugated individuals seek out aggressive, dominant figures and leaders. They become dependent on others to make decisions and require a strong figure to tell them what to do and how to feel.
mechanism: The passive, subjugated person seeks dominant partners who will direct their lives, creating total dependency that maintains the subjugation pattern.
behavioral_signature: doing everything to avoid partner's anger

intervention.verbal: Shannon feels totally dependent upon her husband. She feels anxious if they are apart even for a few hours. She does everything right to avoid his anger. What she fears most is that in his anger he might abandon her; she feels certain that she could never survive on her own.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your dependence maintains your subjugation, and your subjugation maintains your dependence. Breaking this cycle requires recognizing that you can survive independently.

exact_quotes: She does everything right to avoid his anger. What she fears most is that in his anger he might abandon her; she feels certain that she could never survive on her own

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Counterattack (Rebel Dominating Others)
description: Counterattack mode where the 'rebel type' subjugated person selects a passive partner to dominate and control. Rather than being subjugated, they become the subjugator, flipping the childhood dynamic.
mechanism: The person overcompensates for their childhood subjugation by becoming the dominant, controlling figure in relationships, subjugating others as they were once subjugated.
behavioral_signature: refusing to comply with others' requests

intervention.verbal: Even if you are the rebel type and have selected a passive partner to dominate and control, the process is still one of subjugation.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Being the controller instead of the controlled is still about subjugation—it's the same pattern in reverse. True freedom comes from relating as equals.

exact_quotes: Even if you are the rebel type and have selected a passive partner to dominate and control, the process is still one of subjugation

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Counterattack (Tyrannical with Subordinates)
description: Counterattack mode where a person subjugates themselves to superiors but becomes tyrannical with those below them. They take out their accumulated anger on subordinates through bullying behavior.
mechanism: The person splits their response—surrendering to those with power over them while counterattacking against those they have power over, displacing their rage onto safer targets.
behavioral_signature: two extremes: anger-less vs. out of control

intervention.verbal: I'm like a tyrant in the department. I snap out commands, and people better step to. And if they mess up, they are going to hear it from me, loud and clear all through the department. That's revenge. You treat them even worse than your boss treats you.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Taking out your anger on subordinates is revenge for how you're treated by superiors. It doesn't resolve your subjugation—it just passes it down the chain.

exact_quotes: I'm like a tyrant in the department. I snap out commands, and people better step to
That's revenge. You treat them even worse than your boss treats you

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Escape (Passive-Aggressive Behavior)
description: Escape mode where the subjugated person avoids direct confrontation but expresses anger indirectly through passive-aggressive means. They act out rather than verbalize their anger.
mechanism: Unable to express anger directly due to fear, the person escapes through indirect expression—procrastination, sullenness, excuse-making, and sabotage—allowing anger release while avoiding confrontation.

intervention.verbal: Carlton causes trouble in hidden ways. He acts out his anger rather than expressing it directly. He walks through the office with a sullen expression on his face. He complains to the other employees behind his father's back and encourages others to complain. He procrastinates, and then apologizes or makes excuses for not having his work done.
intervention.behavioral: practicing direct assertion, expressing anger appropriately as soon as felt
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Acting out your anger indirectly damages relationships without solving the problem. Learning to express anger directly, as it arises, is healthier and more effective.

exact_quotes: To his face I say it's just fine, but he should know from the way that I say it that I don't mean it

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Escape (Silent Treatment)
description: Escape mode where the subjugated person withdraws communication entirely when angry or hurt, using silence as an indirect way to express displeasure without confrontation.
mechanism: When direct expression feels impossible, the person escapes into silence, hoping the other person will notice their withdrawal—but often they don't, increasing frustration.
behavioral_signature: hoping partner notices without being told

intervention.verbal: I was giving Dennis the silent treatment the other night, and he didn't even notice... I kept trying to tell him something, and he wouldn't listen. He kept interrupting me and talking about himself. So I decided not to talk to him at all. And he didn't even notice.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The silent treatment rarely gets your needs met—often the other person doesn't even notice. Direct communication, while uncomfortable, is more likely to create change.

exact_quotes: I was giving Dennis the silent treatment the other night, and he didn't even notice

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Surrender (Workplace Yes Person)
description: Surrender mode in workplace settings where the person agrees with superiors and coworkers to please them rather than expressing their true opinions. They hide valuable ideas and stay silent when they should speak.
mechanism: Fear of conflict and desire to please authority figures leads to suppression of genuine opinions and ideas, presenting overly optimistic views and accepting too much work.
behavioral_signature: saying what people want to hear

intervention.verbal: Helen tends to say what people at work want to hear, rather than what she views as the truth, especially when she deals with authority figures. She refrains from making suggestions or disagreeing, even when she has something important to offer. Many of her valuable opinions and ideas remain hidden because she is silent when she should speak.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your expertise and opinions have value. By staying silent to please others, you deny the organization your contributions and prevent your own advancement.

exact_quotes: Many of her valuable opinions and ideas remain hidden because she is silent when she should speak

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Surrender (Underselling Accomplishments)
description: Surrender mode where the person consistently plays down their importance and contributions, allowing others to take credit for their work and backing down in negotiations.
mechanism: Discomfort with attention and fear of seeming demanding leads to systematic undervaluing of self, allowing others to benefit from one's work while staying in a diminished position.
behavioral_signature: doing tedious work that should be delegated

intervention.verbal: I work hard. I contribute a lot to the team. But I'm not confident enough. I don't like being the center of attention. Like the other day, when Greg took credit for my cake mix idea, I had trouble speaking up, setting the record straight... At the last minute, I let Greg do it. And he ended up taking the credit.
intervention.behavioral: taking credit for accomplishments, presenting own work, practicing negotiation
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you let others take credit for your work, you reinforce your own feelings of unimportance. Speaking up for yourself builds confidence and gets you recognized.

exact_quotes: At the last minute, I let Greg do it. And he ended up taking the credit

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Challenging Fear of Partner's Anger
description: A Socratic question that explores the catastrophic thinking behind fear of a partner's anger, helping the patient examine whether the feared consequences are realistic.
mechanism: By asking directly about the feared outcome, the therapist helps the patient articulate and examine beliefs that may be exaggerated or based on childhood fears rather than current reality.
what_activates_it: patient expressing fear of partner's anger

intervention.verbal: What is so bad about Anthony getting mad at you?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding what you're really afraid of helps you see if those fears match your current reality or are echoes from childhood.

exact_quotes: What is so bad about Anthony getting mad at you?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Eliciting Personal Preferences
description: A series of Socratic questions that gently guide a subjugated patient to focus on their own opinion rather than deferring to their partner's view.
mechanism: By persistently asking for the patient's own view, the therapist helps them practice forming and expressing personal preferences they habitually suppress.

intervention.verbal: Did you like it? ... Well, try to think about it now. ... So much that you didn't like it?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Learning to form and express your own opinions is an essential skill for overcoming subjugation. Start with low-stakes topics like movies.

exact_quotes: Did you like it?
Well, try to think about it now

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Exploring Direct Expression with Authority
description: A Socratic question that explores whether the patient has ever directly communicated their limits to authority figures, highlighting the passive-aggressive alternative.
mechanism: The question reveals the pattern of indirect communication and opens discussion about the possibility of direct assertion.

intervention.verbal: Do you ever tell your father that the work is too much?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Direct communication gives others a chance to respond to your actual needs. They can't read your mind or interpret your sighs.

exact_quotes: Do you ever tell your father that the work is too much?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Exploring Decision-Making Process
description: A Socratic question that helps the patient examine how they approach decisions, revealing patterns of excessive reliance on others' opinions.
mechanism: By having the patient describe their decision process, the therapist helps them see how seeking others' opinions can increase rather than decrease confusion.

intervention.verbal: How did you go about making the decision?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding your decision-making process helps you see if you're relying too much on others and losing your own sense of direction.

exact_quotes: How did you go about making the decision?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Surrender
description: The surrender response to the Subjugation lifetrap involves excessive accommodation to others' needs, difficulty saying no, avoiding the word 'I' when expressing feelings, and allowing relationships to remain imbalanced with the person giving far more than they receive.
mechanism: The individual accepts the schema as true and behaves consistently with it, suppressing their own needs to prioritize others, using indirect language to minimize their own importance, and maintaining one-sided relationships where they continuously give without receiving.
behavioral_signature: saying 'People feel angry' instead of 'I felt angry'
excessive time listening to others vs talking about self
anxiety when talking about self for 'too long'
switching conversation back to other person

intervention.primary: Assertiveness training with specific behavioral practice
intervention.verbal: Speaking in terms of your own feelings is an important component of assertiveness. This is, in part, a practical matter. No one can argue with your feelings. If you say, 'I was right, you were wrong,' a person can argue; but if you say, 'I felt angry when you did that,' no one can argue. No one can say, 'No, you didn't feel angry.' By expressing what you feel, you make a statement that how you feel matters.
intervention.behavioral: Use the word 'I' when expressing feelings; practice asking others to take care of you; discuss your problems; achieve balance between giving and getting
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you talk about yourself and ask for help, it actually brings you closer to people rather than pushing them away. If there are people who simply do not want to listen, then it is time to reevaluate their importance in your life.

exact_quotes: Instead of saying, 'I felt angry when you cut me off,' they say such things as 'People feel angry when they are cut off that way.'
Many subjugated patients tell us that when they talk about themselves for 'too long,' they start to get anxious, and they switch back to the other person.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Counterattack (Rebellion)
description: The rebel counterattack response to Subjugation involves doing the opposite of what authority figures tell you to do. While appearing free and autonomous, rebels are actually just as controlled by others—their decisions are still dictated by external influence, just in opposition.
mechanism: The individual overcompensates against the schema by reflexively opposing authority, creating an illusion of independence while actually remaining enslaved to others' expectations in a reactive way. They may become overly aggressive rather than appropriately assertive.
behavioral_signature: inability to agree with authority even when appropriate

intervention.primary: Cognitive restructuring to recognize rebellion as continued oppression
intervention.verbal: Liberate yourself from outside influence—from those you rebel against. Look to yourself for your opinions and direction. You do not know yourself any better than other subjugated people, and you are not any freer. As long as your decisions are dictated by other people, you are as oppressed—and as angry. Give yourself the freedom to agree with authority figures.
intervention.behavioral: Learn appropriate assertiveness rather than over-aggression; try to even out give-get ratio; practice figuring out what you actually want independent of others' wishes
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your rebellion feels like freedom but it's actually another form of being controlled. True freedom means making your own choices—which sometimes may align with what others suggest.

exact_quotes: For those of you who are rebels, liberate yourself from outside influence—from those you rebel against.
As long as your decisions are dictated by other people, you are as oppressed—and as angry.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation — Escape
description: The escape response to Subjugation manifests as giving up too soon on healthy relationships, claiming lack of interest or chemistry, and avoiding balanced partnerships. Subjugated individuals may flee from relationships that feel 'strange' because they offer equality rather than the familiar pattern of subjugation.
mechanism: The person avoids the unfamiliar discomfort of balanced relationships by ending them prematurely or dismissing them as lacking chemistry. The healthy relationship feels wrong precisely because it doesn't activate the schema, leading to escape from potential healing relationships.
what_activates_it: partners who show genuine care for needs
being asked for opinions and having them valued
what_it_maintains: pattern of choosing controlling or needy partners
behavioral_signature: giving up on good relationships claiming 'no chemistry'
returning to familiar patterns with controlling partners

intervention.primary: Relationship pattern awareness and tolerance of healthy discomfort
intervention.verbal: Subjugated people frequently give up too soon on good relationships, claiming they just are not interested, the relationship does not feel right, something is missing, or there is not enough chemistry. As long as you feel some chemistry—even a moderate amount—give the relationship a chance. As you become more accustomed to your new role, the chemistry might increase.
intervention.behavioral: Stay in healthy relationships longer; tolerate the strangeness of equality; notice when high chemistry correlates with unhealthy patterns
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The relationships that feel most familiar and exciting may be the ones that repeat your lifetrap. A relationship that feels 'strange' because your partner respects you deserves a chance—the chemistry can grow as you adjust to being treated well.

exact_quotes: Subjugated people frequently give up too soon on good relationships, claiming they just are not interested, the relationship does not feel right, something is missing, or there is not enough chemistry.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Self-Sacrifice Flashcard — Refusing Unreasonable Requests
description: A flashcard written by Carlton to remind himself of his right to be assertive and refuse unreasonable requests, addressing anticipated guilt and affirming self-respect as outcomes of saying no.
mechanism: The flashcard provides immediate cognitive intervention during triggering situations, offering alternative beliefs and predicted positive outcomes to counter automatic subjugation responses.
what_activates_it: urge to say yes despite personal cost

intervention.primary: Flashcard intervention for in-vivo cognitive restructuring
intervention.verbal: I have the right to say 'no' when people ask me to do unreasonable things. If I say 'yes,' I will only get angry at the other person and at myself. I can live with the guilt of saying 'no.' Even if I cause the other person a little pain, it will only be temporary. People will respect me if I say 'no' to them. And I will respect myself.
intervention.behavioral: Carry the flashcard; read it when lifetrap is triggered and assertiveness is needed
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Flashcards help bridge the gap between knowing something intellectually and actually feeling it emotionally. Reading the card in the moment helps make the slow transition from understanding to genuine acceptance.

exact_quotes: I have the right to say 'no' when people ask me to do unreasonable things. If I say 'yes,' I will only get angry at the other person and at myself. I can live with the guilt of saying 'no.' Even if I cause the other person a little pain, it will only be temporary. People will respect me if I say 'no' to them. And I will respect myself.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Submission Flashcard — Relationship Rights and Self-Respect
description: A flashcard written by Mary Ellen about her relationship with Dennis, affirming her right to respect and equal treatment, her capacity to demand change, and her ability to leave if necessary.
mechanism: The flashcard counters automatic submission responses by affirming self-worth, establishing boundaries, and providing a cognitive framework for assertive action including the option to leave damaging relationships.

intervention.verbal: What I want is important. I deserve to be treated with respect. I don't have to let Dennis treat me badly. I deserve better than that. I can stand up for myself. I can calmly demand that he treat me with respect or the discussion is over. If he can't grow enough to give me my equal rights in this relationship, then I can leave the relationship and find one that better suits my needs.
intervention.behavioral: Carry flashcard; read when triggered by partner mistreatment; use as basis for assertive confrontation
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This card reminds you of what you deserve and what you're capable of doing. Reading it in difficult moments helps you remember that you have choices and rights, even when your lifetrap makes you feel powerless.

exact_quotes: What I want is important. I deserve to be treated with respect. I don't have to let Dennis treat me badly. I deserve better than that. I can stand up for myself. I can calmly demand that he treat me with respect or the discussion is over. If he can't grow enough to give me my equal rights in this relationship, then I can leave the relationship and find one that better suits my needs.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Conditional Love — You Only Matter When You Succeed
description: The internalized message that the child only exists or matters to parents when achieving success, winning awards, or meeting high standards. The child experiences themselves as invisible or worthless except during moments of accomplishment.
mechanism: Parents only provide affection, approval, or attention contingent on success or perfection, teaching the child that their intrinsic worth is zero and they must earn love through achievement. This creates an endless pursuit of accomplishment to feel loved.
behavioral_signature: rushing to tell parents about achievements

intervention.verbal: It was as though I didn't exist for them except when I won some award or got the highest grade. I remember when they told me I was valedictorian, my first thought was rushing home to tell my parents, that they would be happy with me. Most of the time they just didn't seem to care.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding that you learned to equate achievement with love helps explain why you can't stop pushing yourself. You're still running that childhood race, trying to win love that should have been given freely.

exact_quotes: It was as though I didn't exist for them except when I won some award or got the highest grade.
Most of the time they just didn't seem to care.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Endless Pursuit — Running Toward a Receding Goal
description: The internalized experience of childhood as an endless race to win parental love, captured in imagery of running toward a house that keeps moving further away no matter how fast one runs.
mechanism: The imagery represents the impossible task of earning unconditional love through achievement—the goal recedes because conditional love can never be fully satisfied, creating perpetual striving.
behavioral_signature: taking on more responsibilities when exhausted
belief that next achievement will finally satisfy

intervention.primary: Imagery work to access childhood experience
intervention.verbal: I'm running and running toward my house, but the house keeps receding, and the faster I run, the further away the house becomes.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This image captures why no achievement ever feels like enough—you're trying to reach a goal that was designed to stay out of reach.

exact_quotes: I'm running and running toward my house, but the house keeps receding, and the faster I run, the further away the house becomes.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Challenging the Fantasy of Future Release
description: A Socratic question challenging the belief that continuing to strive will eventually lead to a state of satisfaction, when in reality the person applies the same relentless standards to every new achievement.
mechanism: The question helps patients recognize the pattern: each new goal brings the same pressure rather than satisfaction, suggesting the problem is the approach rather than the specific achievements.
what_activates_it: patient expressing hope for future satisfaction

intervention.verbal: But no matter what you get, whether it's a new job, a new girlfriend, a new car, or a new trip, you always apply those same relentless standards. It's really those standards that have to change.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You might notice that every new thing you achieve takes on the same heavy feeling of pressure. This suggests it's not about reaching any particular goal—it's about how you approach everything.

exact_quotes: But no matter what you get, whether it's a new job, a new girlfriend, a new car, or a new trip, you always apply those same relentless standards. It's really those standards that have to change.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Recognizing Play Turned to Work
description: A therapist observation framed to help the patient recognize how their unrelenting standards transform leisure activities into stressful performance demands.
mechanism: By labeling enjoyable activities as having become 'work,' the therapist helps the patient see the pervasive effect of their standards and question whether this transformation is inevitable or changeable.
what_activates_it: patient describing recreational activities with stress

intervention.verbal: So even your play is work.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Notice how activities that could bring joy become another source of pressure. This pattern suggests your approach to everything may need to change.

exact_quotes: So even your play is work.
