source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards — Pressure and Chronic Dissatisfaction
description: The primary emotional experience of unrelenting standards is constant pressure, inability to relax, chronic frustration with self and surroundings, irritation, anxiety about time, and depression at the emptiness of achievements.
mechanism: The lifetrap generates multiple negative emotions: frustration at not meeting standards, anger at self or environment, anxiety about performance and time pressure, and existential depression from the grim, empty quality of accomplishments.

intervention.verbal: The primary feeling is pressure. You can never relax and enjoy life. You are always pushing, pushing, pushing, to get ahead... Unrelenting Standards can create the full gamut of negative emotions. You feel constantly frustrated and irritated with yourself for not meeting your standards. You may feel chronically angry, and certainly you feel high levels of anxiety.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding the emotional toll of your standards—the constant pressure, anxiety, and emptiness—can help motivate you to question whether these standards truly serve you.

exact_quotes: The primary feeling is pressure. You can never relax and enjoy life.
A major focus of your anxiety is time: You have so much to do and so little time.
And you can feel depressed at the grimness of your life and at the emptiness of what you have achieved.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards — Surrender
description: Giving in to the lifetrap by relentlessly pursuing perfection, overworking, and maintaining impossibly high standards in all areas of life. The person accepts the internal pressure as normal and continues driving themselves.
mechanism: The person internalizes perfectionism as their identity and way of being, never questioning whether standards are too high. They sacrifice health, relationships, and pleasure to meet standards that feel normal to them.
behavioral_signature: treating all tasks as equally important

intervention.verbal: What would happen if you lowered your standards by 25%? Would you still be successful? Would your life improve in other ways?
intervention.behavioral: time management charts limiting time on tasks
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You can still be successful and take pride in your work without being perfect. Between perfection and failure is a whole gray area of very good work.

exact_quotes: Sometimes I feel like a machine, like I'm not really alive. Like I'm running on automatic.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards — Surrender (Status Oriented)
description: Surrendering to the status-oriented form of unrelenting standards by planning one's entire life around advancing social class, pursuing status symbols, and seeking the 'perfect' partner based on external qualities.
mechanism: The person accepts that status and external markers of success are what matters most, sacrificing genuine connection and happiness for appearances and social advancement.

intervention.verbal: What else might you be doing that is really more important in life? Is being seen with the 'perfect' person as gratifying as genuine fun and connection?
intervention.behavioral: spending time with less 'perfect' but more genuine people
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Status and external markers cannot make you happy. Real fulfillment comes from genuine connection and enjoyment.

exact_quotes: I felt like where I came from wasn't good enough. I wanted to get to where the rich kids were. I wanted the things they had. I decided early on I was going to get the things the rich kids had.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards — Escape (Procrastination)
description: Escaping the lifetrap through procrastination when the pressure of perfectionism becomes overwhelming. The person avoids tasks because the expected performance level feels impossible to achieve.
mechanism: The overwhelming expectation of perfect performance creates paralysis. The person delays starting because they cannot face the pressure, especially on important projects where standards are highest.

intervention.verbal: The level at which you expect to perform is so high that it is overwhelming. What if you allotted a reasonable amount of time and accepted whatever level you achieved at the end?
intervention.behavioral: time charts with firm limits on task duration
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Procrastination often comes from perfectionism. By accepting 'good enough,' you can actually get more done and feel less pressure.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Unrelenting Standards — Escape (Collapse)
description: Escaping through complete collapse when the relentless pressure becomes unbearable. The person may stop functioning entirely as a way to escape having to meet impossible expectations again.
mechanism: After prolonged periods of driving oneself to meet impossible standards, the person reaches a breaking point where they simply cannot continue and collapse, unable to face the expectations.

intervention.verbal: At some point, you may even collapse and stop functioning. You just cannot stand the thought of having to meet those expectations again. How can we prevent this pattern?
intervention.behavioral: building in rest and recovery before collapse
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Collapse is your body's way of forcing rest. By building in balance before you reach that point, you can avoid the crash.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Love Withdrawal Upon Failure
description: The internalized message that love is actively withdrawn when one falls short of expectations. Parents punish failure with coldness, silence, or rejection.
mechanism: Parents use emotional withdrawal as punishment for not meeting their expectations, creating deep fear of failure and shame around imperfection.

intervention.verbal: Your mother withdrew love when you didn't meet her expectations. A child should never have to earn a parent's love.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Having love withdrawn for normal failures taught you that imperfection is unacceptable. This was wrong of your parents.

exact_quotes: In my freshman year of college, l didn't get asked to pledge the top fraternity. My mother wouldn't talk to me for a week.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Internalized Voice of Constant Pressure
description: The internalized parental voice that creates constant pressure to be doing more, never allowing rest or relaxation.
mechanism: A parent who pushed the child relentlessly becomes an inner voice that continues the pressure into adulthood, never allowing the person to feel they have done enough.

intervention.verbal: That voice saying you should be doing more - whose voice is that? What would you say back to that voice now?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The pressure you feel is an internalized parental voice. You can learn to talk back to it and give yourself permission to rest.

exact_quotes: I'm in bed trying to sleep, and I hear her voice going on and on. She's saying, 'You better get going, there are things you should be doing. Have you finished your homework, don't you need to practice your tennis, don't you have friends to call?'

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Inferiority Requiring Compensation
description: The internalized message that one's background, family, or circumstances are inferior and must be compensated for through achievement and status.
mechanism: Shame about social class, family, or circumstances drives compensatory striving. Achievement becomes a way to escape feeling inferior rather than genuine fulfillment.

intervention.verbal: Your standards developed to compensate for feeling inferior. What would it mean to accept yourself as you are, regardless of status?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You're driving yourself to escape shame about your background. But achievement can never heal that shame - only self-acceptance can.

exact_quotes: I felt like where I came from wasn't good enough. I wanted to get to where the rich kids were.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Cost-Benefit Analysis of Standards
description: Questions that help patients weigh the advantages and disadvantages of their unrelenting standards to determine if the costs outweigh the benefits.
mechanism: By systematically listing and examining both the rewards and the costs of high standards, patients can make a more informed decision about whether to continue.

intervention.verbal: Now you have to weigh the advantages and disadvantages, and decide what makes the most sense. Do the advantages make it all worthwhile? Or do the disadvantages clearly outweigh the advantages?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: By looking at both sides honestly, you can decide whether your current standards are really worth the price you're paying.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Effects of Lowering Standards 25%
description: A Socratic question that challenges all-or-nothing thinking by exploring what would actually happen if standards were lowered moderately rather than abandoned entirely.
mechanism: Challenges the belief that anything less than perfection equals failure by introducing the concept of a gray area between perfect and terrible.

intervention.verbal: Consider what the effects would be if you lowered your standards about 25 percent. Would you still get most of the same rewards without having to pay such a heavy price?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Between perfection and failure there is a whole gray area. You can do something 80% and still do a very good job.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Determining Reasonable Standards
description: Questions that direct patients to seek external perspectives on what constitutes reasonable standards, since their own judgment is compromised.
mechanism: Recognizes that people with Unrelenting Standards cannot trust their own assessment of what is reasonable and need objective input from balanced others.

intervention.verbal: If there are people in your life who seem to lead balanced lives, who seem to have high standards but still manage to enjoy life, ask them how much time they spend working, relaxing, with family, with friends, exercising, vacationing, and sleeping.
intervention.behavioral: surveying balanced others about their standards
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Unbalanced standards don't feel unbalanced to you. You cannot trust yourself in this matter and need outside perspective.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Imagining Life Without Pressure
description: An imagery exercise where patients visualize what their life could be like if they let go of the relentless pressure of their unrelenting standards.
mechanism: By imagining an alternative life with less pressure, patients can connect with their deeper needs and desires that are being sacrificed to maintain impossible standards.

intervention.verbal: Sometime when you are under pressure, when you feel that familiar drive, stop and imagine what it could mean to your life to let some of that pressure go. Sit back and close your eyes, and let an image come. What else might you be doing that is really more important in life?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This exercise can help you understand that the disadvantages of your life are directly linked to your standards. If you lowered them, you could eliminate many of these problems.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Flashcard: Lowering Standards Without Failure
description: A coping flashcard that Pamela carries to remind herself that she can maintain self-respect while lowering her perfectionist standards.
mechanism: The flashcard challenges all-or-nothing thinking about standards and gives permission to do things moderately well without self-criticism.

intervention.verbal: I can lower my standards without having to feel like a failure. I can do things moderately well, feel good about them, and not have to keep trying to perfect them.
intervention.behavioral: carry and read card when tempted to over-perfect
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This reminder helps you challenge the belief that anything less than perfect is failure.

exact_quotes: I can lower my standards without having to feel like a failure. I can do things moderately well, feel good about them, and not have to keep trying to perfect them.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mel's Entitlement Flashcard — Sexual Attraction to Another Woman
description: A specific example flashcard written by a patient named Mel to manage his entitled impulse to have affairs. The flashcard addresses the situation of feeling attracted to another woman.
mechanism: Activates competing motivations (love for wife, fear of losing marriage) to override immediate gratification impulse through cognitive rehearsal

intervention.verbal: I know that right now I am attracted to this woman, and I'm starting to plan how to go to bed with her. But doing this will make Katie very angry and very hurt. I don't want Katie sleeping around, so I shouldn't either. Having sex with this woman is not as important as my marriage to Katie. If I keep sleeping around, I'll lose her for sure. I love Katie and want to be with her for the rest of my life.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: This flashcard helps you remember what's truly important to you when you're tempted by attraction—your love for your partner and your commitment to the relationship.

exact_quotes: I know that right now I am attracted to this woman, and I'm starting to plan how to go to bed with her. But doing this will make Katie very angry and very hurt. I don't want Katie sleeping around, so I shouldn't either. Having sex with this woman is not as important as my marriage to Katie. If I keep sleeping around, I'll lose her for sure. I love Katie and want to be with her for the rest of my life.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Entitlement — Counterattack
description: Entitlement functioning as a Counterattack coping style against underlying core lifetraps such as Emotional Deprivation, Defectiveness, or Social Exclusion. The entitled behavior serves to avoid experiencing vulnerability.
mechanism: Drastic overcompensation to avoid experiencing the pain of underlying feelings of deprivation, defectiveness, or exclusion through demanding, controlling, and entitled behavior
what_activates_it: situations that might expose underlying vulnerability
potential experiences of deprivation or rejection
behavioral_signature: seeking adoration rather than genuine connection

intervention.verbal: Your Entitlement is a drastic attempt to Counterattack so that you will not have to experience the pain of your vulnerability. Unless you experience that underlying feeling of deprivation, defectiveness, or social exclusion, you will not be able to change.
intervention.behavioral: Follow change techniques for underlying lifetraps (Emotional Deprivation, Defectiveness, or Social Exclusion chapters)
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your entitled behavior is protecting you from painful feelings underneath. To truly change, you need to face and heal those deeper wounds, not just manage the surface behavior.

exact_quotes: Your Entitlement is a drastic attempt to Counterattack so that you will not have to experience the pain of your vulnerability.
Unless you experience that underlying feeling of deprivation, defectiveness, or social exclusion, you will not be able to change.
Your Entitlement is all-or-nothing. Either you get everything you want or you are deprived; either you are perfect or you are defective; either you are adored or you are rejected.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Pattern — Entitled Lack of Empathy
description: The characteristic emotional signature of Spoiled Entitlement involving genuine ignorance of others' feelings, operating without social-emotional cues, and inability to recognize when stepping beyond reasonable bounds.
mechanism: Lifelong self-centeredness creates a missing aspect of human relatedness where the person operates in a social vacuum without awareness of how they affect others
behavioral_signature: genuine confusion about why others are upset
assuming if it feels good to self it must be acceptable

intervention.verbal: I just don't understand why it's such a big deal. Why do you make such a big deal about it?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Other people's reactions are important cues that help us decide how to act. Learning to read and respond to these cues will help you have better relationships.

exact_quotes: Mel had a real ignorance of other people's feelings. This is true of most people with Spoiled Entitlement.
Being self-centered is such a lifelong pattern that they are largely unaware of how they are affecting others. A whole aspect of human relatedness is missing for them.
He could not recognize when he was stepping beyond reasonable bounds. He just assumed that if it felt good to him, it was all right.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Mirroring Exercise for Empathy Building
description: A couples therapy technique using active listening to help entitled individuals develop empathy. The exercise has two parts: reflecting back what the other person said, then identifying how they seem to feel.
mechanism: Forces attention onto partner's perspective and emotional state, building the capacity to read and respond to others' feelings that is typically underdeveloped in entitled individuals

intervention.verbal: KATIE: I'm starting to get really mad about the way he orders me around the house. If we're watching television and he wants something to eat, he tells me to go and get it. And, if I tell him to wait until the commercial, he gets snippy. THERAPIST (to Mel): Can you mirror that and then respond? MEL: You're saying that I order you around the house too much, that I make you get me something to eat when we're watching television. And how do you feel? You're mad.
intervention.behavioral: First, reflect back what you heard the other person say. Next, say how the person seems to feel.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Practicing listening and reflecting helps you tune into what others are feeling, which strengthens your relationships and helps you understand how your behavior affects them.

exact_quotes: In couples therapy we do mirroring exercises to help people learn to empathize. Mirroring is a form of active listening. It has two parts. First, you reflect back what you heard the other person say. Next, you say how the person seems to feel.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Challenging Minimization of Partner's Pain
description: A question revealing the entitled person's genuine inability to understand why their behavior causes significant distress to others, demonstrating the empathy deficit characteristic of the lifetrap.
mechanism: Exposes the cognitive distortion and empathy gap that maintains entitled behavior, creating opportunity for therapist to address the missing perspective-taking capacity
what_activates_it: partner expressing hurt about entitled behavior

intervention.verbal: I just don't understand why it's such a big deal. Why do you make such a big deal about it?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding why something matters to your partner—even if it doesn't seem like a big deal to you—is essential for maintaining your relationship.

exact_quotes: MEL: I just don't understand why it's such a big deal. Why do you make such a big deal about it?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Time-Out Technique for Anger Control
description: A structured technique for managing emotional dyscontrol, particularly anger, in entitled individuals. Uses an anger rating scale (0-8) to trigger self-removal from situations before acting out.
mechanism: Breaks the automatic anger-to-action cycle by inserting a pause for self-monitoring and rational decision-making, preventing impulsive venting that is later regretted
what_activates_it: anger reaching 4 or higher on 0-8 scale
urge to act out in anger

intervention.verbal: I'm sorry, but I need to be alone to think for a moment. Let's finish this discussion in a few minutes.
intervention.behavioral: Anger Scale: 0 Not at all Angry, 2 Mildly Angry, 4 Moderately Angry, 6 Very Angry, 8 Extremely Angry. Whenever your anger is 4 or higher on the 0-8 scale, use the time-out procedure. Excuse yourself and leave the situation. If leaving is impossible, start counting to yourself instead. Count until your anger goes below 4.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Learning to step away when anger rises gives you time to think clearly and respond in a way you won't regret later. You can still express your anger, but in a controlled, effective way.

exact_quotes: What we want you to do is learn to use your rising anger as a cue to institute the control strategy.
Whenever your anger is 4 or higher on the 0-8 scale, use the time-out procedure.
Once your anger is manageable, spend some time pondering how you want to respond in the situation.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Underlying Core Messages in Counterattack Entitlement
description: The hidden childhood messages underlying Entitlement when it functions as a Counterattack against core lifetraps. These messages drive the all-or-nothing quality of entitled behavior.
mechanism: Original core beliefs about being deprived, defective, or excluded create unbearable vulnerability, leading to entitled overcompensation as protection against re-experiencing these painful beliefs
what_activates_it: situations threatening to expose underlying vulnerability
potential experiences of deprivation, criticism, or rejection

intervention.verbal: Your Entitlement is all-or-nothing. Either you get everything you want or you are deprived; either you are perfect or you are defective; either you are adored or you are rejected.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Beneath your entitled behavior are painful childhood beliefs about being deprived, flawed, or rejected. Facing these underlying feelings is the key to lasting change.

exact_quotes: Your Entitlement is all-or-nothing. Either you get everything you want or you are deprived; either you are perfect or you are defective; either you are adored or you are rejected. You need to learn that there is a middle ground, that you can get your needs met in a normal way.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Emotional Pattern — Partner's Experience of Entitled Person
description: The characteristic emotional experience of someone in a relationship with an entitled person, marked by confusion about why demonstrations of hurt don't stop the behavior, and the painful realization that empathy approaches will not work.
mechanism: The partner's natural assumption that showing pain will elicit empathy and behavior change fails because the entitled person lacks the capacity to be moved by others' distress
what_activates_it: repeated demonstrations of pain that don't change anything
behavioral_signature: repeatedly showing hurt hoping it will create change
inability to understand partner's continued behavior

intervention.verbal: I couldn't understand it. I would never hurt him like that. I wouldn't be able to stand it, seeing him hurt like that. I mean, there were times I was almost suicidal. I just couldn't understand how he could see he was hurting me and keep right on having affairs.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Demonstrations of hurt are almost always useless with an entitled person. What you have to do instead is to set limits. Use whatever leverage you have.

exact_quotes: I couldn't understand it. I would never hurt him like that. I wouldn't be able to stand it, seeing him hurt like that. I mean, there were times I was almost suicidal. I just couldn't understand how he could see he was hurting me and keep right on having affairs.
Katie had to learn that her hurt was never going to stop Mel.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Empathic Self-Confrontation
description: A healthy attitude toward change that combines showing compassion for oneself while continually pushing to change. Avoids both harsh self-criticism and excessive leniency that makes excuses.
mechanism: Balances self-compassion with personal responsibility, allowing the person to acknowledge the difficulty of change while maintaining motivation and accountability for growth.

intervention.verbal: Show compassion for yourself, while continually pushing yourself to change. Be understanding of your limitations and flaws. Remember that lifetraps are hard to change. Remember the origins of your lifetraps, and try to empathize with yourself when you were a child. However, it is also important to take responsibility for changing. Be persistent. Do not keep putting off change, waiting for a more convenient time. The best time to begin changing is now. No matter how damaged you were as a child, this does not excuse you from taking responsibility for change.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Being too hard on yourself leads to discouragement, while being too easy leads to no change. Finding the balance helps you sustain effort over time.

exact_quotes: Show compassion for yourself, while continually pushing yourself to change
Childhood pain explains why change is so difficult and takes so long; it does not explain why someone allows destructive patterns to continue without working hard to alter them

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Victim Identity Without Responsibility
description: A problematic internalized belief where the person sees themselves only as a victim of their parents without learning to take responsibility for changing their patterns.
mechanism: Creates a fixed identity as damaged that paradoxically maintains the status quo by providing an excuse not to change.

intervention.verbal: Many self-help groups have been criticized for encouraging members to feel like victims of their parents without teaching them to take responsibility for changing. Childhood pain explains why change is so difficult and takes so long; it does not explain why someone allows destructive patterns to continue without working hard to alter them.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Understanding your past helps explain your patterns, but it doesn't mean you have to stay stuck in them.

exact_quotes: Many self-help groups have been criticized for encouraging members to feel like victims of their parents without teaching them to take responsibility for changing

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: All Lifetraps — Escape (Self-Delusion)
description: An escape response where the person avoids facing reality by holding onto illusions about themselves or others, remaining unwilling to acknowledge their actual characteristics.
mechanism: Self-delusion protects from painful truths but maintains self-defeating patterns and prevents genuine relationships.

intervention.verbal: Be honest with yourself. Place a high value on facing reality. So many people delude themselves, holding on to illusions of what they would like themselves to be or what they would like other people to be. They are unwilling to face the reality of what they are like—of their coldness, their sadness, their anger, their anxieties. Self-delusion will only lead you to continue acting in self-defeating ways, and keep you from having genuine relationships.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Facing the truth about yourself is uncomfortable but necessary for real change and authentic connections.

exact_quotes: So many people delude themselves, holding on to illusions of what they would like themselves to be or what they would like other people to be

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Defectiveness Lifetrap — Attraction to Critical Therapist
description: A pattern where individuals with the Defectiveness lifetrap feel strong attraction to critical, superior therapists, even though this dynamic is destructive to them.
mechanism: The familiar dynamic of being criticized feels like chemistry because it matches the childhood experience, reinforcing the lifetrap rather than healing it.
behavioral_signature: mistaking destructive dynamics for good fit

intervention.verbal: Avoid inappropriate chemistry in therapy that might be generated by your lifetraps. For example, if you have a Defectiveness lifetrap, you might feel a lot of attraction to a critical, superior therapist, even though this is destructive to you. You would do better with a therapist who seems to like and respect you.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: What feels familiar isn't always what's healthy. A therapist who respects you may feel unfamiliar at first but will help you heal.

exact_quotes: if you have a Defectiveness lifetrap, you might feel a lot of attraction to a critical, 'superior' therapist, even though this is destructive to you

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Disconnection Pattern — Cold Therapist Mismatch
description: A pattern where individuals with disconnection problems would be poorly served by a cold, remote therapist and instead need someone who actively pushes them to relate.
mechanism: A cold therapist would maintain the disconnection pattern rather than challenging it, missing opportunities for corrective relational experience.

intervention.verbal: If your problem is disconnection, a therapist who is cold and remote is not the one for you. You need someone who will push you to relate. You do not want a therapist who generates chemistry, but someone who will provide a healing environment for your particular problem.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You need a therapist who challenges your tendency to disconnect, not one who makes it easy to stay distant.

exact_quotes: If your problem is disconnection, a therapist who is cold and remote is not the one for you

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Limited Reparenting in Therapy
description: A therapeutic approach where the therapist provides a partial antidote to childhood problems by offering what was missing developmentally, while maintaining appropriate professional boundaries.
mechanism: The therapist provides corrective emotional experiences that partially compensate for developmental deficits, modeling healthy relating while respecting therapeutic limits.

intervention.verbal: In some respects you want a therapist who can be the parent you never had. We call this 'limited reparenting' in therapy. One aspect of therapy involves providing a partial antidote to problems in your childhood. If you did not get enough nurturing, your therapist nurtures you. If you were overly criticized, your therapist supports and validates you. If you had a parent who was too intrusive, your therapist respects your boundaries. If you were abused, the therapist contains and protects you. Naturally, you cannot expect a therapist to substitute completely for the parenting you lacked. This is unrealistic. There is only so much reparenting you can receive in one or two therapy hours a week.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Therapy can provide some of what you missed growing up, helping you develop new ways of relating, though it can't replace everything.

exact_quotes: In some respects you want a therapist who can be the parent you never had
We call this 'limited reparenting' in therapy

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Subjugation/Dependence — Escape (Cult Involvement)
description: An escape response where individuals avoid adult responsibility by joining cult groups that foster dependency and subjugation through charismatic leadership and special membership feelings.
mechanism: Cults exploit vulnerabilities by providing structure and belonging while encouraging members to remain childlike and follow external rules rather than developing autonomy.

intervention.verbal: Beware of cult groups. These are groups that have charismatic leaders, require you to recruit new members (proselytizing), and generally cost large sums of money to join or complete. Cult groups foster dependency and subjugation. Members are made to feel special, that they have a secret that no one else does. In reality, cult members are encouraged to remain children rather than come to grips with the demands of adulthood. They are encouraged to follow the rules of the leader, instead of discovering their own natural inclinations.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Groups that make you feel special while taking your money and independence are keeping you stuck, not helping you grow.

exact_quotes: Cult groups foster dependency and subjugation
cult members are encouraged to remain children rather than come to grips with the demands of adulthood

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Enlisting Support for Change
description: Questions to help identify support resources and recognize when professional help is needed.
mechanism: Helps the person evaluate their support system and recognize the limits of self-help approaches.
behavioral_signature: recognizing when professional help is needed

intervention.verbal: Who are the people who love you that you can reach out to for help? Can friends or family members serve as mentors or role models for reaching your goals? Do you have close family and friends, or are they too disturbed themselves to be of much help? Do your family members reinforce your lifetraps rather than help you change?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Change is easier with support. Identifying who can help—and when you need professional help—is an important part of the process.

exact_quotes: It is hard to change alone. Change is easier when you have support

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Entitlement — Blocked Concern for Others
description: A pattern where the Entitlement lifetrap prevents the person from showing genuine concern for others, blocking access to the gratification that comes from giving and empathy.
mechanism: Entitlement keeps focus on self-interest, preventing the development of empathy and the satisfaction that comes from contributing to others.

intervention.verbal: One of the most gratifying aspects of life is learning to give to other people and to empathize with them. Entitlement may keep you from showing concern for the people around you. It feels good to make a contribution. Social involvement, charity, having children and giving to children, helping your friends—these involve a connection to something greater than yourself and your individual life. How can you contribute to the world at large?
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Giving to others isn't just altruism—it's one of the most fulfilling parts of life that entitlement may be blocking you from experiencing.

exact_quotes: Entitlement may keep you from showing concern for the people around you

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Leap of Faith for Major Changes
description: Recognition that some changes require major breaks with the past rather than gradual steps—leaving relationships, switching careers, or moving—requiring surrender of childhood pattern security.
mechanism: Major life changes cannot always be accomplished incrementally; sometimes growth requires high-risk decisions that break established patterns.
what_activates_it: recognition that gradual change is insufficient
awareness of incompatibility with current life

intervention.verbal: Unfortunately, some changes cannot be accomplished in small steps. They require a leap of faith, a high level of risk. Sometimes we must make major changes in order to grow; these include leaving a relationship, switching careers, or moving to another city. As we become more aware of our natural inclinations and overcome our lifetraps, we may have to make major breaks with the past. You may have to surrender the security of childhood patterns in order to grow into the adult you want to be.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Sometimes real growth requires big, scary changes. The security of old patterns may be holding you back from becoming who you're meant to be.

exact_quotes: some changes cannot be accomplished in small steps. They require a leap of faith, a high level of risk
You may have to surrender the security of childhood patterns in order to grow into the adult you want to be

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Coping Styles Terminology Correspondence
description: Technical note clarifying that Surrender, Escape, and Counterattack in this book correspond to Maintenance, Avoidance, and Compensation in formal schema therapy terminology.
mechanism: The three coping responses to lifetraps map directly onto formal schema therapy coping styles.

intervention.verbal: In schema therapy, these correspond to the coping styles of maintenance, avoidance, and compensation.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Different terms are used in research and clinical settings for the same concepts.

exact_quotes: In schema therapy, these correspond to the coping styles of maintenance, avoidance, and compensation

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Entitlement (Spoiled) — Surrender
description: Surrendering to spoiled entitlement involves fully acting on entitled beliefs without restraint. The person takes what they want, controls others, disregards rules, and expects special treatment as their right.
mechanism: The person accepts their entitled schema as reality and acts accordingly—demanding their way, controlling partners, expressing anger freely, and expecting exemption from normal consequences.
behavioral_signature: taking what they want without guilt
parking in tow-away zones expecting no consequences

intervention.verbal: Right now you are acting on your entitlement—expecting Katie to sacrifice her needs for yours. The normal expectation would be reciprocity, finding a compromise you both like. If you keep bullying her, she won't want to go with you and will be angry all night. Is getting your way worth losing her?
intervention.behavioral: Complete Limits Chart comparing entitled expectations to normal expectations
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: When you surrender to entitlement, you get short-term gratification but damage relationships long-term. People eventually leave or resent you.

exact_quotes: I get things my own way and I like that
I take what I want—money, women, my comforts
By getting angry, I can usually get people to do what I want
I can control most people and I like that

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Entitlement (Dependent) — Surrender
description: Surrendering to dependent entitlement involves placing oneself in a weak, incompetent, needy role and expecting others to be strong and take care of everything. The person demands dependent care as their right.
mechanism: The person feels entitled to depend on others much like a child feels toward a parent. They let others assume responsibility for everyday affairs and decision-making, expressing resentment passively when care is inadequate.
behavioral_signature: stealing from food money for clothes

intervention.verbal: You feel entitled to be taken care of because you feel weak and vulnerable. But this keeps you dependent and drains the people around you. Let's make a hierarchy of tasks you currently let others do for you, and gradually start proving to yourself that you are competent.
intervention.behavioral: Make hierarchy of tasks graded in difficulty; gradually do things others do for you
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your dependent entitlement comes from feeling weak, but it actually keeps you weak. Learning to do things yourself will make you feel stronger.

exact_quotes: Well, he's supposed to be taking care of things better! It's not supposed to come to this!
Your entitlement comes from the fact that you feel weak and vulnerable. You need help, and people must give it to you.

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Entitlement — Counterattack for Emotional Deprivation
description: Entitlement develops as overcompensation for core lifetraps of Defectiveness, Emotional Deprivation, or Social Exclusion. The person who was deprived as a child becomes demanding, narcissistic, and controlling to ensure their needs are never unmet again.
mechanism: Early deprivation (emotional coldness, lack of nurturing, material deprivation) leads to counterattacking by becoming entitled. The person overcorrects so drastically that they alienate people who could meet their needs.
behavioral_signature: pushing away people closest to them

intervention.verbal: Your entitlement may have been adaptive when you were young—a way out of the loneliness and lack of care you experienced. The problem is you went too far. You became so afraid of being deprived again that you started pushing away the very people who could meet your needs. We need to address the underlying deprivation you still feel.
intervention.behavioral: Follow change techniques for underlying Emotional Deprivation or Defectiveness
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your demandingness is actually a sign you still feel deprived inside. Working on that core feeling will reduce your need to control everything.

exact_quotes: Your Entitlement may have offered you a way out of the loneliness, the lack of loving, caring, and attention that you experienced as a child
As an adult, you were so afraid of being deprived or cheated again that you became demanding, narcissistic, and controlling
In trying to make sure that your needs got met, you began to push away the very people who could most meet them

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Impulsivity/Insufficient Self-Control — Surrender
description: Surrendering to impulsivity involves acting on desires and feelings without regard for consequences, difficulty tolerating frustration, and lack of organization and discipline. The person cannot get themselves to focus and persevere.
mechanism: The person acts on impulses between the impulse and action without inserting thought. Short-term gratification always wins over long-term goals. Tasks are procrastinated or done half-heartedly.

intervention.verbal: Between the impulse and the action, you need to learn to place thought. Right now you act on what you want without considering consequences. Let's make a hierarchy of tasks graded by how boring or frustrating they are, and gradually work your way up, building your tolerance.
intervention.behavioral: Make hierarchy of tasks graded by boredom/frustration level; gradually complete increasingly difficult tasks
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Your difficulty postponing gratification keeps you from reaching your life goals. Learning to tolerate frustration—'no pain, no gain'—is the only way to get what you really want long-term.

exact_quotes: Between the impulse and the action, she had to learn to place thought
You have a problem with short-term versus long-term gratification
I thought that if this was what the application process was like, I definitely wouldn't like the job

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You Can Have Whatever You Want
description: Core message internalized when parents spoil and indulge children, giving them whatever they want whenever they want it. The child learns they control the parents and deserve special treatment.
mechanism: Parents fail to exercise sufficient discipline and control. The child is given material desires or their own way without earning it, creating expectation that the world will continue to accommodate them.

intervention.verbal: The message you got as a child was that you could have whatever you wanted. Your parents gave you everything without teaching reciprocity—that to get something, you have to give something back. This worked at home but doesn't work in adult relationships.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: You learned you could have everything as a child, but adult relationships require give and take.

exact_quotes: I got everything I wanted as a child. I was just like a little princess. And everyone took care of me, my parents and my brothers
Children are given whatever they want, whenever they want it
The child controls the parents

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: You Won't Have to Pay Consequences
description: Core message that the child is above normal rules and will somehow escape negative consequences that others would face. Parents allow acting out without following through with aversive consequences.
mechanism: Parents do not impose sufficient negative consequences for irresponsible behavior or impulse expression. The child learns to expect exemption from punishment.
behavioral_signature: expecting to talk way out of tickets

intervention.verbal: You expect to escape the negative consequences other people would incur. This belief comes from childhood, when your parents didn't follow through with consequences. But in the adult world, consequences do happen—you could actually lose your job, your spouse, or face legal trouble.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The expectation that you'll escape consequences is a childhood belief that doesn't hold up in adult reality.

exact_quotes: You do not expect to have to pay the normal consequences for your actions
Even if I got a ticket, I could talk my way out of it
You expect that you will somehow manage to escape the negative consequences other people would incur

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: People Owe It to You to Take Care of You
description: Core message that the child has a right to be taken care of, like a child toward a parent. Others should be strong, handle responsibilities, and provide support as the child's due.
mechanism: Parents overindulge in ways that make children dependent—taking on everyday responsibilities, decisions, and difficult tasks. The environment is so protected that the child comes to demand this level of care.
behavioral_signature: feeling victimized when not cared for

intervention.verbal: You feel entitled to depend on others much like a child feels toward a parent. Your parents took on so much for you that you came to demand this level of care. But this keeps you weak and vulnerable—the very thing you fear.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The message that others must take care of you keeps you dependent and actually increases your vulnerability.

exact_quotes: You feel entitled much in the same way as a child feels toward a parent. It is your right. People owe it to you.
The parents take on everyday responsibilities, decisions, and difficult tasks for the child

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Why Should You Change? — Finding Leverage
description: Core motivational question for entitled patients who see no reason to change because their lifetrap seems to benefit them. Therapist must find the personal cost or leverage point.
mechanism: Entitled patients rarely feel distress from the lifetrap itself—others around them suffer. Change only becomes desirable when unavoidable negative consequences occur. The therapist must identify these leverage points.
what_activates_it: patient stating they like things as they are

intervention.verbal: Why should you change? How is the lifetrap hurting you in your personal and work lives? Consider carefully what the lifetrap is costing you.
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: The only reason to change is if your entitlement is actually costing you something you care about.

exact_quotes: Indeed, why should he change? On the surface, his lifetrap only seems to benefit him.
When we work with Entitled patients, we are always looking for the leverage. Why should they change?

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Is This Immediate Need Worth the Consequences?
description: Socratic question to insert between impulse and action, helping entitled/impulsive patients evaluate whether short-term gratification is worth long-term costs.
mechanism: Creates a cognitive pause between impulse and action, forcing consideration of consequences that the patient typically ignores.

intervention.verbal: Ask yourself if your immediate need is important enough to risk the negative consequences (e.g., alienating friends, losing your job).
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Pausing to ask this question helps you consider whether what you want right now is worth what it might cost you.

exact_quotes: Ask yourself if your immediate need is important enough to risk the negative consequences (e.g., alienating friends, losing your job)

source: Young, J.E. & Klosko, J.S. — Reinventing Your Life
name: Consequence Visualization for Entitlement
description: Imagery exercise where the entitled patient visualizes the negative consequences of their behavior actually occurring—losing their partner, losing their job—to make consequences feel more real and increase motivation to change.
mechanism: Entitlement is maintained partly by denial of consequences. Imagery makes abstract future costs emotionally vivid and present, overcoming the entitled person's belief that bad things won't happen to them.

intervention.verbal: Close your eyes and get an image of what that would be like. [Nina's example: 'I see myself on the phone, calling my mother, calling my friends, trying to get them to do things for me. It's humiliating. I feel like I'm begging.'] Try to comprehend the cost of your Entitlement before the negative consequences occur. Imagine your loved one abandoning you, your job lost.
intervention.behavioral: Close eyes and visualize specific negative consequences in vivid detail
intervention.rationale_to_give_patient: Making the consequences feel real helps you understand what you stand to lose before it actually happens.

exact_quotes: Close your eyes and get an image of what that would be like
I see myself on the phone, calling my mother, calling my friends, trying to get them to do things for me. It's humiliating. I feel like I'm begging
Try to comprehend the cost of your Entitlement before the negative consequences occur
